topics.title: Simulation of Signals: Unit Impulse Signal, Unit Pulse Signal, Unit Ramp Signal, Bipolar Pulse, and Triangular Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Signals: Unit Impulse Signal, Unit Pulse Signal, Unit Ramp Signal, Bipolar Pulse, and Triangular Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Elementary Discrete-Time Signals

In Digital Signal Processing (DSP), **elementary discrete-time signals** form the foundational building blocks used to analyze, design, and test any digital system. Just like atoms constitute physical matter, these elementary signals constitute the language through which every complex DSP algorithm is built, tested, and verified. The KTU 2024 Scheme's *PCCPL507 – DSP Lab* dedicates this experiment (Experiment 2) to the **MATLAB/Python simulation of five canonical discrete-time signals**, namely: the **unit impulse**, **unit step/pulse**, **unit ramp**, **bipolar pulse**, and **triangular signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PCCPL507):**
> The lab manual explicitly lists *Generation/Simulation of basic sequences* — Unit impulse, Unit step, Exponential, Ramp, Triangular, and Bipolar pulse — as the second core experiment, mapping to **CO1** (Apply DSP concepts using computational tools) and **CO2** (Analyze signals using transforms).

---

### 1.2 Formal Definitions of the Five Signals

Let $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ denote the discrete-time integer index. Each signal is defined over a finite observation window $n \in [-N, N]$, typically $N = 5$ or $N = 10$ in lab simulations.

#### 1.2.1 Unit Impulse Signal $\delta[n]$

$$x[n] = \delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The discrete-time unit impulse (also called the *Kronecker delta* or *unit sample*) is **1 at the origin ($n=0$) and zero everywhere else.** It is the discrete analog of the Dirac delta function $\delta(t)$.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine dropping a single drop of ink into the still center of a pond. The impact is instantaneous — at one exact moment there is a "splash" (amplitude 1), and the very next instant, the water is calm again (amplitude 0). This single, isolated "splash" is your unit impulse. In digital circuits, this is equivalent to a **single clock-cycle pulse**.

---

#### 1.2.2 Unit Pulse (Rectangular) Signal $P_N[n]$

$$x[n] = P_N[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le n \le N-1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A unit pulse (or rectangular pulse train sample) is **1 over a finite duration of $N$ samples** and zero outside this window. It is the discrete equivalent of a rectangular gate function.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a **traffic signal turning green**. The "GO" state is active (1) for a fixed duration of, say, 30 seconds, and then turns off (0). That active interval of length $N$ is your unit pulse.

---

#### 1.2.3 Unit Ramp Signal $r[n]$

$$x[n] = r[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \ge 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The unit ramp is a **linearly growing sequence** that starts at zero when $n = 0$ and increases by exactly **one unit** for every increment in $n$.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a **speedometer needle the moment a vehicle begins to accelerate**. The reading is 0 at $t=0$, climbs to 1, then 2, 3, ... km/h — steadily rising. This monotonic, linear growth is your unit ramp.

---

#### 1.2.4 Bipolar Pulse Signal

$$x[n] = \text{bipolar}[n] = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{for one half-cycle} \\ -1, & \text{for the other half-cycle} \end{cases}$$

A typical periodic definition over period $2M$:

$$x[n] = (-1)^{n} = \begin{cases} +1, & n \text{ even} \\ -1, & n \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A bipolar pulse carries **both positive and negative amplitudes** (most commonly $\pm 1$) over alternating intervals. It is the discrete equivalent of a square wave centered about zero — the **mean value is zero**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Consider an **AC electrical supply** — the voltage swings positively, then negatively, in equal magnitude. A bipolar pulse is the digital, sampled version of this alternating waveform. It is the canonical test signal for analyzing **zero-mean** system behavior and is fundamental in **modulation theory** (e.g., BPSK).

---

#### 1.2.5 Triangular Signal $\text{tri}[n]$

$$x[n] = \text{tri}[n] = \begin{cases} n+1, & -1 \le n \le 0 \\ 1-n, & 0 \le n \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

A generalized triangular pulse of peak amplitude $A$ and half-width $L$:

$$x[n] = A \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\vert n \vert}{L}\right), \quad -L \le n \le L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The triangular signal **rises linearly to a peak at the center**, then **falls linearly back to zero**, forming an isosceles triangle. It is the discrete convolution of two rectangular pulses, i.e., $\text{tri}[n] = P_L[n] * P_L[n]$.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **mountain's silhouette on a graph** — you go up the slope linearly to the summit, then descend linearly on the other side. The shape is perfectly triangular. In real engineering, triangular signals are used as **sweep generators** in spectrum analyzers and as **interpolation kernels** in digital resampling.

---

### 1.3 Master Snapshot Table — The Five Signals at a Glance

| # | Signal | Mathematical Form | Key Property |
|---|--------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta[n]=1$ at $n=0$ | Sampling property |
| 2 | Unit Pulse | $P_N[n]=1$ over length $N$ | Finite energy |
| 3 | Unit Ramp | $r[n]=n \cdot u[n]$ | Monotonic growth |
| 4 | Bipolar Pulse | $(-1)^n$ | Zero mean, alternating |
| 5 | Triangular | $\text{tri}[n] = P_L * P_L$ | Self-convolution of rect |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why These Five Signals? — The Engineering Rationale

Every discrete-time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system can be **completely characterized** by its response to a unit impulse. By simulating these five basic sequences, we:

1. **Build the response vocabulary** of any DSP system.
2. **Verify sampling theorems** using impulses and pulses.
3. **Test linearity and time-invariance** with ramps and bipolar pulses.
4. **Design windowing and shaping filters** using triangular signals.
5. **Establish zero-mean analysis** using bipolar signals (crucial for AC coupled circuits).

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Conditions & Domain Specifications

#### 2.2.1 Unit Impulse — Boundary Conditions

- **Domain:** $n \in [-N, N]$
- **Support:** Exactly one sample ($n = 0$)
- **Energy:** $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta[n]^2 = 1$
- **Power:** $P = 0$ (since signal is finite-duration and non-periodic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Property (CRITICAL):** The unit impulse acts as an identity element for signal decomposition: any signal $x[n]$ can be written as a weighted sum of shifted impulses — $x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,\delta[n-k]$. This is the foundation of convolution.

#### 2.2.2 Unit Pulse — Boundary Conditions

- **Domain:** $n \in [0, N-1]$
- **Length/Width:** $N$ samples
- **Energy:** $E = N$
- **Average Power over length:** $P = 1$

#### 2.2.3 Unit Ramp — Boundary Conditions

- **Domain:** $n \in [0, N]$ (truncated)
- **Linearity:** Strictly $r[n] = n$ for all $n \ge 0$
- **Energy over $[0,N]$:** $E = \sum_{n=0}^{N} n^2 = \dfrac{N(N+1)(2N+1)}{6}$
- **Power:** Grows unbounded as $N \to \infty$

#### 2.2.4 Bipolar Pulse — Boundary Conditions

- **Domain:** $n \in [0, 2M-1]$ (one full period)
- **Period:** $2M$ samples
- **Mean (DC) value:** $0$ (perfectly balanced positive and negative halves)
- **Energy per period:** $E = 2M$

#### 2.2.5 Triangular Signal — Boundary Conditions

- **Domain:** $n \in [-L, L]$
- **Peak Amplitude:** $A$ (at $n = 0$)
- **Symmetry:** Even function — $\text{tri}[n] = \text{tri}[-n]$
- **Energy:** $E = \dfrac{A^2(2L+1)}{3}$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop reference for KTU exam problems on this experiment. Memorize the relationships.**

| Signal | Time-Domain Definition | Z-Transform | Energy / Power | KTU Use |
|--------|------------------------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$ | $\delta[n] = 1$ iff $n=0$ | $1$ (all $z$) | $E=1$, $P=0$ | System ID via impulse response |
| Unit Step $u[n]$ | $u[n] = 1$ iff $n \ge 0$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $E=\infty$, $P=\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Causality tests |
| Unit Ramp $r[n]$ | $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ | $E=\tfrac{N(N+1)(2N+1)}{6}$ | Polynomial growth |
| Rectangular Pulse $P_N[n]$ | $1$ on $[0,N-1]$ | $\dfrac{1-z^{-N}}{1-z^{-1}}$ | $E=N$ | Windowing |
| Bipolar Pulse $(-1)^n$ | $(-1)^n$ | $\dfrac{z}{z+1}$ | $E=2M$, $P=1$ | Modulation (BPSK) |
| Triangular $\text{tri}[n]$ | $A(1-\frac{\vert n\vert}{L})$ | $\left(\dfrac{1-z^{-L}}{1-z^{-1}}\right)^2$ | $E=\tfrac{A^2(2L+1)}{3}$ | Smoothing kernel |

**CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** In every formula above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the absolute value. In Z-transform and energy formulas, **do not substitute** $|$ for $\mid$ or use the vertical bar inside prose — this breaks KTU's expected formatting in answer sheets.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Signal Used | Practical Application |
|--------|-------------|-----------------------|
| Digital Communications | Bipolar pulse | Line coding (NRZ, AMI), BPSK modulation |
| Audio DSP | Triangular signal | Anti-aliasing, dithering noise shaping |
| Control Systems | Ramp | Tracking error analysis, steady-state response |
| Radar / Sonar | Impulse | Pulse compression, matched filtering |
| Image Processing | Rectangular pulse | 2-D windowing, separable filtering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** The **triangular signal** is the time-domain representation of the **$\text{sinc}^2(f)$ spectrum** — used as the *raised-cosine roll-off* pulse in digital communication transceivers. Understanding its discrete generation is essential for any telecom or audio DSP engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sampling Property of the Unit Impulse

**Goal:** Prove that any arbitrary sequence $x[n]$ can be decomposed as a sum of weighted, shifted impulses.

**Step 1:** Start with an arbitrary finite-length sequence $x[n]$ defined for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$. For each integer $k$ in this range, the shifted impulse $\delta[n-k]$ is defined as:

$$\delta[n-k] = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } n = k \\ 0, & \text{if } n \neq k \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Construct a candidate representation by scaling each shifted impulse by the value of $x[k]$ and summing:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k]\,\delta[n-k]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $y[n]$ at any specific sample, say $n = n_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y[n_0] &= \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k]\,\delta[n_0-k]
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\delta[n_0-k] = 1$ only when $k = n_0$ and zero otherwise, all terms in the sum vanish **except** the term where $k = n_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y[n_0] &= x[n_0] \cdot \delta[n_0-n_0] + \sum_{\substack{k=0 \\ k \neq n_0}}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot 0 \\
y[n_0] &= x[n_0] \cdot 1 = x[n_0]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Since $n_0$ was arbitrary, we conclude:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,\delta[n-k]$$

This is the **sifting property** or **sampling property** of the discrete impulse. It is the cornerstone of the convolution sum.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Ramp, Step, and Impulse

**Step 1:** Define the unit step first:

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \ge 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Express the unit ramp as the running sum of the unit step:

$$r[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} u[k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} 1 = n + 1 \quad \text{for } n \ge 0$$

Equivalently:

$$r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$$

**Step 3:** Verify that the first difference of the ramp yields the step:

$$\begin{aligned}
r[n] - r[n-1] &= n \cdot u[n] - (n-1) \cdot u[n-1] \\
&= n \cdot u[n] - (n-1) \cdot u[n] \quad \text{(since } u[n]=u[n-1] \text{ for } n \ge 1\text{)} \\
&= (n - n + 1) \cdot u[n] = u[n]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the first difference again to obtain the impulse:

$$\begin{aligned}
u[n] - u[n-1] &= \delta[n]
\end{aligned}$$

This **three-level nesting** (impulse → step → ramp) is a **favourite KTU viva question.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triangular Signal as Convolution of Two Pulses

**Step 1:** Define two rectangular pulses of width $L$:

$$P_1[n] = u[n] - u[n-L], \qquad P_2[n] = u[n] - u[n-L]$$

Both are **1** on $[0, L-1]$ and zero elsewhere.

**Step 2:** Compute the convolution $y[n] = P_1[n] * P_2[n]$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} P_1[k]\,P_2[n-k]$$

**Step 3:** For a given $n$, the product $P_1[k]\,P_2[n-k]$ is **1** only when **both** $P_1[k]=1$ and $P_2[n-k]=1$ simultaneously. This requires $k \in [0, L-1]$ AND $n-k \in [0, L-1]$, i.e., $k \in [\max(0, n-L+1), \min(L-1, n)]$.

**Step 4:** The number of valid integer $k$ values (i.e., the convolution sum) depends on $n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \begin{cases}
0, & n < 0 \\
n+1, & 0 \le n \le L-1 \\
2L-1-n, & L \le n \le 2L-2 \\
0, & n \ge 2L-1
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** This piecewise linear function is precisely a **triangular pulse** that:
- Rises from 0 to $L$ over $n \in [0, L-1]$
- Falls from $L$ to $0$ over $n \in [L, 2L-2]$

Thus: $\text{tri}[n] = P_L[n] * P_L[n]$. **This is why triangular signals appear whenever a rectangular window passes through a smoothing filter twice.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy of Triangular Signal

**Step 1:** Use the piecewise definition with peak $A=1$, half-width $L$:

$$\text{tri}[n] = \begin{cases} 1 - \dfrac{\vert n\vert}{L}, & \vert n\vert \le L \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the discrete energy formula:

$$E = \sum_{n=-L}^{L} \left(1 - \frac{\vert n\vert}{L}\right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Use even symmetry (the signal is symmetric about $n=0$). Double the positive-$n$ half and add $n=0$:

$$E = (1)^2 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{L}\left(1 - \frac{n}{L}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $m = L-n$ so that as $n: 1 \to L$, $m: L-1 \to 0$. Then $1 - \dfrac{n}{L} = \dfrac{m}{L}$ and $dn = -dm$:

$$E = 1 + 2\sum_{m=0}^{L-1}\left(\frac{m}{L}\right)^2 = 1 + \frac{2}{L^2}\sum_{m=0}^{L-1}m^2$$

**Step 5:** Use the standard identity $\sum_{m=0}^{L-1}m^2 = \dfrac{(L-1)L(2L-1)}{6}$:

$$E = 1 + \frac{2(L-1)(2L-1)}{6L} = 1 + \frac{(L-1)(2L-1)}{3L}$$

Multiplying through and simplifying:

$$E = \frac{3L + 2L^2 - 3L + 1}{3L} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \text{(check)} \Rightarrow E = \frac{2L^2+1}{3L} = \frac{(2L+1)(L)}{3L} + \text{remainder}$$

For large $L$, $E \approx \dfrac{2L}{3}$, confirming the tabulated formula $E = \dfrac{A^2(2L+1)}{3}$ when $A=1$ is asymptotically consistent.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Signal to Use When

| If the lab question asks for… | Use this signal | Why |
|-------------------------------|-----------------|-----|
| "Test system impulse response" | $\delta[n]$ | Definition of $h[n]$ |
| "Apply a finite-duration excitation" | $P_N[n]$ | Bounded, finite energy |
| "Examine linearity of response" | $r[n]$ | Polynomial excitation |
| "Show zero-mean behavior" | Bipolar / $(-1)^n$ | DC = 0 |
| "Demonstrate smoothing / convolution" | $\text{tri}[n]$ | Self-convolution |
| "Generate a periodic carrier" | Bipolar pulse | Period $2M$ |

---

### 3.6 Reference MATLAB Implementation Skeleton

> [!NOTE]
> *The KTU DSP Lab officially uses MATLAB. The skeleton below is the standard approved lab implementation. Students are expected to type and execute this during the viva.*

```matlab
%% KTU PCCPL507 — Experiment 2: Simulation of Basic Signals
clc; clear; close all;

n = -5:5;             % Define time index
N = length(n);

%% (a) Unit Impulse Signal
delta = [zeros(1, find(n==0)-1), 1, zeros(1, N-find(n==0))];

%% (b) Unit Pulse Signal of width 4 starting at n=0
pulse = double(n >= 0 & n < 4);

%% (c) Unit Ramp Signal
ramp  = n .* (n >= 0);

%% (d) Bipolar Pulse (alternating +/- 1)
bipolar = (-1).^n;

%% (e) Triangular Signal (half-width L=3, peak A=1)
L = 3;
triangular = max(0, 1 - abs(n)/L);

%% Plotting
subplot(3,2,1); stem(n, delta, 'filled');
title('Unit Impulse \delta[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('\delta[n]'); grid on;

subplot(3,2,2); stem(n, pulse, 'filled');
title('Unit Pulse P_4[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('P_4[n]'); grid on;

subplot(3,2,3); stem(n, ramp, 'filled');
title('Unit Ramp r[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('r[n]'); grid on;

subplot(3,2,4); stem(n, bipolar, 'filled');
title('Bipolar Pulse (-1)^n'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('Amplitude'); grid on;

subplot(3,2,5); stem(n, triangular, 'filled');
title('Triangular Signal tri[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('tri[n]'); grid on;
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Signal Generation Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram represents the **systematic flow** that a student should follow during the lab viva when asked to generate any of these five signals. It is the canonical "thinking architecture" approved by KTU evaluators.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define discrete-time index vector n] --> B{Choose target signal}
    B -->|delta n| C[Set delta n = 1 only at n = 0, else 0]
    B -->|Pulse| D[Set P N n = 1 on interval 0 to N-1, else 0]
    B -->|Ramp| E[Set r n = n times u n, zero for n less than 0]
    B -->|Bipolar| F[Set bipolar n = -1 to the power n, period 2]
    B -->|Triangular| G[Compute tri n = max 0, 1 minus abs n divided by L]
    C --> H[Plot using stem function]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply axis labels, title, grid for presentation]
    I --> J[Verify against analytical definition]
    J --> K[End: Signal successfully simulated]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Relationship Between the Five Signals

This second diagram captures the **mathematical lineage** that links the signals — a frequent viva topic ("How are impulse, step, and ramp related?").

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unit Impulse delta n] -->|First Difference| B[Unit Step u n]
    B -->|First Difference summation| C[Unit Ramp r n]
    A -->|Shifted and scaled sum| D[Rectangular Pulse P N n]
    D -->|Convolution with itself| E[Triangular Signal tri n]
    B -->|Alternating sign -1 to power n| F[Bipolar Pulse]
    F -->|Used as carrier in BPSK modulation| G[Digital Communication Systems]
```

### 4.3 Simulation Pipeline — Functional Architecture

For the end-to-end lab execution flow, including validation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp[Input: User specified signal type and parameters] --> Pars[Parameter Parser: extract N, L, A, n range]
    Pars --> Gen[Signal Generator Module]
    Gen --> Gen1[Impulse Generator]
    Gen --> Gen2[Pulse Generator]
    Gen --> Gen3[Ramp Generator]
    Gen --> Gen4[Bipolar Generator]
    Gen --> Gen5[Triangular Generator]
    Gen1 --> Val[Validation: check amplitude and length]
    Gen2 --> Val
    Gen3 --> Val
    Gen4 --> Val
    Gen5 --> Val
    Val --> Plot[Plotting Engine: stem plot with grid]
    Plot --> Out[Output: Display figure and console summary]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Time-Domain Visualization Reference

Below is a **schematic representation** of how the five signals appear when plotted using `stem(n, x)` for $n \in [-5, 5]$:

| Signal | Visual Schematic |
|--------|------------------|
| Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$ | Vertical stem of height 1 at $n=0$; no other stems |
| Unit Pulse $P_4[n]$ | Four consecutive stems of height 1 from $n=0$ to $n=3$ |
| Unit Ramp $r[n]$ | Stems of heights 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ |
| Bipolar Pulse $(-1)^n$ | Alternating $+1, -1, +1, -1, \ldots$ across all $n$ |
| Triangular $\text{tri}[n]$ | Rises to peak at $n=0$, then symmetrically falls |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

Define the discrete-time unit impulse signal $\delta[n]$. State and prove its sampling (sifting) property.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The discrete-time unit impulse signal is defined as:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sampling property statement (1 mark):** Any discrete-time signal $x[n]$ can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted impulses:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,\delta[n-k]$$

**Proof (1 mark):** Consider the right-hand side at any arbitrary index $n_0$. The term $\delta[n_0-k]$ is nonzero only when $k = n_0$, contributing $x[n_0]\cdot 1 = x[n_0]$. All other terms vanish. Therefore the sum equals $x[n_0]$. Since $n_0$ is arbitrary, the equality holds for all $n$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

Differentiate between a **unipolar pulse** and a **bipolar pulse**. Give one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Unipolar Pulse | Bipolar Pulse |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Amplitude levels | Only $\ge 0$ (typically $0$ and $+1$) | Both positive and negative ($\pm 1$) |
| DC component | Non-zero (positive mean) | Zero mean |
| Example | $P_N[n]$ | $(-1)^n$ |

**Applications (1 mark each):**
- **Unipolar pulse:** Used in **TTL digital logic** (logic '1' = 5V, logic '0' = 0V).
- **Bipolar pulse:** Used in **BPSK modulation** and **AMI line coding** in digital communication, where zero DC prevents baseline wander.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 marks] With neat MATLAB code and output plots, generate the following discrete-time signals over the interval $n = -5$ to $5$:

1. Unit impulse $\delta[n]$
2. Unit ramp $r[n]$
3. Triangular signal $\text{tri}[n]$ of half-width $L = 3$ and peak $A = 1$

**(b)** [7 marks] Starting from the rectangular pulse $P_L[n] = u[n] - u[n-L]$, derive mathematically that the convolution $P_L[n] * P_L[n]$ produces a **triangular signal**, and verify with a stem plot.

---

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — MATLAB code & plots (7 marks):**

```matlab
n = -5:5;
N = length(n);

% Unit impulse
imp = double(n == 0);

% Unit ramp
ramp = n .* (n >= 0);

% Triangular signal
L = 3;
tri = max(0, 1 - abs(n)/L);

subplot(3,1,1); stem(n, imp, 'filled', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
title('Unit Impulse \delta[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('\delta[n]');
grid on; axis([-6 6 -0.2 1.2]);

subplot(3,1,2); stem(n, ramp, 'filled', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
title('Unit Ramp r[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('r[n]');
grid on; axis([-6 6 -0.5 5.5]);

subplot(3,1,3); stem(n, tri, 'filled', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
title('Triangular Signal tri[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('tri[n]');
grid on; axis([-6 6 -0.2 1.2]);
```

**Expected Output Sketches:**

- **Impulse plot:** A single stem of height 1 at $n = 0$; all other stems are zero.
- **Ramp plot:** Stems at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ with heights $0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
- **Triangular plot:** Stems rising as $0, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{2}{3}, 1, \tfrac{2}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, 0$ centered at $n = 0$.

**Mark allocation for (a):** 2 marks for impulse, 2 marks for ramp, 3 marks for triangular (correct `max` formulation + plot).

---

**Part (b) — Mathematical derivation + verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the rectangular pulse:

$$P_L[n] = u[n] - u[n-L] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le n \le L-1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the convolution sum:

$$y[n] = P_L[n] * P_L[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} P_L[k] \cdot P_L[n-k]$$

For both factors to be simultaneously 1, $k$ must lie in the intersection $[0, L-1] \cap [n-L+1, n]$, giving $k \in [\max(0, n-L+1), \min(L-1, n)]$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate the sum by case analysis:

$$y[n] = \begin{cases} 0, & n < 0 \\ n+1, & 0 \le n \le L-1 \\ 2L - 1 - n, & L \le n \le 2L-2 \\ 0, & n \ge 2L-1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** This piecewise linear function is a **triangular pulse** of peak $L$ at $n = L-1$ with linearly rising and falling sides.

**Verification (bonus 0 marks but expected in viva):** Using `conv(P_L, P_L)` in MATLAB yields the same stem plot, confirming the analytic derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** [7 marks] Generate and plot the **bipolar pulse** $x[n] = (-1)^n$ and the **unit rectangular pulse** $P_6[n]$ over $n = 0$ to $10$. Compute and tabulate their **average power** over one period. Explain why the bipolar pulse is preferred in digital communication.

**(b)** [7 marks] Derive the Z-transform of:
1. Unit impulse $\delta[n]$
2. Unit ramp $r[n]$
3. Bipolar pulse $(-1)^n \cdot u[n]$

Show that the unit ramp Z-transform has a **double pole at $z = 1$** and comment on its stability implications.

---

##### **Model Solution (Part a, 7 marks):**

**MATLAB code:**

```matlab
n = 0:10;
bipolar = (-1).^n;
pulse   = double(n >= 0 & n < 6);

subplot(2,1,1); stem(n, bipolar, 'filled', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
title('Bipolar Pulse (-1)^n'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('Amplitude');
grid on; axis([-0.5 10.5 -1.5 1.5]);

subplot(2,1,2); stem(n, pulse, 'filled', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
title('Unit Pulse P_6[n]'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('Amplitude');
grid on; axis([-0.5 10.5 -0.2 1.2]);
```

**Average Power Computation (4 marks):**

For periodic signals, $P = \dfrac{1}{N_0}\sum_{n=\langle N_0\rangle} \vert x[n]\vert^2$.

- **Bipolar pulse** $x[n] = (-1)^n$, period $N_0 = 2$:

$$P_{\text{bipolar}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[(-1)^0|^2 + (-1)^1|^2\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 + 1) = 1$$

- **Unit rectangular pulse** $P_6[n]$, one period $N_0 = 6$:

$$P_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{1}{6}\sum_{n=0}^{5}(1)^2 = \frac{6}{6} = 1$$

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Signal | Mean (DC) | Average Power |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Bipolar pulse | $0$ | $1$ |
| Unit pulse | $\tfrac{5}{6}$ | $1$ |

**Why bipolar is preferred (2 marks):** Bipolar pulse has **zero DC component**, making it ideal for **AC-coupled channels** (e.g., telephone lines, transformer-coupled links). It prevents **baseline wander** in long-distance digital transmission and forms the basis of **BPSK, AMI, and Manchester line codes**.

---

##### **Model Solution (Part b, 7 marks):**

**(i) Z-transform of $\delta[n]$ (1 mark):**

$$X_1(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta[n] z^{-n} = z^0 = 1, \quad \text{ROC: all } z$$

**(ii) Z-transform of $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$ (3 marks):**

$$X_2(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n z^{-n}$$

Using the standard identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x\vert < 1$, with $x = z^{-1}$:

$$X_2(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1-z^{-1})^2} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z\vert > 1$$

**Double pole at $z = 1$:** The denominator $(z-1)^2$ has a **pole of order 2** at $z = 1$, which lies **on the unit circle** (boundary of ROC). This means the ramp is **not absolutely summable** and its energy grows unboundedly — consistent with our earlier energy formula.

**(iii) Z-transform of $(-1)^n u[n]$ (2 marks):**

$$X_3(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-1)^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-z^{-1})^n = \frac{1}{1+z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z+1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z\vert > 1$$

**Stability comment (1 mark):** The pole is at $z = -1$, which lies **exactly on the unit circle** — hence the bipolar pulse is **marginally stable** (bounded but not absolutely summable). This explains its periodic nature.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Wrong indexing:** Many students write $\delta[n] = 1$ "for $n=1$" instead of $n=0$. Always verify the origin index.
> 2. **Confusing ramp with step:** The ramp is $r[n] = n$, NOT 1. It grows linearly.
> 3. **Ignoring ROC in Z-transforms:** KTU examiners *consistently deduct 1 mark* if the Region of Convergence is missing for Z-transform questions.
> 4. **No axis labels / titles in plots:** MATLAB plots without `xlabel`, `ylabel`, `title`, or `grid on` are penalized **1–2 marks** for poor presentation.
> 5. **Forgetting to define $u[n]$:** When answering bipolar/ramp questions, always state the unit step definition first.
> 6. **Mixing continuous and discrete notation:** Use $[n]$ (square brackets) for discrete, $(t)$ for continuous. Mixing them is a frequent examiner pet peeve.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam / Lab Viva:**

- **$\delta[n]$:** 1 only at $n=0$; has the **sifting property** $x[n] = \sum_k x[k]\delta[n-k]$.
- **$u[n]$:** 1 for $n \ge 0$, 0 otherwise; $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$.
- **$r[n]$:** Equal to $n$ for $n \ge 0$; **Z-transform has double pole at $z=1$**.
- **$P_N[n]$:** Rectangular pulse of width $N$; energy $E = N$.
- **Bipolar $(-1)^n$:** Mean = 0; **preferred in digital comms** (BPSK, AMI).
- **Triangular $\text{tri}[n]$:** Equal to $P_L[n] * P_L[n]$; even-symmetric; peaks at $n=0$.
- **Relationship pyramid:** Impulse → (integrate/sum) → Step → (integrate/sum) → Ramp.
- **Convolution identity:** $\text{tri}[n] = P_L[n] * P_L[n]$ — **always quote this** in viva.
- **MATLAB essentials:** `stem(n, x)` for discrete plots; `subplot(m,n,p)` for multi-panel figures.
- **Energy formulas to memorize:**
  - Impulse: $E = 1$
  - Rectangular pulse: $E = N$
  - Triangular (half-width $L$, peak $A$): $E = \dfrac{A^2(2L+1)}{3}$
- **Always include ROC** when answering Z-transform questions in Part B.
- **Always label plots** with `xlabel`, `ylabel`, `title`, and enable `grid on` for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Sampling Theorem Using Interpolation Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Sampling Theorem Using Interpolation Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Sampling Theorem** (also known as the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is a foundational principle in Digital Signal Processing (DSP) that establishes the necessary and sufficient condition for reconstructing a continuous-time (analog) band-limited signal from its discrete-time samples without any loss of information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Statement of the Sampling Theorem:**
> *"A continuous-time band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz (i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$) can be uniquely represented by, and perfectly reconstructed from, its discrete samples $x(nT_s)$ provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:"*
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
> *The minimum sampling rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2 f_m$ is referred to as the **Nyquist Frequency**."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **spinning ceiling fan** with a distinct red mark on one of its blades.

- If the fan spins **slowly**, your eyes capture the red mark at clearly separated positions — you can mentally reconstruct the circular path.
- If the fan spins **very fast**, the red mark appears as a translucent disc or even appears to spin **backwards** — your brain (the reconstruction filter) cannot piece together the true motion.

The "speed" of the fan blades represents the **signal frequency**, and the rate at which your eyes grab a snapshot represents the **sampling frequency**. To faithfully reconstruct the original motion, your eye must sample the fan at least **twice per revolution** (the Nyquist criterion). When the sampling falls below this rate, the perceived motion becomes a distorted, lower-frequency illusion — this is called **aliasing**.

In DSP, the **sinc interpolation function** $\left( \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s} \right)$ is the mathematically perfect "reconstruction pencil" that, when convolved with the discrete samples, paints back the original continuous waveform.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

- **$f_m$ (Hz)** — Maximum (highest) frequency component present in the analog signal $x(t)$.
- **$T_s$ (seconds)** — Sampling period, the uniform time interval between two consecutive samples.
- **$f_s = 1/T_s$ (Hz)** — Sampling frequency (samples per second).
- **$\Omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ (rad/s)** — Sampling angular frequency.
- **$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / F$ (rad/sample)** — Normalized sampling frequency (in DT domain).
- **Nyquist Rate** — The absolute minimum $f_s$ that avoids aliasing, equal to $2 f_m$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** This experiment is the cornerstone for understanding how real-world analog signals (audio, biomedical ECG, communication waveforms) are converted into digital form by an **ADC** and back to analog by a **DAC** with no perceptible loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Sampling–Reconstruction Pipeline

The experiment verifies the Sampling Theorem through **three sequential logical steps**:

**Step 1 — Generation of the Test Signal**
- A band-limited analog test signal is mathematically defined, typically a sum of two or three sinusoids (e.g., $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f_1 t) + 0.5\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$).
- The maximum frequency $f_m = \max(f_1, f_2, \dots)$ is identified, which determines the Nyquist rate.

**Step 2 — Sampling at Different Rates**
- The signal is sampled at three distinct rates to demonstrate three regimes:
  - **Oversampling:** $f_s \gg 2 f_m$ → perfect reconstruction.
  - **Critical (Nyquist) Sampling:** $f_s = 2 f_m$ → boundary case, perfect in theory.
  - **Undersampling (Aliasing):** $f_s < 2 f_m$ → reconstruction yields a *false* (aliased) signal.

**Step 3 — Reconstruction via Sinc Interpolation**
- The discrete samples are passed through an **ideal low-pass filter** (ILPF) whose impulse response is the **sinc interpolation kernel**.
- The reconstructed signal is mathematically expressed as:
$$x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$
where $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ (the **normalized** sinc).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Sinc Function

When a band-limited signal is uniformly sampled, its spectrum $X_s(f)$ becomes a **periodic replica** of the original spectrum $X(f)$ at every multiple of $f_s$. To recover $X(f)$ from $X_s(f)$, we must extract exactly one period using an **ideal rectangular low-pass filter** in the frequency domain:

$$H(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{2 f_m}\right) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The **inverse Fourier transform** of this rectangular filter is, by definition, a **sinc function in the time domain**:
$$h(t) = T_s \cdot \text{sinc}(f_s t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

This sinc kernel, when convolved with the sample impulses, perfectly interpolates the original continuous signal.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Critical Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum signal frequency | $f_m$ | Highest frequency present in $x(t)$ | **Hz** | Band-limited constraint |
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | **seconds** | Must be uniform |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | **Hz** | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyq}$ | $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ | **Hz** | Minimum allowed rate |
| Normalized sinc | $\text{sinc}(x)$ | $\sin(\pi x) / (\pi x)$ | dimensionless | $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$ |
| Reconstruction | $x_r(t)$ | $\sum_n x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | signal units | Requires ideal LPF |
| ILPF gain | $H_0$ | $T_s$ (in time-domain form) | seconds | Compensates for 1/$T_s$ scaling |
| Aliasing frequency | $f_a$ | $\vert f - k f_s \vert$ for nearest $k$ | **Hz** | Appears when $f_s < 2 f_m$ |
| Spectral replicas | $X_s(f)$ | $\frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k} X(f - k f_s)$ | spectrum | Periodicity in $f$ |
| Overlap condition | — | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | **Hz** | Prevents spectral overlap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This theorem is the mathematical foundation of every **CD player (44.1 kHz for 22.05 kHz audio)**, **telephony (8 kHz for 4 kHz voice)**, **digital oscilloscopes**, and **MRI/CT medical imaging** systems. Without it, digitization would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sampling Operation in the Frequency Domain

We begin with an analog signal $x(t)$ whose Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) is $X(f)$, and we mathematically model the impulse-train sampler as multiplying $x(t)$ by a Dirac comb:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

The sampled signal is therefore:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

Taking the CTFT of both sides and using the **multiplication-in-time / convolution-in-frequency** property ($\mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot p(t)\} = X(f) * P(f)$):

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$$

**Step 1:** Compute $P(f)$ — the CTFT of the Dirac comb. By a standard result:

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 2:** Convolve $X(f)$ with this comb. Convolution with a shifted delta simply shifts the function:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * \left[ \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right]$$

$$\boxed{X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)}$$

This equation reveals that **sampling replicates the spectrum $X(f)$ at every integer multiple of $f_s$**, scaled by $1/T_s$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Reconstruction Formula via Inverse Fourier Transform

To recover $X(f)$ from $X_s(f)$, we apply an **ideal low-pass filter** that passes only the baseband replica (centered at $k = 0$) and rejects all others:

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply in the frequency domain:

$$X_r(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot T_s \cdot X(f) = X(f)$$

The $T_s$ factor in $H(f)$ exactly cancels the $1/T_s$ scaling introduced by sampling.

**Step 2:** Apply the inverse CTFT. Multiplication in frequency corresponds to convolution in time, so $x_r(t) = x_s(t) * h(t)$:

$$x_r(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s) \right] * h(t)$$

**Step 3:** Sifting property of the delta (each impulse picks the value of $h$ at the shifted location):

$$x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,h(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $h(t) = T_s\,\text{sinc}(f_s t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$:

$$\boxed{x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\pi (t - nT_s) / T_s\right)}{\pi (t - nT_s) / T_s}}$$

This is the celebrated **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**, valid if and only if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Aliasing Condition (What Happens When $f_s < 2 f_m$)

Consider a single-tone signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ sampled at $f_s$ where $f_s < 2 f_0$. The samples are:

$$x(nT_s) = \cos(2\pi f_0 n T_s) = \cos\!\left(2\pi n \frac{f_0}{f_s}\right)$$

Suppose an observer tries to interpret these samples as having come from a different frequency $f_a$. Then the samples would also equal $\cos(2\pi f_a n T_s)$, which requires:

$$f_a \equiv \pm f_0 \pmod{f_s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$$

for the nearest integer $k$. Solving for the **aliased frequency** by folding $f_0$ into the principal Nyquist interval $[0, f_s/2]$:

$$\boxed{f_a = \left\vert f_0 - f_s \cdot \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_0}{f_s}\right) \right\vert}$$

**Numerical Example:** Let $f_0 = 70$ Hz and $f_s = 50$ Hz (i.e., $f_s < 2 f_0$).

- Nearest integer multiple of $f_s$ to $f_0$ is $k = 1$, so $f_s = 50$ Hz.
- Aliased frequency: $f_a = \vert 70 - 50 \vert = 20$ Hz.
- The 70 Hz tone **appears as a 20 Hz tone** — this is aliasing in its purest form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing Analogy:** This is exactly the "wagon-wheel" effect in films where a rotating wheel appears to spin backwards when the frame rate is too low.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sampling Regimes

| **Regime** | **Condition** | **Spectral Behavior** | **Reconstruction Outcome** | **Observation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oversampling | $f_s \gg 2 f_m$ | Wide gap between replicas | Perfect, low interpolation error | Trivial reconstruction |
| Nyquist Sampling | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Replicas just touch at $f_m$ | Theoretically perfect | Boundary case |
| Undersampling | $f_s < 2 f_m$ | Replicas **overlap** | Aliased (false) signal recovered | High frequency folded to low |
| No Sampling | $f_s \to 0$ | Only DC remains | Total loss of information | Trivial case |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Sampling and Reconstruction System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[Sampler fs Hz]
    C --> D[Discrete Samples x of nTs]
    D --> E[Digital Storage or Transmission]
    E --> F[Ideal Reconstruction LPF sinc Interpolator]
    F --> G[Reconstructed Signal xr of t]
    G --> H{Compare with Original}
    A --> H

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Behavior Diagram (Spectral Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ORIGINAL["ORIGINAL SPECTRUM X of f"]
        X1["Band limited triangle from -fm to plus fm"]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLED["SAMPLED SPECTRUM Xs of f - Replicated"]
        X2["Replica at k=0 centered at 0"]
        X3["Replica at k=1 centered at fs"]
        X4["Replica at k=-1 centered at -fs"]
        X5["Replica at k=2 centered at 2fs"]
    end

    subgraph OVERLAP["ALIASING CASE - fs less than 2fm"]
        Y1["Replicas overlap heavily"]
        Y2["Baseband contaminated"]
        Y3["Reconstruction fails"]
    end

    subgraph RECON["RECONSTRUCTION - Multiply by H of f"]
        Z1["ILPF passes only k=0 replica"]
        Z2["Output equals original X of f"]
    end

    ORIGINAL --> SAMPLED
    SAMPLED --> OVERLAP
    SAMPLED --> RECON

    style X1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style X2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style X3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style X4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style X5 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style Y1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style Y2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style Y3 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style Z1 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825
    style Z2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — MATLAB-Based Verification Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 - Define x of t as sum of sinusoids] --> P2[Step 2 - Identify fm and compute Nyquist rate]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 - Choose three fs values: 5fm, 2fm, 1.2fm]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 - Sample x of t to obtain x of nTs]
    P4 --> P5{fs compared to 2fm}
    P5 -->|fs greater than 2fm| P6[Case A - Oversampling]
    P5 -->|fs equals 2fm| P7[Case B - Critical sampling]
    P5 -->|fs less than 2fm| P8[Case C - Undersampling with aliasing]
    P6 --> P9[Apply sinc interpolation using socr function]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Plot original vs reconstructed signals]
    P10 --> P11[Compute MSE error and visual comparison]
    P11 --> P12[Verify theorem holds when fs is greater than or equal to 2fm]

    style P1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style P5 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825
    style P12 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

### 4.4 Reference MATLAB Code Snippet (For Lab Use Only)

```matlab
% Verification of Sampling Theorem
fm = 10;                       % Maximum signal frequency (Hz)
t  = 0:1e-4:0.5;               % Fine time grid for original
x  = sin(2*pi*fm*t);           % Original analog signal

fs_cases = [5*fm, 2*fm, 1.2*fm];   % Three sampling rates
labels   = {'Oversampled (5fm)', 'Nyquist (2fm)', 'Undersampled (1.2fm)'};

for k = 1:3
    fs  = fs_cases(k);
    Ts  = 1/fs;
    n   = 0:Ts:0.5;
    xn  = sin(2*pi*fm*n);
    tr  = 0:1e-4:0.5;
    xr  = zeros(size(tr));
    % Sinc interpolation
    for i = 1:length(tr)
        xr(i) = sum( xn .* sinc( (tr(i) - n)/Ts ) );
    end
    subplot(3,1,k); plot(t,x,'b',tr,xr,'r--'); legend('Original','Reconstructed');
    title(labels{k}); xlabel('t (s)'); ylabel('Amplitude');
end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The MATLAB `sinc` function uses the **normalized** definition $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, which directly matches the Whittaker–Shannon formula. Octave users can replicate this with the same built-in `sinc` function.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. A band-limited signal has a maximum frequency of 4 kHz. Calculate the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.*

**Model Answer:**

The Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be reconstructed exactly from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

Given $f_m = 4$ kHz:

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

Hence the minimum sampling rate is **8 kHz**. *(3 marks: 1 for theorem + 1 for formula + 1 for numerical answer.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is aliasing? How can it be prevented in a practical ADC system?*

**Model Answer:**

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal appear as false lower-frequency components in the reconstructed signal when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate. It occurs because the spectral replicas of $X(f)$ overlap when $f_s < 2 f_m$.

**Prevention methods:**
1. Use an **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** before the ADC to band-limit the input to $f_m < f_s/2$.
2. Choose a sampling rate sufficiently higher than $2 f_m$ (oversampling).
3. Increase the resolution of the quantizer to reduce spectral leakage artefacts.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for cause + 1 for prevention.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula** for reconstructing a band-limited signal from its samples. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 3\cos(100\pi t) + 2\sin(200\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 150$ Hz. (7 marks)

    (i) Identify the maximum frequency $f_m$ and verify whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.
    (ii) If $f_s$ is changed to **80 Hz**, determine the **aliased frequency** for each component and write the reconstructed (corrupted) signal.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Assume $x(t)$ is band-limited to $f_m$ Hz, i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Model sampling as multiplication by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$. The CTFT of the impulse train is:

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - kf_s)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply convolution property — $X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

This shows periodic spectral replication.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply an ideal low-pass filter $H(f) = T_s$ for $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$, and 0 otherwise, to extract the baseband replica:

$$X_r(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H(f) = X(f)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Take the inverse CTFT. The ILPF's impulse response is $h(t) = T_s\,\text{sinc}(f_s t)$. Convolution in time gives:

$$x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This reconstruction is **exact** if and only if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (so the replicas do not overlap).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given $x(t) = 3\cos(100\pi t) + 2\sin(200\pi t)$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Extract frequencies:
- $3\cos(100\pi t) = 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 50\,t)$ → $f_1 = 50$ Hz
- $2\sin(200\pi t) = 2\sin(2\pi \cdot 100\,t)$ → $f_2 = 100$ Hz

So $f_m = \max(50, 100) = 100$ Hz.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Check Nyquist criterion at $f_s = 150$ Hz: $2 f_m = 200$ Hz, and $f_s = 150 < 200$. **Nyquist criterion is violated — aliasing will occur.**

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For $f_s = 80$ Hz, compute aliased frequencies using $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ for the nearest integer $k$:

- For $f_1 = 50$ Hz: nearest multiple of 80 is $k=1$ (i.e., 80 Hz). $f_{a1} = \vert 50 - 80 \vert = 30$ Hz.
- For $f_2 = 100$ Hz: nearest multiple of 80 is $k=1$ (i.e., 80 Hz). $f_{a2} = \vert 100 - 80 \vert = 20$ Hz.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The reconstructed (corrupted) signal becomes:

$$\boxed{x_r(t) = 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 30\,t) + 2\sin(2\pi \cdot 20\,t) = 3\cos(60\pi t) + 2\sin(40\pi t)}$$

The amplitudes are preserved, but the frequencies are entirely wrong — a textbook demonstration of aliasing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of aliasing** in the frequency domain with neat spectral diagrams. What is the role of an **anti-aliasing filter** in a digital signal acquisition system? (7 marks)

**(b)** A music signal contains frequencies up to **20 kHz**. (7 marks)

    (i) Determine the standard **CD-quality sampling rate** and justify why it is chosen higher than 2$f_m$.
    (ii) If this signal is mistakenly sampled at **30 kHz**, identify the **highest aliased frequency** that will appear after reconstruction.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Aliasing & Anti-Aliasing Filter (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** When $x(t)$ containing components up to $f_m$ is sampled at $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas $X(f - kf_s)$ **overlap** in the baseband region $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$. This overlap is **additive** — the overlapped frequencies cannot be separated, and the reconstructed signal contains **false (ghost) frequencies** that were never present in the original. This phenomenon is aliasing.

**Spectral Diagram (2 marks):** Draw $X(f)$ as a triangle from $-f_m$ to $+f_m$, and overlay shifted replicas at $k=0, \pm1, \pm2$. When $f_s < 2 f_m$, the baseband (k=0) replica is corrupted by the tails of the $\pm 1$ replicas. The non-overlapping part reconstructs correctly; the overlapped part is irreversibly distorted.

**Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter (2 marks):** An analog **low-pass filter** placed *before* the ADC, with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$, removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ that would otherwise alias into the baseband. This is a **non-negotiable** block in any practical data acquisition system.

---

**(b) CD-Quality Sampling (7 marks):**

**(i) (4 marks):** $f_m = 20$ kHz → Nyquist rate = $2 f_m = 40$ kHz. The **standard CD sampling rate is 44.1 kHz**, which is chosen slightly above 40 kHz for the following reasons:
- Provides a **4.1 kHz guard band** to accommodate the finite roll-off of practical (non-ideal) low-pass filters.
- Avoids the need for a sharp, expensive analog anti-aliasing filter.
- Compatible with video sampling rates (44.1 kHz is a multiple of NTSC/PAL horizontal scan rates, allowing easy digital audio recording on video media).
- The signal-to-noise ratio improvement from oversampling: $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76$ dB benefits from the extra 4.1 kHz headroom.

**(ii) (3 marks):** At $f_s = 30$ kHz, the Nyquist limit is $f_s/2 = 15$ kHz. Any signal component between **15 kHz and 20 kHz** will be aliased.
- The **highest original frequency** is 20 kHz. Its aliased position:
$$f_a = \vert 20 - 1 \cdot 30 \vert = 10 \text{ kHz (after folding)}$$

- More precisely, the highest aliased component = $|f_m - f_s| = |20 - 30| = \mathbf{10 \text{ kHz}}$. So the 20 kHz tone will reappear as a **10 kHz tone** in the reconstructed output — well within the audible range, hence audibly destructive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling** in $X_s(f)$ and consequently the $T_s$ gain in the ideal reconstruction filter. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist *rate* (2 $f_m$) with Nyquist *frequency* ($f_m$)** — examiners are very strict on this distinction. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using the unnormalized sinc** ($\sin(x)/x$ instead of $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$) in the reconstruction formula. Always state the **normalized** form unless explicitly told otherwise. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not drawing the spectral replicas** in part (a) of derivation questions — even a hand-sketched diagram earns partial credit. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to verify** the Nyquist condition before proceeding with reconstruction in numerical problems. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- Sampling Theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is **necessary and sufficient** for exact reconstruction.
- Nyquist Rate = $2 f_m$ (Hz), Nyquist Frequency = $f_m$ (Hz) — these are **different** quantities.
- Sampling period: $T_s = 1/f_s$; sampling angular frequency: $\Omega_s = 2\pi f_s$.
- Sampled spectrum: $X_s(f) = (1/T_s) \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$ — periodic with period $f_s$.
- **Whittaker–Shannon formula**: $x_r(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$, with $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.
- Ideal reconstruction LPF: $H(f) = T_s$ for $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$, else 0; impulse response is $T_s\,\text{sinc}(f_s t)$.
- **Aliasing condition**: $f_s < 2 f_m$ causes spectral overlap and irreversible distortion.
- **Aliased frequency formula**: $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ for nearest integer $k$.
- Anti-aliasing filter is an **analog LPF** placed before the ADC, with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$.
- CD audio: 44.1 kHz chosen over 40 kHz (Nyquist) to allow non-ideal anti-aliasing filter.
- Voice (telephony): 8 kHz sampling for 4 kHz audio bandwidth.
- Sinc function properties: $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$, $\text{sinc}(n) = 0$ for integer $n \neq 0$, even symmetry.
- In MATLAB, the built-in `sinc(x)` is the **normalized** version — directly compatible with the Whittaker–Shannon formula.
- Three sampling regimes to demonstrate: **Oversampling**, **Nyquist sampling**, **Undersampling (aliasing)**.
- Practical verification steps: (1) generate $x(t)$, (2) sample at chosen $f_s$, (3) reconstruct via `sinc` interpolation, (4) compare with original via MSE or visual overlay.
- **Aliasing cannot be undone** in DSP — it must be **prevented at the input stage**, never corrected later.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of DFT Properties (Linearity and Parseval's Theorem)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of DFT Properties (Linearity and Parseval's Theorem)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT): Formal Definition

The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is the fundamental frequency-domain representation of a finite-length discrete-time signal $x[n]$ of length $N$. It transforms $N$ time-domain samples into $N$ frequency-domain samples and is formally defined as:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The corresponding **Inverse DFT (IDFT)** that recovers the time-domain signal is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCPL507):** The DFT is the cornerstone of every digital signal processing experiment. In this lab experiment (Exp-2), we computationally **verify two foundational properties** — *Linearity* and *Parseval's Theorem* — using sampled signals and MATLAB's built-in `fft()` function.

## 1.2 Property 1 — Linearity of the DFT

**Formal Definition:**
The DFT is a **linear transformation**. If $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ are two finite-length sequences of length $N$, with DFTs $X_1[k]$ and $X_2[k]$ respectively, and $a, b$ are arbitrary complex (or real) constants, then:

$$\text{DFT}\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,X_1[k] + b\,X_2[k]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Weighing Scale" View:**
> Imagine a weighing scale. If you place two objects of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ on it, the scale shows $m_1 + m_2$. If you then place them at different times, you get the same total. The DFT behaves identically: it does not care **how** a signal is constructed — as a sum of scaled components, or as a single chunk. The frequency-domain "weight" is always the weighted sum of the individual frequency components. **Linearity simply means the operation respects addition and scalar multiplication.**

## 1.3 Property 2 — Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

**Formal Definition:**
Parseval's Theorem for the DFT states that the **total energy** of a finite-length signal computed in the time domain is equal (up to a normalization factor of $1/N$) to the total energy computed in the frequency domain:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" View:**
> Picture a pipeline carrying water. Whether you measure the total water flowing past a single point (time-domain energy) or sum the energy of all the swirling eddies and ripples inside the pipe (frequency-domain energy), the total is identical — water is neither created nor destroyed. The DFT is a **lossless, energy-preserving** transform. This is a *very powerful* property: it allows engineers to perform filtering, compression, and analysis in the frequency domain **without losing information**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Experiment

- **Sampling Rate ($F_s$):** Number of samples per second in **Hz**.
- **Signal Length ($N$):** Number of samples; must satisfy $N \ge$ signal length (zero-padding allowed).
- **Frequency Resolution ($\Delta f$):** $\Delta f = F_s / N$ in **Hz**.
- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, the fundamental complex exponential kernel.
- **Time-domain Energy ($E_t$):** $E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2$.
- **Frequency-domain Energy ($E_f$):** $E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How the Linearity Property Holds

The DFT operator is a **finite linear combination** of the input samples. When we apply it to a sum of scaled signals, the summation distributes naturally:

1. **Operator Definition Step:** The DFT is a sum operator $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(\cdot)\,W_N^{kn}$ applied to the input sequence.
2. **Distributivity Step:** Multiplication by constants $a$ and $b$ commutes with the summation operator — they can be pulled outside.
3. **Decomposition Step:** The sum of two signals splits into two independent sums, each yielding the DFT of the individual signal.
4. **Reconstruction Step:** The individual DFTs, scaled by $a$ and $b$, are simply added.

> **Engineering Utility:** Linearity is what allows us to break any complex signal (like a speech waveform or ECG signal) into simpler basis components (sine waves, exponentials, impulses), process each component **independently** (amplify, filter, attenuate), and then **recombine** them. Without linearity, the entire framework of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems in DSP would collapse.

## 2.2 Why and How Parseval's Theorem Holds

Parseval's Theorem is a direct consequence of the **orthogonality of the complex exponential basis functions** $e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ over one full period. The proof relies on the IDFT expression and the orthogonality relation:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}(k-m)n} = \begin{cases} N, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

1. **Energy as Inner Product Step:** Time-domain energy is the inner product of $x[n]$ with itself: $E_t = \langle x, x \rangle$.
2. **Substitute IDFT Step:** Replace $x[n]$ with its IDFT expression.
3. **Cross-Multiply Step:** Form $x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$ and re-index summations.
4. **Apply Orthogonality Step:** The double sum collapses into a single sum, leaving $\vert X[k]\vert^2$.
5. **Normalize Step:** Divide by $N$ (the IDFT factor) to obtain the final energy identity.

> **Engineering Utility:** Parseval's Theorem is the **mathematical guarantee** that operations like FFT-based filtering, spectral analysis, and lossy compression (MP3, JPEG) preserve or correctly account for signal energy. It is also the foundation of the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** computation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Identity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| DFT Definition | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,W_N^{kn}$ | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ |
| IDFT Definition | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\,W_N^{-kn}$ | Used in derivation |
| **Linearity Property** | $\text{DFT}\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,X_1[k] + b\,X_2[k]$ | $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$ |
| **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$ | Energy conservation |
| Time-Domain Energy | $E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2$ | Joules (normalized) |
| Frequency-Domain Energy | $E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$ | Must equal $E_t$ |
| Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N} = \cos(2\pi/N) - j\sin(2\pi/N)$ | Complex, unit magnitude |
| Orthogonality Relation | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{(k-m)n} = N\,\delta[k-m]$ | Used in proof |
| Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = F_s / N$ | Hz per bin |
| DFT of Scaled Delta | $\text{DFT}\{\delta[n-n_0]\} = W_N^{k n_0}$ | Useful in testing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks you to "verify" a property, your answer must have **two columns** of computation: (1) LHS computed directly from the combined signal, and (2) RHS computed by separately taking the DFT and combining. The two columns must produce **identical numerical results** (within floating-point tolerance, e.g., $10^{-10}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Linearity Property

**Given:** Two finite-length sequences $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, each of length $N$, with DFTs $X_1[k]$ and $X_2[k]$. Constants $a$ and $b$.

**To Prove:** $\text{DFT}\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,X_1[k] + b\,X_2[k]$

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]$. The DFT of $y[n]$ is, by the definition of the DFT:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n]\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the definition of $y[n]$:**

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \big(a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\big)\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

Here we directly substitute the linear combination $a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]$ for $y[n]$ inside the summation.

**Step 2 — Apply the distributive law of multiplication over addition:**

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a\,x_1[n]\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} \;+\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} b\,x_2[n]\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

The summation operator distributes over the sum, yielding two independent summations.

**Step 3 — Pull the constants $a$ and $b$ outside the summations (since they do not depend on $n$):**

$$Y[k] = a\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1[n]\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} \;+\; b\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_2[n]\,e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

This step is valid because the sum is finite and the constants $a$ and $b$ are independent of the summation index $n$.

**Step 4 — Recognize each summation as the DFT of the respective signal:**

$$Y[k] = a\,X_1[k] + b\,X_2[k]$$

By the definition of the DFT, $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N} = X_1[k]$, and similarly for $x_2[n]$.

**Conclusion:** The linearity property is proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Parseval's Theorem

**Given:** A finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ with DFT $X[k]$.

**To Prove:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Start with the time-domain energy definition:**

$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,x^*[n]$$

Energy is the inner product of a signal with its complex conjugate.

**Step 2 — Substitute the IDFT expression for $x[n]$:**

$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\,e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}\right] x^*[n]$$

The IDFT formula expresses $x[n]$ as a linear combination of frequency-domain coefficients.

**Step 3 — Distribute $x^*[n]$ and rearrange the order of summation:**

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^*[n]\,e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}\right]$$

Since the inner sum does not depend on $X[k]$ except through $k$, we can swap the order of summation.

**Step 4 — Recognize the inner sum as $X^*[k]$ (complex conjugate of the DFT):**

The DFT definition is $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$. Taking the complex conjugate:

$$X^*[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^*[n]\,e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

Therefore the inner bracketed term equals $X^*[k]$.

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\,X^*[k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$$

Since $X[k] \cdot X^*[k] = \vert X[k]\vert^2$.

**Conclusion:** Parseval's Theorem is proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Linearity Verification

**Problem:** Let $N = 4$, $x_1 = [1, 2, 0, 0]$, $x_2 = [0, 0, 3, 4]$, and $y[n] = 2\,x_1[n] + 3\,x_2[n]$. Verify the linearity property.

**Solution:**

**Method 1 — Direct (LHS):** Compute $Y[k]$ directly from the combined signal.

The combined signal: $y[n] = 2 \cdot [1, 2, 0, 0] + 3 \cdot [0, 0, 3, 4] = [2, 4, 9, 12]$.

$$Y[0] = 2 + 4 + 9 + 12 = 27$$

**Method 2 — Linearity (RHS):** Compute $X_1[k]$, $X_2[k]$ separately, then form $2X_1[k] + 3X_2[k]$.

For $X_1[k]$ with $x_1 = [1, 2, 0, 0]$:

$$X_1[0] = 1 + 2 + 0 + 0 = 3$$

For $X_2[k]$ with $x_2 = [0, 0, 3, 4]$:

$$X_2[0] = 0 + 0 + 3 + 4 = 7$$

Therefore: $2X_1[0] + 3X_2[0] = 2(3) + 3(7) = 6 + 21 = 27$.

**Verification:** $Y[0] = 27 = 2X_1[0] + 3X_2[0]$ ✓ **Linearity holds.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Parseval's Theorem Verification

**Problem:** Let $x[n] = [1, 2, 3, 4]$, $N = 4$. Verify Parseval's Theorem.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute time-domain energy:**

$$E_t = (1)^2 + (2)^2 + (3)^2 + (4)^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30$$

**Step 2 — Compute the DFT $X[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$:**

For $k = 0$:

$$X[0] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n]\,e^0 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

For $k = 1$ (using $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$):

$$X[1] = 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j$$

For $k = 2$ (using $e^{-j\pi} = -1$):

$$X[2] = 1 + 2(-1) + 3(1) + 4(-1) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2$$

For $k = 3$ (using $e^{-j3\pi/2} = j$):

$$X[3] = 1 + 2(j) + 3(-1) + 4(-j) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude-squared of each $X[k]$:**

$$\vert X[0]\vert^2 = (10)^2 = 100$$

$$\vert X[1]\vert^2 = (-2)^2 + (2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

$$\vert X[2]\vert^2 = (-2)^2 = 4$$

$$\vert X[3]\vert^2 = (-2)^2 + (-2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

**Step 4 — Compute frequency-domain energy:**

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{3}\vert X[k]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 8 + 4 + 8) = \frac{1}{4}(120) = 30$$

**Verification:** $E_t = 30 = E_f$ ✓ **Parseval's Theorem holds.**

---

## 3.5 MATLAB Reference Code for Verification

```matlab
% === Verification of DFT Properties: Linearity & Parseval's Theorem ===
clc; clear; close all;

%% Part A: Linearity Property Verification
N = 8;
n = 0:N-1;

% Define two arbitrary sequences
x1 = [1 2 3 4 0 0 0 0];
x2 = [0 0 0 0 1 2 3 4];

% Define scaling constants
a = 2; b = 3;

% Construct the combined signal
y = a*x1 + b*x2;

% Method 1: Compute DFT of combined signal directly (LHS)
Y_direct = fft(y, N);

% Method 2: Compute individual DFTs and combine (RHS)
X1 = fft(x1, N);
X2 = fft(x2, N);
Y_linearity = a*X1 + b*X2;

% Display and compare
disp('--- Linearity Verification ---');
disp('DFT of combined signal (LHS):'); disp(Y_direct);
disp('Linearity combination (RHS):'); disp(Y_linearity);
disp(['Max absolute error: ', num2str(max(abs(Y_direct - Y_linearity)))]);

%% Part B: Parseval's Theorem Verification
x = [1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8];
X = fft(x, N);

% Time-domain energy
E_time = sum(abs(x).^2);

% Frequency-domain energy
E_freq = (1/N) * sum(abs(X).^2);

disp('--- Parseval Verification ---');
fprintf('Time-domain energy E_t   = %.4f\n', E_time);
fprintf('Frequency-domain energy  = %.4f\n', E_freq);
fprintf('Absolute difference      = %.2e\n', abs(E_time - E_freq));

%% Plotting
figure('Name','DFT Properties Verification','NumberTitle','off');

subplot(2,2,1); stem(n, real(Y_direct),'filled'); 
title('LHS: Re\{DFT(ax_1+bx_2)\}'); xlabel('k'); ylabel('Amplitude'); grid on;

subplot(2,2,2); stem(n, real(Y_linearity),'filled'); 
title('RHS: Re\{aX_1+bX_2\}'); xlabel('k'); ylabel('Amplitude'); grid on;

subplot(2,2,3); stem(n, abs(x),'filled'); 
title('Input signal |x[n]|'); xlabel('n'); ylabel('Magnitude'); grid on;

subplot(2,2,4); stem(n, abs(X),'filled'); 
title('DFT magnitude |X[k]|'); xlabel('k'); ylabel('Magnitude'); grid on;
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Verification Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete procedural architecture** for verifying the two DFT properties inside a DSP Lab environment. It captures the data flow, the decision branches, and the validation feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lab Session Start] --> B[Define N and initialize signals]
    B --> C[Generate x1 of length N]
    B --> D[Generate x2 of length N]
    C --> E[Choose scalars a and b]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Form combined signal y = a*x1 + b*x2]
    F --> G[Branch 1: Linearity Check]
    G --> G1[Compute Y1 = FFT of y, length N]
    G --> G2[Compute X1 = FFT of x1, length N]
    G --> G2b[Compute X2 = FFT of x2, length N]
    G2 --> H[Form Y2 = a*X1 + b*X2]
    G2b --> H
    G1 --> I{Is Y1 equal to Y2?}
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Linearity VERIFIED]
    I -->|No| K[Linearity FAILED: Check code]
    J --> L[Branch 2: Parseval Check]
    K --> M[Debug and re-run]
    M --> G
    L --> L1[Pick signal x of length N]
    L1 --> L2[Compute X = FFT of x, length N]
    L2 --> L3[Et = sum of x squared magnitudes]
    L2 --> L4[Ef = 1 by N times sum of X squared magnitudes]
    L3 --> N{Are Et and Ef equal?}
    L4 --> N
    N -->|Yes| O[Parseval VERIFIED]
    N -->|No| P[Parseval FAILED: Check normalization]
    O --> Q[Display plots and tabulated results]
    P --> R[Apply 1 by N scaling factor]
    R --> L3
    Q --> S[End of Lab Experiment 2]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping: Time-Domain vs Frequency-Domain Energy

This second Mermaid diagram visualizes the **dual-domain energy accounting** that underpins Parseval's Theorem — a block architecture for the energy-flow logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sequence x of length N] --> B[Time Domain Energy Et]
    A --> C[FFT Operation]
    C --> D[Frequency Domain Coefficients X of length N]
    D --> E[Compute magnitudes squared]
    E --> F[Sum all magnitudes squared]
    F --> G[Divide by N]
    G --> H[Frequency Domain Energy Ef]
    B --> I{Energy Equality Check}
    H --> I
    I -->|Match| J[Parseval Theorem Confirmed]
    I -->|Mismatch| K[Error in computation or scaling]
```

## 4.3 Property Verification Architecture (Dual-Branch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[DFT Property Lab Module] --> P1[Property 1: Linearity]
    Start --> P2[Property 2: Parseval Theorem]
    P1 --> P1a[Inputs: x1, x2, scalars a, b]
    P1a --> P1b[Form y = a*x1 + b*x2]
    P1b --> P1c[Path LHS: FFT of y]
    P1b --> P1d[Path RHS: a*FFT of x1 + b*FFT of x2]
    P1c --> P1e[Compare LHS and RHS]
    P1d --> P1e
    P1e --> P1f[Output: Linearity verified or error]
    P2 --> P2a[Input: x of length N]
    P2a --> P2b[Compute Et from x]
    P2a --> P2c[Compute FFT of x]
    P2c --> P2d[Compute Ef from FFT result]
    P2b --> P2e[Compare Et and Ef]
    P2d --> P2e
    P2e --> P2f[Output: Energy conserved or error]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Note:** Each `FFT` block in the architecture is a **unitary, linear operator**. The block diagrams visually enforce the idea that the same input can flow through two parallel computational paths, and the outputs **must** agree — this is the essence of "verification" in this lab experiment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**State and prove the linearity property of the DFT. Mention its significance in digital signal processing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** The DFT is a linear operator. For any two sequences $x_1[n]$, $x_2[n]$ of length $N$ and any scalars $a, b$:

$$\text{DFT}\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,\text{DFT}\{x_1[n]\} + b\,\text{DFT}\{x_2[n]\}$$

**Proof (1.5 Marks):**

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n])\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

$$Y[k] = a\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x_1[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N} + b\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x_2[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N} = a\,X_1[k] + b\,X_2[k]$$

**Significance (0.5 Mark):** Linearity enables **superposition** in LTI systems — complex signals can be decomposed into elementary components, processed independently (filtered, amplified), and recombined without distortion. It is the foundation of **LTI system theory**, **FIR/IIR filtering**, and **spectral analysis**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**State Parseval's theorem for the DFT and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1.5 Marks):** For an $N$-point sequence $x[n]$ and its DFT $X[k]$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$$

**Physical Significance (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Energy Conservation:** The theorem states that the total signal energy computed in the time domain equals the energy computed in the frequency domain (scaled by $1/N$). Energy is neither created nor destroyed by the DFT — it is a **lossless, unitary transform**.

2. **Engineering Applications:**
   - **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** computation in spectral analysis.
   - Validating **FFT-based filtering** and signal compression algorithms (MP3, JPEG).
   - Estimating signal energy in **noisy environments** by selecting dominant frequency bins.
   - Used in **matched filtering** and **coherent detection** in radar/communication systems.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. The student is expected to attempt **one of the two alternatives** offered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider two sequences: $x_1[n] = [1, 2, 3, 4]$ and $x_2[n] = [4, 3, 2, 1]$, each of length $N = 4$. Let $y[n] = 2\,x_1[n] - x_2[n]$. Compute the 4-point DFT of $y[n]$ using **two methods** and verify the linearity property.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Method 1 — Direct DFT of $y[n]$ (LHS):**

$$y[n] = 2[1,2,3,4] - [4,3,2,1] = [2-4, \;4-3, \;6-2, \;8-1] = [-2, 1, 4, 7]$$

The 4-point DFT is $Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} y[n]\,e^{-j\pi kn/2}$ with $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$.

$$Y[0] = -2 + 1 + 4 + 7 = 10$$

$$Y[1] = -2 + 1(-j) + 4(-1) + 7(j) = -2 - j - 4 + 7j = -6 + 6j$$

$$Y[2] = -2 + 1(-1) + 4(1) + 7(-1) = -2 - 1 + 4 - 7 = -6$$

$$Y[3] = -2 + 1(j) + 4(-1) + 7(-j) = -2 + j - 4 - 7j = -6 - 6j$$

**Method 2 — Linearity (RHS):**

Compute $X_1[k]$ from $x_1 = [1,2,3,4]$ (already shown in Section 3.4): $X_1[0]=10$, $X_1[1]=-2+2j$, $X_1[2]=-2$, $X_1[3]=-2-2j$.

Compute $X_2[k]$ from $x_2 = [4,3,2,1]$:

$$X_2[0] = 4+3+2+1 = 10$$

$$X_2[1] = 4 + 3(-j) + 2(-1) + 1(j) = 4 - 3j - 2 + j = 2 - 2j$$

$$X_2[2] = 4 + 3(-1) + 2(1) + 1(-1) = 4 - 3 + 2 - 1 = 2$$

$$X_2[3] = 4 + 3(j) + 2(-1) + 1(-j) = 4 + 3j - 2 - j = 2 + 2j$$

Now form $2X_1[k] - X_2[k]$:

$$2X_1[0] - X_2[0] = 2(10) - 10 = 10 \;\checkmark$$

$$2X_1[1] - X_2[1] = 2(-2+2j) - (2-2j) = -4+4j - 2 + 2j = -6+6j \;\checkmark$$

$$2X_1[2] - X_2[2] = 2(-2) - 2 = -6 \;\checkmark$$

$$2X_1[3] - X_2[3] = 2(-2-2j) - (2+2j) = -4-4j - 2 - 2j = -6-6j \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** $Y[k] = 2X_1[k] - X_2[k]$ for all $k$. **Linearity is verified.** (7 Marks)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the sequence $x[n] = [1, 2, 3, 4]$, compute the 4-point DFT and verify Parseval's theorem by calculating both time-domain and frequency-domain energies.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time-Domain Energy (2 Marks):**

$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{3}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30$$

**Step 2 — DFT Computation (3 Marks):** The DFT coefficients (already derived in Section 3.4):

$$X[0] = 10, \quad X[1] = -2+2j, \quad X[2] = -2, \quad X[3] = -2-2j$$

**Step 3 — Frequency-Domain Energy (2 Marks):**

$$\vert X[0]\vert^2 = 100, \quad \vert X[1]\vert^2 = 8, \quad \vert X[2]\vert^2 = 4, \quad \vert X[3]\vert^2 = 8$$

$$E_f = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 8 + 4 + 8) = \frac{120}{4} = 30$$

**Conclusion (Final Statement):** $E_t = E_f = 30$. **Parseval's theorem is verified.** (7 Marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove Parseval's theorem for the N-point DFT. State clearly each mathematical step and the property of complex exponentials used in the proof.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X[k]$, both of length $N$.

**To Prove:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$

**Proof (Full 7-Mark Solution):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Begin with the time-domain energy and substitute the IDFT for $x[n]$:

$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,x^*[n] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\,e^{j2\pi kn/N}\right] x^*[n]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Bring the constant $1/N$ outside and rearrange the order of summation (valid for finite sums):

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^*[n]\,e^{j2\pi kn/N}\right]$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Recognize the inner sum as $X^*[k]$. The DFT of $x[n]$ is $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$. Taking the complex conjugate:

$$X^*[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^*[n]\,e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute back:

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\,X^*[k]$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Use the identity $X[k]\,X^*[k] = \vert X[k]\vert^2$:

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\vert X[k]\vert^2$$
