The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **signal flow** from a C program running on the DSP Kit to the analog output on an oscilloscope.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CCS Code Editor] --> B[Compile and Link via Code Composer Studio]
    B --> C[Load .out Executable onto TMS320C6713 DSP Kit]
    C --> D[Program Initialization and Cache Setup]
    D --> E{Generation Method Selection}
    E -->|Method 1| F1[LUT Method: Precomputed sin Table in Memory]
    E -->|Method 2| F2[Recursive Method: yn equals 2cos w0 times yn-1 minus yn-2]
    E -->|Method 3| F3[Taylor Series Approximation]
    F1 --> G1[Cyclical Index: n mod N]
    F2 --> G2[MAC Unit: 1 Multiply, 1 Subtract per Sample]
    F3 --> G3[Polynomial Evaluator]
    G1 --> H[Sample Output Buffer]
    G2 --> H[Sample Output Buffer]
    G3 --> H[Sample Output Buffer]
    H --> I[16-bit DAC on DSP Kit]
    I --> J[Anti-Aliasing Reconstruction Filter]
    J --> K[Analog Output to Oscilloscope or Speaker]
    K --> L[Observed Sine Wave on Scope Display]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Standard Test Signal Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Define Signal Length N] --> S2[Allocate Output Array xn of Size N]
    S2 --> S3{Select Test Signal Type}
    S3 -->|Option 1| T1[Unit Impulse: xn at 0 equals 1, else 0]
    S3 -->|Option 2| T2[Unit Step: xn at n equals 1 for n greater than or equal to 0]
    S3 -->|Option 3| T3[Unit Ramp: xn at n equals n for n greater than or equal to 0]
    S3 -->|Option 4| T4[Exponential: xn at n equals a power n times un]
    S3 -->|Option 5| T5[Sinusoidal: xn at n equals A sin w0 n plus phi]
    T1 --> OUT[Display and Verify on Scope]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T5 --> OUT
    OUT --> END[End: Signal Generated Successfully]
```

## 4.3 DSP Kit Hardware Architecture (TMS320C6713)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HW1[Host PC with CCS IDE] -->|JTAG Emulator| HW2[DSP Kit Board: TMS320C6713]
    HW2 --> HW3[CPU Core: 32-bit Fixed and Float]
    HW3 --> HW4[On-Chip Memory: L1P, L1D, L2 Cache]
    HW3 --> HW5[External SDRAM: 16 MB]
    HW4 --> HW6[McBSP and EDMA Peripherals]
    HW5 --> HW6
    HW6 --> HW7[16-bit Stereo Audio Codec AIC23]
    HW7 --> HW8[Line Out Port to Oscilloscope or Speaker]
    HW7 --> HW9[Line In Port from External Source]
    HW8 --> HW10[Analog Sine Wave Observed on Oscilloscope]
```

## 4.4 Recursive Sine Generation — State Machine View

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitState
    InitState: Initialize y0 equals 0, y1 equals sin w0
    InitState --> ComputeState
    ComputeState: Compute yn equals 2cos w0 times yn-1 minus yn-2
    ComputeState --> OutputState
    OutputState: Send yn to DAC
    OutputState --> IncrementState
    IncrementState: n equals n plus 1
    IncrementState --> CheckState
    CheckState: If n less than N continue
    CheckState --> ComputeState: Yes
    CheckState --> ResetState: No
    ResetState: Reset n to 0 and Repeat Forever
    ResetState --> ComputeState
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture of Signal Generation Pipeline

| Stage | Hardware Component | Function | KTU Exam Tip |
|-------|--------------------|----------|--------------|
| 1. Program Memory | Flash / SDRAM | Stores LUT or coefficient | "LUT is stored in external memory" |
| 2. Computation Engine | DSP CPU + MAC unit | Executes recursion or polynomial | "TMS320C6713 has 2 MAC units" |
| 3. Sample Buffer | On-chip RAM | Holds current and 2 past samples | "Circular addressing used" |
| 4. DAC | 16-bit DAC on kit | Converts digital to analog | "Resolution: $2^{16} = 65536$ levels" |
| 5. Reconstruction Filter | Low-pass RC / active filter | Smooths staircase output | "Cuts off above $F_s/2$" |
| 6. Output | Oscilloscope / speaker | Visualization | "Observe one period" |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: Define Unit Impulse and Unit Step Signals. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **unit impulse signal** $\delta(n)$ is a discrete-time signal defined as:

$$
\delta(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

It has a value of **1** at $n = 0$ and **0** elsewhere. The total energy of the impulse is 1, and it acts as the **identity element for convolution**, meaning the output of any LTI system to $\delta(n)$ is its impulse response $h(n)$.

A **unit step signal** $u(n)$ is a discrete-time signal defined as:

$$
u(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$

It transitions from 0 to 1 at $n = 0$ and remains at 1 for all subsequent samples. Mathematically, the unit step can be expressed as the cumulative sum of the unit impulse: $u(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(n-k)$.

**Use in DSP:** Impulse and step signals are fundamental test inputs for characterizing LTI systems — $\delta(n)$ reveals the impulse response, while $u(n)$ reveals the step response, both of which completely describe a linear system.

---

### Question 2: What is the Nyquist Sampling Theorem? State its significance in sine wave generation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** states that a band-limited continuous-time signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ (in Hz) can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $F_s$ satisfies:

$$
F_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum sampling rate $F_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Significance in sine wave generation:**
- If $F_s < 2f$, the generated discrete sine wave will suffer from **aliasing** — the apparent frequency on an oscilloscope will be different from the intended frequency.
- For example, generating a 4 kHz sine wave with $F_s = 6$ kHz violates Nyquist ($6k < 2 \times 4k = 8k$); the observed signal will appear as a 2 kHz aliased wave.
- On the DSP Kit, $F_s$ is determined by the codec (typically 8 kHz, 16 kHz, 44.1 kHz, or 48 kHz), so the maximum generatable sine frequency is $f_{max} = F_s / 2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full ESE-Style Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Recursive Sine Wave Generation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the recursive formula $y(n) = 2\cos(\omega_0) y(n-1) - y(n-2)$ for sine wave generation. State the initial conditions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

We begin with the trigonometric identity:

$$
\sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\beta) + \cos(\alpha)\sin(\beta)
$$

We also write the same identity with $\alpha$ replaced by $(\alpha - \beta)$:

$$
\sin(\alpha) = \sin(\alpha - \beta)\cos(\beta) + \cos(\alpha - \beta)\sin(\beta)
$$

Solving for $\cos(\alpha - \beta)\sin(\beta)$ from the second equation and substituting into the first, we get:

$$
\sin(\alpha + \beta) = 2\cos(\beta)\sin(\alpha) - \sin(\alpha - \beta)
$$

Now substitute $\alpha = n\omega_0$ and $\beta = \omega_0$:

$$
\sin((n+1)\omega_0) = 2\cos(\omega_0)\sin(n\omega_0) - \sin((n-1)\omega_0)
$$

Defining $y(n) = \sin(n\omega_0)$ and shifting index:

$$
\boxed{y(n) = 2\cos(\omega_0) \cdot y(n-1) - y(n-2)}
$$

**Initial Conditions:**

$$
y(0) = \sin(0) = 0
$$

$$
y(1) = \sin(\omega_0) = \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi f}{F_s}\right)
$$

**Why This Matters:** This recursion needs only **one coefficient** ($2\cos(\omega_0)$), **two memory locations** (for $y(n-1)$ and $y(n-2)$), and **one MAC operation** per sample — making it ideal for real-time DSP Kits with limited memory.

---

**(b)** A sine wave of frequency $f = 500$ Hz is to be generated on a DSP Kit with $F_s = 8000$ Hz using the recursive method. Compute the recursion coefficient, the first four samples, and the total number of samples in one complete period. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Normalized Angular Frequency**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{8000} = \frac{\pi}{8} = 0.3927 \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 2: Recursion Coefficient**

$$
c = 2\cos(\omega_0) = 2\cos(\pi/8) = 2 \times 0.9239 = 1.8478
$$

**Step 3: Initial Conditions**

$$
y(0) = \sin(0) = 0
$$

$$
y(1) = \sin(\pi/8) = 0.3827
$$

**Step 4: Recursive Computation for $n = 2, 3, 4$**

$$
y(2) = 1.8478 \times 0.3827 - 0 = 0.7071
$$

$$
y(3) = 1.8478 \times 0.7071 - 0.3827 = 0.9239
$$

$$
y(4) = 1.8478 \times 0.9239 - 0.7071 = 1.0000
$$

**Step 5: Verification (Direct Formula)**

| $n$ | Recursive $y(n)$ | Direct $\sin(n\pi/8)$ | Match |
|-----|-------------------|------------------------|-------|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | ✓ |
| 1 | 0.3827 | 0.3827 | ✓ |
| 2 | 0.7071 | 0.7071 | ✓ |
| 3 | 0.9239 | 0.9239 | ✓ |
| 4 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | ✓ |

**Step 6: Samples per Period**

$$
N = \frac{F_s}{f} = \frac{8000}{500} = 16 \text{ samples/period}
$$

**Conclusion:** The recursion produces a perfect sine wave that repeats every 16 samples, requiring only the coefficient $1.8478$ and two past values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Standard Test Signals Generation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Generate and plot the first 8 samples of the following standard test signals: (i) Unit Impulse $\delta(n)$, (ii) Unit Step $u(n)$, (iii) Unit Ramp $r(n)$, (iv) Exponential $x(n) = (0.8)^n u(n)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Unit Impulse $\delta(n)$:**

$$
\delta(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(n)$ | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Plot: A single spike of height 1 at $n = 0$, zero elsewhere.

**(ii) Unit Step $u(n)$:**

$$
u(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $u(n)$ | **1** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Plot: A constant value of 1 from $n = 0$ onwards.

**(iii) Unit Ramp $r(n)$:**

$$
r(n) = n \cdot u(n)
$$

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $r(n)$ | **0** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |

Plot: A linearly increasing sequence with slope 1.

**(iv) Exponential $x(n) = (0.8)^n u(n)$:**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x(n)$ | **1.000** | 0.800 | 0.640 | 0.512 | 0.410 | 0.328 | 0.262 | 0.210 |

Plot: A monotonically decreasing sequence approaching zero (since $\vert a \vert = 0.8 < 1$).

---

**(b)** Explain the procedure to generate a unit impulse signal on a TMS320C6713 DSP Kit using Code Composer Studio (CCS). List the hardware and software tools required. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Hardware Tools Required:**
1. **TMS320C6713 DSP Starter Kit (DSK)** — the main processing board
2. **JTAG Emulator** — for program loading and debugging
3. **Host PC** — running CCS IDE
4. **Oscilloscope** — for output visualization
5. **Cables:** USB / parallel port, audio line-out cable

**Software Tools Required:**
1. **Code Composer Studio (CCS)** — IDE for writing, compiling, and debugging
2. **TMS320C6x Compiler** — converts C code to DSP machine code
3. **DSK Support Library** (Board Support Package / BSP) — for codec and peripheral access

**Procedure to Generate Unit Impulse:**

**Step 1:** Open CCS, create a new project named `Impulse_Gen`, and select the **TMS320C6713** target.

**Step 2:** Write the C program:

```c
#include <dsk6713.h>
#include <dsk6713_aic23.h>
#define N 1000

float impulse[N] = {0};

void main() {
    DSK6713_init();
    impulse[0] = 1.0;
    while(1) {
        // Output impulse to codec continuously
        DSK6713_AIC23_write(impulse, N);
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Build the project (compile + link) to generate the `.out` executable.

**Step 4:** Load the executable onto the DSP Kit via the JTAG emulator.

**Step 5:** Run the program. The impulse array (containing 1 at index 0 and 0 elsewhere) is sent sample-by-sample to the **AIC23 audio codec** at the configured sampling rate (e.g., 8 kHz).

**Step 6:** Connect the **Line Out** of the kit to an oscilloscope. A single voltage spike followed by zero will be observed, corresponding to $\delta(n)$.

**Step 7:** Use breakpoints and memory view in CCS to verify the contents of the `impulse[]` array.

**Conclusion:** The DSP Kit acts as a real-time signal generator, with the impulse array acting as a **digital waveform buffer** sent to the DAC at the sampling rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting Initial Conditions (Loss: 2-3 marks):** When deriving or implementing the recursive sine formula, students often write $y(n) = 2\cos(\omega_0) y(n-1) - y(n-2)$ but **forget to specify** $y(0) = 0$ and $y(1) = \sin(\omega_0)$. Without these, the recursion has no starting point and produces garbage values. **Always state both initial conditions explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing Continuous and Discrete Frequencies (Loss: 1-2 marks):** Students sometimes use $f$ and $F_s$ in the wrong units (mixing Hz with rad/sample). **Remember:** $\omega_0$ is in radians per sample, and $f$ is in Hz. Always show the conversion $\omega_0 = 2\pi f / F_s$.
> 3. **Ignoring Nyquist (Loss: 1 mark):** When asked to generate a sine wave of a given frequency, students must verify that $f < F_s / 2$, otherwise aliasing occurs. KTU examiners **love** to give trick questions where the chosen $f$ violates Nyquist.
> 4. **Skipping the Reconstruction Filter (Loss: 1 mark):** When explaining DSP Kit output, students forget to mention the **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** between the DAC and the oscilloscope. Without it, the output looks like a staircase, not a smooth sine wave.
> 5. **Confusing $u(n)$ and $r(n)$ Definitions (Loss: 1-2 marks):** Ramp is $r(n) = n \cdot u(n)$, **not** $r(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$. Ramp values are $0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$, not $1, 1, 1, \dots$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU DSP Lab Exam:**

- **Sine wave equation:** $x(n) = A\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$, where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f / F_s$
- **Recursive sine formula:** $y(n) = 2\cos(\omega_0) y(n-1) - y(n-2)$ — **must memorize**
- **Initial conditions:** $y(0) = 0$, $y(1) = \sin(\omega_0)$
- **LUT formula:** $x(n) = \sin(2\pi n / N)$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$
- **Samples per period:** $N = F_s / f$ (must be an integer for a clean periodic signal)
- **Nyquist theorem:** $F_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — always verify before generation
- **Unit impulse:** $\delta(n) = 1$ at $n=0$, else 0
- **Unit step:** $u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$, else 0
- **Unit ramp:** $r(n) = n$ for $n \geq 0$, else 0
- **Exponential:** $x(n) = a^n u(n)$ — decays if $\vert a \vert < 1$, grows if $\vert a \vert > 1$
- **DSP Kit:** TMS320C6713 with 16-bit DAC, sampling via AIC23 codec
- **Software:** Code Composer Studio (CCS) for C-code development and JTAG loading
- **Output stages:** Computation → DAC → Anti-aliasing filter → Oscilloscope
- **Recursive method advantage:** Only 1 multiplication + 1 subtraction per sample
- **LUT method advantage:** No accumulation error, perfectly periodic
- **Verification trick:** Always cross-check recursive output with direct $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ formula
- **Common trap:** Mixing continuous-time $t$ with discrete-time $n$ in equations
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling an Analog Signal and Studying Aliasing Effect Using DSP Kit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Sampling an Analog Signal and Studying Aliasing Effect Using DSP Kit ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by capturing its instantaneous amplitude values at uniformly spaced time intervals. According to the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**, a band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2f_m$$ The minimum allowable sampling rate, $2f_m$, is termed the **Nyquist Rate**, while $f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** (or folding frequency). **Aliasing** is the distortion phenomenon that occurs when a continuous-time signal is sampled at a rate lower than twice its highest frequency component. High-frequency components of the original signal "fold back" into the lower frequency spectrum, masquerading (aliasing) as different, lower-frequency signals after reconstruction. This results in irreversible information loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the lab, the experiment is performed using a **TMS320C67xx/C55xx DSP Processor Kit** (or equivalent Texas Instruments DSP starter kit), a function/staircase generator, and an oscilloscope/CRO. Students must verify the Nyquist criterion experimentally and observe the spectral folding caused by undersampling. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are filming a rotating ceiling fan with your phone camera. If the fan spins at **900 RPM (15 revolutions per second)** and your camera captures **10 frames per second**, the fan blades appear stationary, or worse, they appear to rotate **backward** at 5 revolutions per second. The camera is **undersampling** the true motion. - The actual fan speed = the true analog signal frequency. - The camera frame rate = your sampling frequency. - The backward/forward illusion = **Aliasing**. To capture the true motion, you need to film at *more than twice* the rotation speed (i.e., > 30 FPS for a 15 Hz fan). This is precisely the Nyquist Criterion in action. In DSP, the same phenomenon happens with sinusoidal signals. A 1 kHz sine wave sampled at 800 Hz does not appear as 1 kHz on the scope — it appears as a **200 Hz** sine wave (the "alias"), because $1\text{ kHz} - 800\text{ Hz} = 200\text{ Hz}$. ### 1.3 Standardized Metrics and Physical Constants - **Standard Audio CD Sampling Rate:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (covers human hearing up to ~22.05 kHz). - **Standard Voice/Telephony Sampling Rate:** $f_s = 8$ kHz (covers speech up to 4 kHz). - **Nyquist Margin (Engineering Rule of Thumb):** Always sample at **$f_s \geq 2.5 \times f_m$** (a 25% safety margin) to allow for practical **anti-aliasing filter roll-off**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Recap:** > - **$f_m$** = Maximum frequency component in the analog signal. > - **$f_s$** = Sampling frequency (samples per second). > - **$f_N = f_s/2$** = Nyquist (folding) frequency. > - **Alias Frequency** = $\vert f - k \cdot f_s \vert$, where $k$ is the integer that brings the result into the range $[0, f_s/2]$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown **Step 1: Signal Conditioning (Anti-Aliasing)** - Before sampling, the analog signal $x(t)$ is passed through a **low-pass analog filter** (anti-aliasing filter) with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$. - This removes any frequency components above $f_s/2$ that would otherwise corrupt the digital representation. **Step 2: Sampling (Sample-and-Hold)** - The **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** captures and holds the input signal's voltage constant during the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) conversion interval. - On a DSP kit (e.g., TMS320C6713), the on-board AIC23 codec performs sampling at a programmable rate (typically 8, 16, 32, 44.1, or 48 kHz). **Step 3: Quantization and Encoding** - The held voltage is quantized into discrete levels (e.g., 16-bit gives $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ levels). - The binary codes are stored in DSP memory as $x[n]$. **Step 4: Reconstruction (Zero-Order Hold)** - The DAC on the DSP kit reconstructs the analog signal using a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — holding each sample value constant until the next sample arrives. - A reconstruction (smoothing) low-pass filter removes the staircase artifacts. **Step 5: Aliasing Mechanism (Why it Happens)** - In the frequency domain, sampling multiplies $X(f)$ by an impulse train of period $f_s$, creating spectral replicas at $\pm k f_s$. - If $f_s < 2 f_m$, these replicas **overlap** (alias), and the original spectrum cannot be recovered. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ | Hz | Minimum $f_s$ to avoid aliasing | | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Highest recoverable frequency | | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | seconds | Time between consecutive samples | | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Hz | Distorted frequency observed after undersampling | | Condition: No Aliasing | $f_s > 2 f_m$ | Hz | Nyquist criterion (strict inequality) | | Condition: Aliasing Occurs | $f_s \leq 2 f_m$ | Hz | Spectral overlap — info loss | | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff | $f_c = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Pre-sampling LPF cutoff | | Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_c = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Post-DAC smoothing LPF | | Practical Sampling Rule | $f_s = 2.5 \times f_m$ to $4 \times f_m$ | Hz | Engineering rule of thumb | | Quantization SNR | $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76$ | dB | N = number of ADC bits | | Spectral Replicas | $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | — | Frequency domain sampling equation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examination Tip:** In KTU valuation, examiners often test whether students confuse the **Nyquist Rate** ($2f_m$) with the **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_s/2$). The Nyquist Rate is a *requirement on $f_s$*; the Nyquist Frequency is a *property of the sampled system*. Memorizing this distinction fetches full marks. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Audio (MP3, CD, Streaming):** 44.1 kHz sampling enables faithful reproduction of audio up to 22.05 kHz. - **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT Scans):** k-space sampling must obey Nyquist to avoid spatial aliasing artifacts. - **Telecommunications (4G/5G, OFDM):** Bandwidth-efficient sampling is fundamental to digital modems. - **Stroboscopy & High-Speed Cameras:** Frame-rate selection directly maps to the sampling theorem. - **Radar and Sonar:** Doppler shift detection requires careful anti-aliasing filtering before digitization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Alias Frequency for a Sinusoid **Problem Setup:** A pure sinusoid $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$ is sampled at $f_s$. Find the observed (aliased) frequency when $f_s < 2f_0$. **Step 1:** The sampled signal is $x[n] = x(nT_s) = A \cos(2\pi f_0 n T_s + \phi)$. Substituting $T_s = 1/f_s$: $$x[n] = A \cos\left(2\pi \frac{f_0}{f_s} n + \phi\right)$$ **Step 2:** A discrete-time cosine is periodic in its normalized frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi (f_0 / f_s)$. Since cosine is even and $2\pi$-periodic in its argument, we may subtract integer multiples of $2\pi$ without changing the sequence. Equivalently, we can subtract integer multiples of $f_s$ from $f_0$ and take the absolute value: $$f_{alias} = \left\vert f_0 - k f_s \right\vert$$ where $k$ is the integer chosen so that $0 \leq f_{alias} \leq f_s/2$. **Step 3 (Textual Logic):** If $f_0$ is slightly larger than $f_s/2$, subtracting one $f_s$ (i.e., $k=1$) yields a small positive frequency — explaining why high frequencies "fold back" to appear as low frequencies. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Evaluation **Given:** Analog signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 1100 \cdot t)$ volts, sampled at $f_s = 800$ Hz. **Find:** The reconstructed (apparent) frequency. **Step 1:** Identify the input frequency. $$f_0 = 1100 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_s = 800 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2:** Check Nyquist criterion. $$2 f_0 = 2200 \text{ Hz} > f_s = 800 \text{ Hz} \implies \text{Aliasing occurs.}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the Nyquist frequency. $$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{800}{2} = 400 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4:** Determine the alias frequency using $k = 1$ (since $f_0 = 1100$ is between $f_s$ and $2f_s$): $$f_{alias} = \vert 1100 - (1)(800) \vert = \vert 1100 - 800 \vert = 300 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 5 (Conclusion):** A 1100 Hz signal sampled at 800 Hz will appear as a **300 Hz** sine wave at the DAC output. The 1100 Hz information is **permanently lost**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Outcome of Sampling a 700 Hz Sine at Various Rates | Sampling Frequency $f_s$ (Hz) | $f_s / 2$ (Hz) | Nyquist Satisfied? | Aliased Frequency (Hz) | Reconstructed Wave | |---|---|---|---|---| | 4000 | 2000 | Yes (since $700 < 2000$) | 700 | Correct 700 Hz | | 1600 | 800 | Yes (since $700 < 800$) | 700 | Correct 700 Hz | | 1000 | 500 | No (since $700 > 500$) | $\vert 700 - 1000 \vert = 300$ | Distorted 300 Hz | | 800 | 400 | No | $\vert 700 - 800 \vert = 100$ | Distorted 100 Hz | | 600 | 300 | No (boundary case) | $\vert 700 - 600 \vert = 100$ | Distorted 100 Hz | | 400 | 200 | No | $\vert 700 - 2(400) \vert = 100$ | Distorted 100 Hz | > [!NOTE] > Notice how the aliased frequency always falls in the range $[0, f_s/2]$. This is the geometric origin of "spectral folding." ### 3.4 Derivation: Spectral Folding (Why it Happens Mathematically) The Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) of a uniformly sampled signal, $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$, is given by the Poisson summation formula: $$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$ **Step 1:** The original spectrum $X(f)$ is centered at $f = 0$, with bandwidth $f_m$. **Step 2:** Sampling creates infinite replicas of $X(f)$ at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ **Step 3:** If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, adjacent replicas do **not overlap**, and a low-pass filter of cutoff $f_s/2$ recovers $X(f)$ perfectly. **Step 4:** If $f_s < 2f_m$, the replica centered at $f_s$ **overlaps** with the baseband replica, summing their amplitudes and **corrupting** the spectrum. This is aliasing. ### 3.5 The Anti-Aliasing Filter Design Rule Given a signal with max frequency $f_m$ and a chosen $f_s$, the anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter must have: $$f_c \leq f_s/2 \quad \text{and} \quad f_c \geq f_m$$ A common choice is $f_c = 0.45 \cdot f_s$, leaving a **transition band** of $0.05 f_s$ for practical filter roll-off. For example, for $f_s = 8$ kHz (telephony), $f_c \approx 3.6$ kHz. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Sampling Experiment ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Function Generator
Analog Signal x of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing
Low-Pass Filter] B --> C[DSP Kit
Analog Input] C --> D[ADC
Sample and Hold] D --> E[TMS320C6x
DSP Processor] E --> F[DAC
Reconstruction] F --> G[Reconstruction
Low-Pass Filter] G --> H[Oscilloscope
or CRO Display] I[Host PC
Code Composer Studio] --> E E --> I ``` **Interpretation:** - The function generator supplies an analog sine wave (variable frequency, e.g., 100 Hz to 3 kHz). - The anti-aliasing filter is implemented using op-amp circuitry (or is built into the DSP kit's AIC23 codec). - The DSP kit samples the signal at a programmable $f_s$ (set in software). - The output is observed on the CRO to verify whether the reconstructed frequency matches the input. ### 4.2 Detailed Sampling & Aliasing Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph InputChain S1[Analog Sine Wave
f0 equals 1100 Hz] --> F1[Anti-Aliasing LPF
fc equals 400 Hz] end F1 --> A1[ADC Sampling
fs equals 800 Hz] A1 --> DSP[DSP Memory
Stored Samples x of n] DSP --> DAC[DAC Zero Order Hold] DAC --> F2[Reconstruction LPF] F2 --> O1[Oscilloscope
Displays 300 Hz] subgraph AliasingMath M1[fo greater than fs slash 2] M2[f alias equals abs fo minus fs] end ``` ### 4.3 Spectrum Visualization (Conceptual Folding) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Baseband["Baseband Spectrum f equals 0"] X0["X of f
centered at 0
bandwidth fm"] end subgraph Replica1["Replica at f equals fs"] X1["X of f minus fs
centered at fs"] end subgraph Replica2["Replica at f equals 2 fs"] X2["X of f minus 2fs
centered at 2fs"] end Baseband --> Replica1 Replica1 --> Replica2 Note1["If fs greater than 2 fm: NO OVERLAP, recoverable"] Note2["If fs less than 2 fm: OVERLAP, ALIASING occurs"] ``` ### 4.4 Practical DSP Kit Lab Connection ```mermaid flowchart TB PC[PC with Code Composer Studio CCS] -- USB Emulator --> KIT[TMS320C6713 DSK Kit] KIT --> CODEC[AIC23 Audio Codec ADC and DAC] FG[Function Generator] --> LINEIN[Line In Jack] LINEIN --> CODEC CODEC --> LINEOUT[Line Out Jack] LINEOUT --> CRO[CRO or Oscilloscope] CRO --> OBS[Observation:
Reconstructed Waveform] ``` **Lab Procedure Summary:** 1. Generate a sine wave of known frequency (say 500 Hz) on the function generator. 2. Open CCS, write a C program using the `DSK6713_AIC23_*` codec configuration functions to set $f_s$ (e.g., 8 kHz). 3. The program routes the input directly to the output (loopback) after sampling. 4. Observe the CRO: the original 500 Hz appears (no aliasing, since $f_s = 8\text{ kHz} > 2 \times 500 = 1\text{ kHz}$). 5. Now increase the function generator frequency to **5 kHz** while keeping $f_s = 8$ kHz: the CRO shows a **3 kHz** signal (alias: $\vert 5000 - 8000 \vert = 3000$ Hz). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *State the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem. What is the Nyquist rate for a signal with maximum frequency 4 kHz?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. For $f_m = 4$ kHz, the **Nyquist rate** is $2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8$ kHz. (1 mark — theorem statement, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — final answer with unit.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *What is aliasing? How can it be prevented?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Aliasing is the distortion that occurs when an analog signal is sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate, causing high-frequency components to appear as lower (aliased) frequencies in the reconstructed signal. **(1 mark)** It is prevented by: 1. Sampling at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **(1 mark)** 2. Using an analog **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ before the ADC. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** *Explain the sampling theorem in detail. Derive the condition to avoid aliasing. Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal.* **(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** The sampling theorem is a fundamental result in digital signal processing that bridges the analog and digital domains. **(0.5 mark)** **Theorem Statement:** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely recovered from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum sampling rate $2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. **(1 mark)** **Mathematical Model of Sampling:** Sampling can be modeled as multiplication of the continuous signal $x(t)$ by a periodic impulse train $p(t)$: $$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$ The sampled signal is: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **(1 mark)** **Fourier Transform Derivation:** The Fourier transform of $p(t)$ is another impulse train in the frequency domain: $$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - kf_s)$$ By the modulation property, multiplication in time corresponds to convolution in frequency: $$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$ **(1.5 marks)** **Condition to Avoid Aliasing:** For the original spectrum $X(f)$ (bandwidth $f_m$) to be recoverable, the shifted replicas centered at $\pm f_s$ must not overlap with the baseband. This requires: $$f_s - f_m > f_m \implies f_s > 2 f_m$$ **(2 marks)** **Conclusion:** When $f_s > 2 f_m$, the replicas are non-overlapping, and a low-pass filter of cutoff $f_s/2$ recovers $X(f)$ exactly. When $f_s \leq 2 f_m$, aliasing occurs and reconstruction is impossible. **(1 mark)** --- **(b)** *A 1.2 kHz sinusoidal signal is sampled at 1 kHz. Determine the alias frequency and the reconstructed signal waveform. What would be the effect of increasing the sampling frequency to 4 kHz?* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Input signal frequency: $f_0 = 1.2$ kHz $= 1200$ Hz - Sampling frequency: $f_s = 1$ kHz $= 1000$ Hz - Signal: $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi \cdot 1200 \cdot t)$ **Step 1 — Check Nyquist Criterion:** **(1 mark)** $$2 f_0 = 2400 \text{ Hz} > f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz} \implies \text{Aliasing occurs.}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Alias Frequency:** **(2 marks)** Using $f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ with $k = 1$: $$f_{alias} = \vert 1200 - 1000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Reconstructed Signal:** **(2 marks)** The reconstructed waveform will be a 200 Hz sine wave of the **same amplitude** $A$ as the input. The original 1.2 kHz information is **irreversibly lost**. $$x_{rec}(t) = A \cos(2\pi \cdot 200 \cdot t)$$ **Step 4 — Effect of Increasing $f_s$ to 4 kHz:** **(2 marks)** At $f_s = 4$ kHz, the Nyquist frequency becomes $f_N = 2$ kHz. Since $f_0 = 1.2$ kHz $< 2$ kHz, the Nyquist criterion is satisfied: $$2 f_0 = 2400 \text{ Hz} < f_s = 4000 \text{ Hz} \implies \text{No aliasing.}$$ The reconstructed signal will be a faithful 1.2 kHz sine wave. **Conclusion:** Increasing $f_s$ above the Nyquist rate eliminates aliasing and enables perfect reconstruction (subject to anti-aliasing filtering and quantization effects). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the process of sampling and reconstruction of an analog signal. Discuss the role of anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters.* **(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (Textual Description):** **(1.5 marks)** ``` Analog Input x(t) → [Anti-Aliasing LPF] → [Sample-and-Hold] → [ADC] → [DSP Processor / Digital Storage] → [DAC] → [Reconstruction LPF] → Analog Output y(t) ``` **Explanation of Each Block:** **(3 marks)** 1. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** Limits the input bandwidth to $f_s/2$ so that no spectral content above the Nyquist frequency enters the sampler. Without it, high-frequency noise would fold back and corrupt the baseband. 2. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Captures the instantaneous input voltage and holds it constant during the ADC conversion window, ensuring stable quantization. 3. **ADC:** Converts each held voltage to a binary code (e.g., 16-bit) at rate $f_s$. 4. **Digital Processing/Storage:** The discrete samples $x[n]$ are processed (filtered, modulated, etc.) or stored in memory. 5. **DAC:** Converts each digital code back to a voltage (typically using a Zero-Order Hold). 6. **Reconstruction Low-Pass Filter:** Smooths the staircase output of the DAC and removes the spectral images above $f_s/2$, yielding a clean analog waveform $y(t)$. **Role of Filters:** **(2.5 marks)** - The anti-aliasing filter is an **analog LPF placed before the ADC**. It enforces $f_{in,max} \leq f_s/2$. - The reconstruction filter is an **analog LPF placed after the DAC**. It removes the high-frequency spectral images and the staircase ripple. - Both filters must have cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ for ideal reconstruction. **Conclusion:** The combination of anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters, with proper sampling, ensures faithful round-trip analog-to-digital-to-analog conversion. --- **(b)** *Describe the procedure to perform the sampling and aliasing experiment using a TMS320C6713 DSP kit. List the equipment required and explain the expected observations.* **(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Equipment Required:** **(1.5 marks)** 1. TMS320C6713 (or TMS320C55x) DSP Starter Kit (DSK). 2. Function/Staircase Generator (sine wave source, 100 Hz – 10 kHz). 3. Oscilloscope / CRO (dual-channel, ≥ 20 MHz). 4. Connecting probes, BNC cables, and a host PC with **Code Composer Studio (CCS)**. 5. Power supply and USB emulator cable. **Procedure:** **(3.5 marks)** 1. **Setup:** Connect the function generator output to the DSP kit's **Line-In** jack. Connect the DSP kit's **Line-Out** to the CRO. Power on the kit and launch CCS on the host PC. 2. **Software Configuration:** Open the project in CCS. Use the `dsk6713_aic23.h` and `dsk6713.c` libraries to configure the AIC23 codec for: - Line input enabled - Sample rate: $f_s = 8$ kHz (or as required) - 16-bit data format 3. **Code:** Write (or load) a C program that performs **audio loopback** — reading samples from the codec input and writing them directly to the codec output. The sampling rate is set via `DSK6713_AIC23_setFreq(DSK6713_AIC23_FREQ_8KHZ)`. 4. **First Test (No Aliasing):** Set the function generator to **1 kHz** sine wave with amplitude 1 Vpp. Observe the CRO. The CRO should display a clean 1 kHz sine wave (since $f_s = 8$ kHz $> 2 \times 1$ kHz). 5. **Second Test (Aliasing):** Set the function generator to **5 kHz** while $f_s$ remains 8 kHz. Observe the CRO — it will display a **3 kHz** signal (aliased: $\vert 5 - 8 \vert = 3$ kHz). 6. **Third Test (Extreme Aliasing):** Increase the input to **9 kHz**. The CRO shows a **1 kHz** signal (alias: $\vert 9 - 8 \vert = 1$ kHz). 7. **Variation:** Change $f_s$ to 16 kHz, 32 kHz, 48 kHz via software and repeat the observations to confirm that aliasing disappears as $f_s$ increases. **Expected Observations Table:** | Input Freq $f_0$ | $f_s$ | $f_N$ | Aliased? | Observed Output | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 kHz | 8 kHz | 4 kHz | No | 1 kHz | | 3 kHz | 8 kHz | 4 kHz | No | 3 kHz | | 5 kHz | 8 kHz | 4 kHz | Yes | 3 kHz | | 6 kHz | 8 kHz | 4 kHz | Yes | 2 kHz | | 7 kHz | 8 kHz | 4 kHz | Yes | 1 kHz | **(2 marks for observations)** **Conclusion:** The experiment validates the Nyquist theorem and demonstrates aliasing clearly when the input frequency exceeds $f_s/2$. **(Bonus conceptual credit.)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $2f_m$ (Nyquist Rate) with $f_s/2$ (Nyquist Frequency).** These are reciprocals of each other; examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for using them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the strict inequality** $f_s > 2f_m$ for *guaranteed* no aliasing. The equality $f_s = 2f_m$ is a boundary case that requires infinite filter roll-off and is practically unrealizable. > 3. **Not mentioning the anti-aliasing filter.** Even at $f_s = 2f_m$, out-of-band noise can alias. KTU examiners specifically test this point. > 4. **Sign error in alias frequency formula:** Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) and ensure the result lies in $[0, f_s/2]$. > 5. **Confusing sampling rate with bit rate.** Bit rate $= f_s \times N$ (where $N$ is the number of bits per sample). This is a frequent viva trap. > 6. **In lab record submission:** Students often forget to sketch the CRO waveforms with axes labeled. Always include: time/div, volts/div, input frequency, $f_s$, and conclusions. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ for perfect reconstruction. - **Nyquist Rate:** $2 f_m$ — the *minimum* sampling rate. - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_s/2$ — the *highest recoverable* frequency in the sampled system. - **Alias Frequency Formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$, choose $k$ so result is in $[0, f_s/2]$. - **Aliasing Cause:** Spectral overlap of replicas when $f_s < 2f_m$. - **Aliasing Prevention:** (i) Increase $f_s$; (ii) Use anti-aliasing analog LPF with cutoff $f_s/2$. - **Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement:** Always **before** the ADC (analog domain). - **Reconstruction Filter Placement:** Always **after** the DAC (analog domain). - **Spectral Replicas Equation:** $X_s(f) = (1/T_s) \sum_{k} X(f - k f_s)$. - **Quantization SNR:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB (for N-bit ADC). - **Lab Equipment:** TMS320C6713 DSK, function generator, CRO, Code Composer Studio. - **Lab Procedure:** Configure AIC23 codec in CCS for desired $f_s$; route input to output (loopback); vary input frequency and observe CRO for aliasing. - **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Sample at $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $4 f_m$ for practical robustness. - **Standard Rates:** Audio 44.1 kHz / 48 kHz; Voice 8 kHz. - **Common Viva Question:** *"Can you recover a signal sampled exactly at the Nyquist rate?"* — **Answer:** Not practically, because ideal brick-wall filters are unrealizable. Always use $f_s > 2f_m$. - **Common Pitfall:** "Aliasing is reversible with better filtering" — **Wrong.** Aliasing causes permanent information loss; it cannot be undone in software. > **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Sampling an Analog Signal and Studying Aliasing Effect Using DSP Kit**

topics.title: Study of Embedded Components Such as Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of Embedded Components Such as Sensors and Actuators ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensors and Actuators** are the fundamental **transduction elements** that form the physical interface between an embedded/DSP system and its external environment. A **sensor** is a device that detects physical, chemical, or biological quantities (such as temperature, pressure, light, sound, or motion) and converts them into equivalent electrical signals, while an **actuator** performs the reverse operation by converting electrical signals into physical actions (such as motion, heat, light, or sound). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **PCCPL507 – DSP Lab**, the study of these components is essential because any **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** application begins with a sensor capturing a real-world analog signal, which is then sampled, quantized, and processed, after which the resulting control signals are sent to actuators to produce a tangible effect. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **human body**: - **Sensors** are your five senses — **skin (touch)**, **eyes (vision)**, **ears (hearing)**, **nose (smell)**, and **tongue (taste)**. They gather information about the world and convert it into **neural electrical signals** your brain can process. - **Actuators** are your **muscles and vocal cords** — they receive commands from the brain and **act** on the environment (move, speak, grasp). - The **brain** is analogous to the **DSP/Microcontroller** — it processes signals and decides what action to take. In an embedded system, this human "sense → think → act" cycle is mirrored as **Sensor → ADC → DSP Processor → DAC → Actuator**. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)**: Must be at least **twice the highest signal frequency** per the **Nyquist–Shannon Theorem** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$). > - **Resolution (bits)**: Quantization levels $= 2^n$, where $n$ is the ADC bit-width. > - **Standard Supply Voltages**: **3.3 V** and **5 V** for most modern embedded boards. > - **Operating Temperature Range**: Typically **$-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+85^{\circ}\text{C}$** for industrial-grade components. ### 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture A typical embedded signal chain follows this logical flow: $$ \text{Physical Phenomenon} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP} \rightarrow \text{DAC} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Physical Action} $$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Sensors Sensors can be classified on multiple axes, each critical for exam purposes. #### 2.1.1 Based on Output Signal Type - **Analog Sensors**: Produce a continuous output voltage/current proportional to the input. *Example*: LM35 temperature sensor, thermistor, LDR. - **Digital Sensors**: Produce discrete binary/serial output. *Example*: DHT11 (temperature + humidity), DS18B20, ultrasonic HC-SR04. #### 2.1.2 Based on Power Requirement - **Active Sensors**: Require an external excitation signal or power source. *Example*: Strain gauge, RTD. - **Passive Sensors**: Generate their own electrical output in response to the stimulus. *Example*: Thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor, photovoltaic cell. #### 2.1.3 Based on the Physical Quantity Measured > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024 exams, you must memorize the **sensor-to-physical-quantity mapping**: > > | Measured Quantity | Common Sensor | > |---|---| > | Temperature | LM35, DHT11, Thermistor, RTD, Thermocouple | > | Light Intensity | LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor, TSL2561 | > | Pressure | MPX5010, BMP180, Piezoelectric sensor | > | Sound / Vibration | Microphone (electret), Piezo buzzer (as input) | > | Distance / Proximity | HC-SR04 (ultrasonic), IR sensor, PIR | > | Motion / Acceleration | ADXL345 (accelerometer), MPU6050 (IMU) | > | Humidity | DHT11, DHT22, SHT31 | > | Gas / Chemical | MQ-2, MQ-135, MQ-7 | > | Magnetic Field | Hall effect sensor, Reed switch | > | Position / Angle | Potentiometer, Rotary encoder, LVDT | ### 2.2 Classification of Actuators Actuators convert electrical energy into physical action. They are classified as: - **Electrical Actuators**: DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor, relay, solenoid. - **Mechanical Actuators**: Linear actuator, pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders. - **Thermal Actuators**: Heating element, Peltier cooler. - **Optical Actuators**: LEDs, laser diode, LCD/display. - **Acoustic Actuators**: Buzzer, speaker, piezo sounder. ### 2.3 Why Signal Conditioning is Required > [!NOTE] > Raw sensor outputs are often **noisy**, **low-amplitude**, and **non-linear**. Before feeding them into a DSP/ADC, the signal must be **conditioned** through: > > 1. **Amplification** — Boosting weak signals (e.g., using instrumentation amplifiers like INA128). > 2. **Filtering** — Removing noise using **Low-Pass (anti-aliasing)** filters. > 3. **Linearization** — Correcting non-linear sensor responses. > 4. **Level Shifting** — Adjusting voltage range to match ADC input range. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for this experiment. Pay special attention to the units and conditions. | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | ADC Quantization | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Step size for an n-bit ADC | | Number of Levels | $L = 2^n$ | Discrete levels of an n-bit ADC | | Quantization Error | $e_q \le \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Maximum quantization noise | | SQNR (Theoretical) | $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency | | Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta \text{Input}}$ | Ratio of electrical output per unit input | | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | Steinhart–Hart simplified form | | LM35 Sensitivity | $10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Linear voltage output | | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Half of round-trip time × speed of sound | | ADC Digital Output | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$ | Decimal equivalent of sampled value | > **Speed of sound in air** at **$20^{\circ}\text{C}$**: $v \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$. > **Reference voltage** $V_{ref}$ is typically **3.3 V** or **5 V** depending on the MCU. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Industry Relevance Note**: Sensor-actuator pairs form the backbone of: > - **IoT Smart Home systems** (DHT11 + Relay-controlled fan) > - **Automotive ECUs** (MAP sensor + Fuel injector actuator) > - **Medical Devices** (ECG electrode sensor + Defibrillator actuator) > - **Industrial Automation / Industry 4.0** (Pressure sensor + Pneumatic valve) > - **Robotics** (IMU + Servo motors) > - **Audio DSP** (Microphone sensor + Speaker actuator) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Conversion Process for a Sensor Output Consider the **LM35 temperature sensor** connected to a **12-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. The room temperature is **$25^{\circ}\text{C}$**. **Step 1**: Compute the sensor's analog output voltage. The LM35 has a linear sensitivity of **$10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$**. $$ V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C} \times 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 250 \text{ mV} = 0.25 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2**: Determine the ADC resolution (step size). For a 12-bit ADC, $n = 12$ and $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. $$ \Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{4096} = 8.056 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.8056 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 3**: Convert the analog voltage to a digital count. $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0.25}{3.3} \times 4095 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 310.23 \right\rfloor = 310 $$ **Step 4**: Express the digital value in hexadecimal (commonly used in embedded C). $$ D_{hex} = 310_{10} = 0x136 $$ **Step 5**: Reverse the conversion to recover temperature (reconstruction). $$ V_{recovered} = \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{310 \times 3.3}{4095} = 0.2498 \text{ V} $$ $$ T = \frac{V_{recovered}}{10 \text{ mV}} = \frac{249.8 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}} = 24.98^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **Quantization Error**: $$ e_q = 25^{\circ}\text{C} - 24.98^{\circ}\text{C} = 0.02^{\circ}\text{C} $$ This is well within the theoretical maximum: $$ e_{q, max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{0.8056 \text{ mV}}{2 \times 10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}} = 0.0403^{\circ}\text{C} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement The **HC-SR04** sensor emits a 40 kHz ultrasonic burst and measures the echo return time. **Given**: Measured echo pulse width $t = 6 \text{ ms}$, speed of sound at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ is $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$. **Step 1**: Apply the distance formula (factor of 2 because sound travels to the object and back). $$ d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \text{ m/s} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2**: Temperature compensation (advanced). Speed of sound depends on temperature: $$ v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad [\text{m/s}] $$ At $T = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$: $$ v = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 349 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ d = \frac{349 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.047 \text{ m} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Decision Table > [!TIP] > Use this matrix when answering viva or design-based questions. | Selection Criterion | Question to Ask | Example Choice | |---|---|---| | Output Type | Analog or digital interface needed? | Digital → DHT11; Analog → LM35 | | Range | What is the expected input range? | $0$–$100^{\circ}\text{C}$ → LM35 | | Accuracy | What tolerance is acceptable? | $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ → DS18B20 | | Speed | How fast does the quantity change? | Audio (20 kHz) → Electret mic | | Environment | Indoor, outdoor, underwater? | Underwater → Waterproof DS18B20 | | Power Budget | Battery or mains powered? | Low-power → BME280 | | Interface | I²C, SPI, UART, Analog? | I²C → MPU6050 | | Cost | Budget per unit? | $<\$2$ → LM35 | ### 3.4 Actuator Control — PWM Duty Cycle Derivation For a **DC motor** controlled via **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, the average voltage applied to the motor is: $$ V_{avg} = D_{cycle} \times V_{supply} $$ where the duty cycle is: $$ D_{cycle} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} $$ **Example**: To run a 12 V DC motor at 6 V (half speed): $$ D_{cycle} = \frac{V_{desired}}{V_{supply}} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 = 50\% $$ For a **servo motor**, the angle is controlled by the pulse width within a 20 ms period: $$ \theta = \theta_{min} + \frac{(t_{pulse} - t_{min})}{(t_{max} - t_{min})} \times (\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}) $$ For a standard SG90 servo: $t_{min} = 1 \text{ ms}$ ($\theta_{min} = 0^{\circ}$), $t_{max} = 2 \text{ ms}$ ($\theta_{max} = 180^{\circ}$). If $t_{pulse} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$: $$ \theta = 0 + \frac{(1.5 - 1)}{(2 - 1)} \times 180 = 0.5 \times 180 = 90^{\circ} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Generic Embedded Sensor-Processor-Actuator Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Temperature Light Pressure Sound Motion] --> B[Sensor
Transduction Element] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier and Filter] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital Converter] D --> E[DSP Processor
Algorithm Execution] E --> F[DAC or PWM
Digital to Analog Conversion] F --> G[Actuator
Motor Buzzer LED Relay] G --> H[Physical Action
Movement Light Heat Sound] H -. Feedback Loop .-> A ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram: Temperature Control System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Tank
Hot Liquid] --> B[LM35 Sensor
10 mV per degC] B --> C[Op-Amp Buffer
Impedance Matching] C --> D[RC Low Pass Filter
Cutoff 100 Hz] D --> E[12-bit ADC
Vref equals 3.3V] E --> F[DSP or MCU
Compare with Setpoint] F --> G{Threshold
Decision Logic} G -->|Temp too high| H[PWM Generator
Duty Cycle Reduce] G -->|Temp too low| I[PWM Generator
Duty Cycle Increase] H --> J[MOSFET Driver] I --> J J --> K[DC Fan or Heater
Actuator Load] K --> A ``` ### 4.3 Sensor Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sensors] --> B[Analog] A --> C[Digital] A --> D[Active] A --> E[Passive] B --> B1[LM35 Temperature] B --> B2[LDR Light] B --> B3[Potentiometer] C --> C1[DHT11 Humidity] C --> C2[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic] C --> C3[DS18B20 1-Wire] D --> D1[Strain Gauge] D --> D2[RTD PT100] D --> D3[Thermistor in Bridge] E --> E1[Thermocouple] E --> E2[Piezoelectric] E --> E3[Photovoltaic Cell] ``` ### 4.4 Actuator Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Actuators] --> B[Electromechanical] A --> C[Thermal] A --> D[Optical] A --> E[Acoustic] B --> B1[DC Motor] B --> B2[Stepper Motor] B --> B3[Servo Motor] B --> B4[Relay and Solenoid] C --> C1[Resistive Heater] C --> C2[Peltier TEC] D --> D1[LED] D --> D2[LCD and OLED Display] D --> D3[Laser Diode] E --> E1[Piezo Buzzer] E --> E2[Dynamic Speaker] ``` ### 4.5 Signal Conditioning Sub-System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Sensor
Output mV Level] --> B[Instrumentation
Amplifier INA128] B --> C[Anti-Aliasing
Low Pass Filter] C --> D[Level Shifter
to ADC Range] D --> E[ADC Input Pin
of DSP or MCU] F[Reference
Voltage Source] --> D ``` ### 4.6 HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Timing Diagram (Functional Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as Microcontroller participant TRIG as HC-SR04 Trigger Pin participant ECHO as HC-SR04 Echo Pin participant OBJ as Object MCU->>TRIG: Send 10 us pulse TRIG->>OBJ: Emit 40 kHz ultrasonic burst OBJ-->>ECHO: Reflected sound received ECHO->>MCU: Echo pin HIGH for duration t Note over MCU: Measure pulse width t
Compute d equals v times t divided by 2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] Differentiate between **active** and **passive sensors**. Give **one example** of each. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Criterion | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor | |---|---|---| | Power | Requires external excitation | Self-generating | | Output | Modulated by excitation | Directly produced | | Example | Strain gauge, RTD | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric | *(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples — total 3 marks)* --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] What is the **role of an anti-aliasing filter** in a DSP-based sensor system? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An anti-aliasing filter is a **low-pass filter** placed between the sensor (after signal conditioning) and the ADC. Its role is to **remove frequency components above $f_s/2$** (the Nyquist frequency) **before sampling**, thereby preventing **aliasing** — the phenomenon where high-frequency signals appear as false low-frequency signals after sampling. The filter cutoff is typically chosen at **$f_c \approx 0.45 f_s$** to balance roll-off and passband fidelity. *(3 marks: 1 for placement, 1 for Nyquist relation, 1 for practical cutoff value)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — Sensor Signal Chain Design **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete signal chain** from a physical quantity (temperature) to actuator control. Label each block clearly and describe its function. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The complete signal chain for a temperature control application is: 1. **Physical Quantity (Temperature)** — The variable to be measured. 2. **Sensor (LM35)** — Converts temperature to a linear analog voltage at **$10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$**. 3. **Signal Conditioning**: - **Amplifier**: Boosts the small mV signal to a 0–3.3 V range. - **Low-Pass Filter**: Removes high-frequency noise (anti-aliasing). 4. **ADC**: Samples and quantizes the conditioned analog signal into digital codes. 5. **DSP / MCU**: Executes the control algorithm (e.g., PID), compares measured value to setpoint. 6. **DAC / PWM**: Generates a control signal proportional to the error. 7. **Actuator (Heater or Fan)**: Produces physical action to correct the temperature. 8. **Feedback**: The corrected temperature is sensed again, closing the loop. *(Block diagram: 2 marks, explanation of each block: 5 marks)* --- **(b)** An **LM35 sensor** outputs **$0.45 \text{ V}$** when connected to a **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$. Calculate: (i) The actual temperature in °C (ii) The digital output in decimal and binary (iii) The quantization step size and the theoretical SQNR **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Actual temperature:** $$ T = \frac{V_{out}}{\text{Sensitivity}} = \frac{0.45 \text{ V}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}/^{\circ}\text{C}} = 45^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **(ii) Digital output:** For $n = 10$, $2^n = 1024$: $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0.45}{5} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 92.07 \right\rfloor = 92 $$ In binary, $92_{10} = 0001011100_2$. **(iii) Quantization step size:** $$ \Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.883 \text{ mV} $$ **Theoretical SQNR:** $$ \text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB} \approx 62 \text{ dB} $$ *(2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii))* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Actuator & Distance Sensor **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of the **HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor** with a timing diagram. Derive the formula for distance measurement. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The HC-SR04 has **four pins**: VCC, GND, TRIG, and ECHO. **Working Principle:** 1. The MCU sends a **$10 \mu s$ pulse** to the TRIG pin. 2. The sensor internally emits **eight 40 kHz ultrasonic bursts**. 3. These waves travel through air, hit an object, and reflect back. 4. The ECHO pin is held **HIGH** for a duration $t$ proportional to the distance. 5. The MCU measures this pulse width using a timer. **Derivation:** Let $d$ = distance to object, $v$ = speed of sound, $t$ = measured echo time. The sound travels a total distance of $2d$ (to and fro) in time $t$: $$ 2d = v \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} $$ For accurate results, the speed of sound must be temperature-compensated: $$ v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s)} $$ *(3 marks for working, 2 marks for timing, 2 marks for derivation)* --- **(b)** A **servo motor** is controlled by a 50 Hz PWM signal (period = 20 ms). The pulse width is **$1.5 \text{ ms}$**. Determine the shaft angle. Also explain the role of the **H-bridge driver (L298N)** in driving a DC motor from a microcontroller. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Servo Angle Calculation:** For a standard SG90 servo: - $t_{min} = 1 \text{ ms} \rightarrow 0^{\circ}$ - $t_{max} = 2 \text{ ms} \rightarrow 180^{\circ}$ - Period = 20 ms ✓ $$ \theta = 0 + \frac{(1.5 - 1)}{(2 - 1)} \times 180 = \frac{0.5}{1} \times 180 = 90^{\circ} $$ **H-Bridge Driver (L298N) Role:** A microcontroller GPIO pin can source only **$\approx 20 \text{ mA}$**, which is **insufficient** to drive a DC motor (typically 200 mA – 2 A). The L298N H-bridge: 1. **Amplifies** the current to motor-rated levels. 2. Allows **bidirectional control** (forward and reverse) using two input pins. 3. Provides **electrical isolation**, protecting the MCU from back-EMF. 4. Accepts **PWM input** for speed control and logic input for direction. *(3 marks for servo calculation, 4 marks for H-bridge explanation)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing units**: Writing LM35 sensitivity as "10 mV" instead of "**10 mV/°C**". Always include the unit in the denominator. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance formulas — the echo time is **round-trip**, not one-way. > 3. **Wrong ADC formula**: Using $2^n$ instead of $(2^n - 1)$ in the digital output calculation, or vice versa. > 4. **Mixing up active and passive sensors**: Students often say "thermocouple is active" — it is **passive** (self-generating). Strain gauges **are active** (need excitation). > 5. **Missing anti-aliasing filter**: When asked about a sensor-to-DSP chain, always include the **low-pass filter** before the ADC. > 6. **Not labeling diagram axes**: In a servo or PWM question, forgetting to specify the period (20 ms) loses 1 mark. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** > > - Sensors = senses (input); Actuators = muscles (output) of an embedded system. > - LM35 sensitivity = **$10 \text{ mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$** (linear, analog, calibrated in °C). > - Thermistor is **non-linear**; use Steinhart–Hart equation for precision. > - Thermocouple is **passive and self-generating**; measures very high temperatures. > - DHT11 is **digital, single-wire**; gives temperature and humidity. > - HC-SR04 distance formula: $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$; speed of sound at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ = **343 m/s**. > - Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — sample at least twice the highest frequency. > - ADC step size: $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$. > - Quantization error: $e_q \le \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$. > - SQNR: $\approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB. > - Servo angle is controlled by **pulse width**, not duty cycle (1 ms = 0°, 2 ms = 180°). > - DC motor speed is controlled by **PWM duty cycle**; direction by **H-bridge**. > - Anti-aliasing filter is **mandatory** before any ADC in a sensor chain. > - **Active sensors** need external power; **passive sensors** generate their own output. > - Common interfaces: **I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire, Analog** — know the pin count and clock requirements.

topics.title: Signal Integration, Differentiation, and Logarithmic Computation Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Signal Integration, Differentiation, and Logarithmic Computation Activities ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Signal Integration** is the discrete-time numerical operation that computes the cumulative area under a sampled signal curve using finite-difference approximations of the continuous-time integral operator. In the KTU 2024 DSP Lab context, it is implemented using methods such as the **Rectangular Rule**, **Trapezoidal Rule**, and **Simpson's 1/3 Rule**. **Signal Differentiation** is the discrete-time numerical operation that estimates the instantaneous rate of change (slope) of a sampled signal at each sample point using finite-difference approximations of the continuous-time derivative operator. Standard methods include **Forward Difference**, **Backward Difference**, and **Central Difference** schemes. **Logarithmic Computation** refers to the conversion of signal amplitudes to the **logarithmic (decibel) domain**, primarily used for spectral analysis, dynamic range compression, and visualization. It is computed as: $$L_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{A}{A_{ref}}\right) \text{ for amplitude}$$ $$P_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) \text{ for power}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Directive (PCCPL507 - Experiment 2):** Students must demonstrate the generation of standard test signals (sine, cosine, exponential), apply numerical integration/differentiation using the built-in MATLAB functions (`cumtrapz`, `diff`, `log`, `log10`) as well as by writing **user-defined `.m` scripts** for academic understanding. Output waveforms and error plots must be submitted as part of the continuous evaluation record. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Speedometer & Odometer Analogy (Plain-English Intuition):** > > - **Differentiation** is like a **car's speedometer**. It tells you the *instantaneous rate* at which distance is changing at any given moment. If your odometer shows the car covers 60 km in 1 hour, the speedometer shows 60 km/h *at that instant*. In signals, differentiation gives the slope of the signal at every sample. > > - **Integration** is like the **odometer** itself. It *accumulates* the total distance traveled by summing up all the small speed contributions over time. Mathematically, it sums up the area under the signal curve. > > - **Logarithmic Computation** is like a **telescopic magnifying glass for tiny values**. Linear plots often hide small-amplitude details (e.g., side-lobes in a spectrum). The log (dB) scale "zooms in" on these weak components, making it indispensable in radar, audio engineering, and communications. A ratio of **1000:1** becomes a manageable **60 dB**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (Bolded for KTU Board Emphasis) - **Sampling Frequency (Fs):** The number of samples per second. For Experiment 2, the typical value is **Fs = 100 Hz** or higher. - **Signal Duration (t):** Total time window, usually **t = 0 to 1 second**. - **Reference Amplitude (A_ref):** Standard reference is **1 unit** for normalized dB conversion. - **Dynamic Range of Human Ear:** Approximately **0 dB (threshold) to 120 dB (pain)** — a ratio of **10^6 : 1**, demonstrating the necessity of logarithmic scales. - **Neper (Np):** Alternative logarithmic unit. **1 Np ≈ 8.686 dB**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic: How Numerical Integration Works The continuous integral of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as: $$y(t) = \int_{a}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$ In the discrete domain, the signal becomes $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period. The integral is approximated by summing weighted samples: **Step-by-step Operational Logic:** 1. **Discretize the time axis** into $N$ samples with spacing $T_s$. 2. **Choose a numerical integration rule** (Rectangular, Trapezoidal, or Simpson's). 3. **Apply the rule cumulatively** from the start of the signal to the end. 4. **Plot** the resulting integrated signal against time to verify the operation. ### 2.2 Operational Logic: How Numerical Differentiation Works The continuous derivative of $x(t)$ is: $$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{x(t + \Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}$$ Since discrete samples are spaced by finite $T_s$, the limit becomes an approximation: **Step-by-step Operational Logic:** 1. **Identify adjacent samples** $x[n]$ and $x[n+1]$. 2. **Compute the difference** $\Delta x = x[n+1] - x[n]$. 3. **Divide by the step size** $T_s$ to obtain the derivative estimate. 4. **Repeat for all sample pairs** to construct the differentiated signal. ### 2.3 Operational Logic: Logarithmic Computation in DSP 1. **Take the absolute value** of the signal (since log of a negative number is undefined in real domain). 2. **Apply `log10`** to convert to base-10 logarithm. 3. **Multiply by 20** for amplitude-domain dB scaling. 4. **Visualize** — this reveals spectral components that are invisible in linear plots. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is a high-yield revision block. Master every entry before your lab viva.** | Operation | Method | Formula | MATLAB Command | Truncation Error Order | |---|---|---|---|---| | Integration | Rectangular (Left) | $I = T_s \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$ | `sum(x(1:end-1))*Ts` | $O(T_s)$ | | Integration | Rectangular (Right) | $I = T_s \cdot \sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]$ | `sum(x(2:end))*Ts` | $O(T_s)$ | | Integration | Trapezoidal | $I = T_s \cdot \left[\frac{x_0 + x_N}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} x_n\right]$ | `cumtrapz(x)*Ts` | $O(T_s^2)$ | | Integration | Simpson's 1/3 Rule | $I = \frac{T_s}{3} \cdot [x_0 + x_N + 4 \sum x_{odd} + 2 \sum x_{even}]$ | User-defined `.m` file | $O(T_s^4)$ | | Differentiation | Forward Difference | $y[n] = \frac{x[n+1] - x[n]}{T_s}$ | `diff(x)/Ts` | $O(T_s)$ | | Differentiation | Backward Difference | $y[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[n-1]}{T_s}$ | `diff(x)/Ts` (shifted) | $O(T_s)$ | | Differentiation | Central Difference | $y[n] = \frac{x[n+1] - x[n-1]}{2T_s}$ | `(x(3:end)-x(1:end-2))/(2*Ts)` | $O(T_s^2)$ | | Log Computation | Linear-to-dB (Amplitude) | $L_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A \vert}{A_{ref}}\right)$ | `20*log10(abs(x))` | Exact | | Log Computation | Linear-to-dB (Power) | $P_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert P \vert}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | `10*log10(abs(x))` | Exact | | Log Computation | Natural Log | $y[n] = \ln(\vert x[n] \vert + \epsilon)$ | `log(abs(x) + eps)` | Exact (with $\epsilon$ guard) | > **Note on notation:** Vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ denote **absolute value (magnitude)**. A small constant $\epsilon$ (machine epsilon, $\approx 10^{-16}$) is added inside the log to prevent $\log(0) = -\infty$ errors — this is called the **$\epsilon$-padding technique** and is a favorite viva question. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why does KTU test this? Because every modern DSP system uses these operations:** > > - **Integration:** Used in **accumulators in digital filters**, **charge computation in biomedical signals** (ECG integration for heart rate variability), and **velocity-to-position conversion** in inertial navigation systems (INS). > - **Differentiation:** Used in **edge detection in image processing**, **frequency estimation** (zero-crossing detection), and **FM demodulation** in communication receivers. > - **Logarithmic Computation:** Used in **audio engineering** (VU meters are logarithmic), **spectral analysis** (FFT magnitude is plotted in dB), and **radar cross-section (RCS) measurement**, where target strengths span many orders of magnitude. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Trapezoidal Integration Rule **Problem Context:** Given a discrete signal $x[n]$ with $N$ samples and sampling period $T_s$, derive the trapezoidal integration formula and compute the integral of $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$ for $f = 5$ Hz, $F_s = 100$ Hz, $N = 100$. **Step 1: Start with the Definition of the Continuous Integral** The area under the curve from $t = 0$ to $t = t$ is: $$I(t) = \int_{0}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$ **Step 2: Discretize the Integral into a Sum of Trapezoids** Between two consecutive samples $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$, the area is approximated by a trapezoid of width $T_s$ and parallel sides $x(t_n)$ and $x(t_{n+1})$: $$A_n = \frac{1}{2} \cdot T_s \cdot \left[ x(t_n) + x(t_{n+1}) \right]$$ **Step 3: Sum All Trapezoids from $n = 0$ to $N - 1$** $$I = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n = \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ x(t_n) + x(t_{n+1}) \right]$$ **Step 4: Re-index to Combine Terms** Expanding the sum: $$I = \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot \left[ x(t_0) + 2x(t_1) + 2x(t_2) + \cdots + 2x(t_{N-1}) + x(t_N) \right]$$ **Step 5: Final Compact Form (KTU Board-Worthy Equation)** $$\boxed{I = T_s \cdot \left[ \frac{x(t_0) + x(t_N)}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} x(t_n) \right]}$$ **Step 6: Apply to the Sine Signal** For $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$ with $f = 5$ Hz, $T_s = 0.01$ s, $N = 100$: - $x(0) = \sin(0) = 0$ - $x(0.01) = \sin(0.1\pi) \approx 0.3090$ - $x(0.02) = \sin(0.2\pi) \approx 0.5878$ - ... (and so on for all 100 samples) **Step 7: Analytical (Exact) Integral for Comparison** The exact integral of $\sin(2\pi f t)$ from $0$ to $t$ is: $$I_{exact}(t) = -\frac{1}{2\pi f} \cos(2\pi f t) + \frac{1}{2\pi f}$$ For $t = 1$ s, $f = 5$ Hz: $$I_{exact}(1) = -\frac{1}{10\pi} \cos(10\pi) + \frac{1}{10\pi} = -\frac{1}{10\pi} (1) + \frac{1}{10\pi} = 0$$ This is correct since one full period of a sine wave integrated over its period yields **zero net area**. **Step 8: MATLAB Implementation for User-Defined Function** ```matlab % User-defined Trapezoidal Integration for a Sine Wave fs = 100; % Sampling frequency t = 0:1/fs:1; % Time vector x = sin(2*pi*5*t); % Signal: 5 Hz sine Ts = 1/fs; % Sampling period N = length(x); % Number of samples % Initialize cumulative integral I_trap = zeros(1, N); % Apply trapezoidal rule cumulatively for n = 2:N I_trap(n) = I_trap(n-1) + (Ts/2)*(x(n) + x(n-1)); end % Compare with built-in function I_builtin = cumtrapz(x) * Ts; % Plot results figure; subplot(3,1,1); plot(t, x); title('Original Signal x(t) = sin(2\pi 5t)'); xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('Amplitude'); grid on; subplot(3,1,2); plot(t, I_trap, 'b', t, I_builtin, 'r--'); title('Trapezoidal Integration: User-defined (blue) vs Built-in (red dashed)'); xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('Integral'); legend('User-defined', 'cumtrapz'); grid on; subplot(3,1,3); plot(t, abs(I_trap - I_builtin)); title('Error between User-defined and Built-in Methods'); xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('|Error|'); grid on; ``` **Step 9: Expected Output (Analytical Result)** The integrated signal $I(t) = -\frac{1}{10\pi} \cos(2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot t) + \frac{1}{10\pi}$ is a **cosine wave of frequency 5 Hz with amplitude $\frac{1}{10\pi} \approx 0.0318$**, oscillating around a mean of $\frac{1}{10\pi} \approx 0.0318$. The error between user-defined and built-in methods should be on the order of **10⁻¹⁶** (machine precision). --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Central Difference Differentiation **Problem Context:** Derive the central difference approximation and apply it to $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$ at $f = 5$ Hz. **Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion of $x(t + T_s)$ and $x(t - T_s)$** $$x(t + T_s) = x(t) + T_s \cdot x'(t) + \frac{T_s^2}{2} x''(t) + \frac{T_s^3}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ $$x(t - T_s) = x(t) - T_s \cdot x'(t) + \frac{T_s^2}{2} x''(t) - \frac{T_s^3}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ **Step 2: Subtract the Two Expansions** $$x(t + T_s) - x(t - T_s) = 2 T_s \cdot x'(t) + O(T_s^3)$$ The $T_s^2$ terms cancel out, and the leading error term is $O(T_s^3)$, which when divided by $2T_s$ gives $O(T_s^2)$ — **second-order accurate**. **Step 3: Solve for $x'(t)$** $$x'(t) = \frac{x(t + T_s) - x(t - T_s)}{2 T_s} + O(T_s^2)$$ **Step 4: Discrete-Time Form** $$\boxed{y[n] = \frac{x[n+1] - x[n-1]}{2 T_s}}$$ **Step 5: Apply to Sine Wave** Analytical derivative: $\frac{d}{dt}\sin(2\pi f t) = 2\pi f \cos(2\pi f t) = 10\pi \cos(10\pi t)$ For $f = 5$ Hz, this is a **cosine of amplitude $10\pi \approx 31.416$** at 5 Hz. **Step 6: MATLAB Implementation** ```matlab % User-defined Central Difference Differentiation fs = 100; t = 0:1/fs:1; x = sin(2*pi*5*t); Ts = 1/fs; % Central difference (loses first and last sample) y_central = (x(3:end) - x(1:end-2)) / (2*Ts); t_central = t(2:end-1); % Forward difference (built-in diff) y_forward = diff(x) / Ts; t_forward = t(1:end-1); % Exact analytical derivative y_exact = 10*pi * cos(2*pi*5*t); % Plot figure; plot(t, y_exact, 'k', 'LineWidth', 1.5); hold on; plot(t_central, y_central, 'ro--'); plot(t_forward, y_forward, 'b^--'); title('Differentiation Methods Comparison'); xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('dy/dt'); legend('Exact', 'Central Diff (O(T_s^2))', 'Forward Diff (O(T_s))'); grid on; ``` **Step 7: Error Analysis** The maximum absolute error for the **forward difference** method is: $$E_{forward} = \frac{T_s}{2} \max \vert x''(t) \vert = \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot (2\pi f)^2$$ For $f = 5$ Hz, $T_s = 0.01$: $$E_{forward} = \frac{0.01}{2} \cdot (10\pi)^2 = 0.5 \pi^2 \approx 4.93$$ The central difference error is: $$E_{central} = \frac{T_s^2}{6} \max \vert x'''(t) \vert$$ This is **100× smaller** for the same sampling rate, demonstrating why central difference is preferred when the signal is known at past and future samples. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Error Convergence Table | Method | $T_s = 0.1$ | $T_s = 0.01$ | $T_s = 0.001$ | Convergence Rate | |---|---|---|---|---| | Forward Difference | $\sim 4.93$ | $\sim 0.493$ | $\sim 0.0493$ | Linear ($O(T_s)$) | | Central Difference | $\sim 0.041$ | $\sim 4.1 \times 10^{-4}$ | $\sim 4.1 \times 10^{-6}$ | Quadratic ($O(T_s^2)$) | | Trapezoidal Integration | $\sim 0.0032$ | $\sim 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\sim 3.2 \times 10^{-7}$ | Quadratic ($O(T_s^2)$) | | Simpson's 1/3 Rule | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-12}$ | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-16}$ | Quartic ($O(T_s^4)$) | > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Halving $T_s$ (doubling the sampling rate) reduces the forward-difference error by a factor of 2, but the central-difference error by a factor of 4. Simpson's 1/3 rule reduces it by a factor of 16. This is why **high-order methods are essential in precision DSP applications**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Experiment 2 Procedure Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Load Experiment 2 Script]) --> initParams initParams["Initialize Parameters:
fs = 100 Hz
t = 0 to 1 s
N = 100 samples
f_signal = 5 Hz"] initParams --> genSignal genSignal["Generate Test Signal:
x_t = sin(2*pi*f*t)
Plot Original Waveform"] genSignal --> branchOp branchOp{Branch by
User Choice} branchOp --> integPath[Integration Path] branchOp --> diffPath[Differentiation Path] branchOp --> logPath[Logarithmic Path] integPath --> integRules integRules["Apply Integration Rules:
1. Rectangular Left and Right
2. Trapezoidal cumtrapz
3. Simpson 1/3 User Defined"] integRules --> integPlot integPlot["Plot Integrated Signal
Compute Error vs Analytical
Display Convergence Table"] diffPath --> diffRules diffRules["Apply Differentiation Rules:
1. Forward diff x
2. Backward diff
3. Central Difference User Defined"] diffRules --> diffPlot diffPlot["Plot Derivative Signal
Compare with 10pi cos 2 pi f t
Display Error Magnitudes"] logPath --> logSteps logSteps["Apply Log Conversion:
1. x_dB = 20 log10 abs x plus eps
2. Power dB = 10 log10
3. Plot Linear vs Log Scales"] logSteps --> logPlot logPlot["Display Dynamic Range
Compare Linear and Log Plots
Show Side Lobe Visibility"] integPlot --> saveAll diffPlot --> saveAll logPlot --> saveAll saveAll["Save All Figures:
fig1_original.png
fig2_integration.png
fig3_differentiation.png
fig4_logscale.png"] saveAll --> endNode([End: Submit to Lab Record]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Signal Processing Pipeline Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Signal Source
Sine Generator"] --> adc["Sampler
Fs = 100 Hz"] adc --> proc{"DSP Processor
MATLAB Engine"} proc --> intBlock["Integration Block
cumtrapz
Simpson User Defined"] proc --> diffBlock["Differentiation Block
diff Forward
Central User Defined"] proc --> logBlock["Logarithmic Block
20 log10
10 log10"] intBlock --> compBank["Comparison Bank
Error Metrics"] diffBlock --> compBank logBlock --> compBank compBank --> dispUnit["Display Unit
Plots and Figures"] compBank --> saveUnit["Storage
PNG and FIG files"] dispUnit --> viva["Viva Voce
