Explanation of Results"] saveUnit --> record["Lab Record Submission"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Logarithmic Domain Conversion ```mermaid flowchart TB xLinear["Linear Signal x n
Amplitude Range 0.001 to 1000"] xLinear --> absBlock["Absolute Value
abs x n"] absBlock --> epsAdd["Epsilon Padding
add eps = 2.2e-16"] epsAdd --> logBranch{Branch by Domain} logBranch --> ampDB["Amplitude dB
20 log10 x"] logBranch --> pwrDB["Power dB
10 log10 x squared"] logBranch --> natLog["Natural Log
ln x for Math Operations"] ampDB --> plotLinear["Linear Axis Plot
y from 60 to 60 dB"] pwrDB --> plotLinear natLog --> plotLinear plotLinear --> insight["Engineering Insight
Reveals Hidden Weak Components
Compresses Dynamic Range"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > > **Q1.** Define numerical integration. List any two methods used for numerical integration of discrete-time signals. > > **Model Answer:** > Numerical integration is the process of computing the area under a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by approximating the continuous integral $\int x(t) \, dt$ using finite-difference rules. Two common methods are: > 1. **Trapezoidal Rule** — approximates the area between two samples as a trapezoid: $I = T_s \cdot \left[\frac{x_0 + x_N}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} x_n\right]$, with second-order accuracy $O(T_s^2)$. > 2. **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** — uses parabolic arcs over three consecutive samples: $I = \frac{T_s}{3} \cdot [x_0 + x_N + 4 \sum x_{odd} + 2 \sum x_{even}]$, with fourth-order accuracy $O(T_s^4)$. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > > **Q2.** What is the significance of logarithmic computation in DSP? Write the formula for converting a voltage signal to decibels. > > **Model Answer:** > Logarithmic computation **compresses the dynamic range** of a signal, enabling visualization of components that differ by several orders of magnitude (e.g., spectral side-lobes). It is essential in audio metering, spectral analysis, and radar cross-section measurement. > > The decibel conversion formula for an amplitude (voltage) signal is: > $$V_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert V \vert}{V_{ref}}\right)$$ > A common reference is $V_{ref} = 1$ V, giving $V_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert V \vert$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2]** > > ### Question A (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Derive the trapezoidal rule for numerical integration of a discrete signal $x[n]$ with $N$ samples and sampling period $T_s$. Show that the truncation error is of order $O(T_s^2)$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For the signal $x(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 5 t)$ sampled at $F_s = 100$ Hz for 1 second, write a user-defined MATLAB function to compute the integral using the trapezoidal rule. Compare your result with the built-in `cumtrapz` function and plot the error. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Derivation:** > 1. Between samples $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$, the area is approximated as a trapezoid: $A_n = \frac{T_s}{2}[x(t_n) + x(t_{n+1})]$. > 2. Summing over all intervals: > > $$I = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n = \frac{T_s}{2}\left[x_0 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{N-1} x_n + x_N\right]$$ > > 3. To find the truncation error, expand $x(t + T_s)$ in Taylor series: > > $$x(t + T_s) = x(t) + T_s x'(t) + \frac{T_s^2}{2} x''(t) + \frac{T_s^3}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ > > 4. The true area under the curve between $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$ is $\int_{t_n}^{t_{n+1}} x(\tau)d\tau$. The trapezoidal approximation differs from this by: > > $$E_n = \int_{t_n}^{t_{n+1}} x(\tau)d\tau - \frac{T_s}{2}[x(t_n) + x(t_{n+1})]$$ > > 5. Substituting the Taylor expansion and integrating term by term, the $T_s$ and $T_s^2$ terms cancel, leaving the leading error term as $-\frac{T_s^3}{12} x''(\xi_n)$ for some $\xi_n \in [t_n, t_{n+1}]$. > > 6. Total error: $E_{total} = \sum E_n = O(T_s^2)$ globally (after multiplying by $N \propto 1/T_s$). > > **(b) MATLAB Code:** > > ```matlab > fs = 100; t = 0:1/fs:1; x = sin(2*pi*5*t); > Ts = 1/fs; N = length(x); > I_user = zeros(1, N); > for n = 2:N > I_user(n) = I_user(n-1) + (Ts/2)*(x(n) + x(n-1)); > end > I_builtin = cumtrapz(x) * Ts; > error_signal = abs(I_user - I_builtin); > subplot(2,1,1); plot(t, I_user, 'b', t, I_builtin, 'r--'); > legend('User', 'cumtrapz'); title('Integration Comparison'); > subplot(2,1,2); plot(t, error_signal); > title('Absolute Error'); ylabel('|Error|'); > max(error_signal) > ``` > > The maximum error should be on the order of **10⁻¹⁵** (machine precision), confirming the correctness of the user-defined function. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2]** > > ### Question B (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Derive the central difference formula for numerical differentiation. Explain why it is more accurate than the forward difference method. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A signal $x(t) = e^{-2t} \cos(4\pi t)$ is sampled at $F_s = 200$ Hz for $t \in [0, 2]$ seconds. Write a MATLAB script to compute and plot: > - (i) The forward difference approximation of the derivative > - (ii) The central difference approximation of the derivative > - (iii) The analytical derivative for comparison > Display the maximum error for both methods in a table. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Derivation:** > 1. Taylor expansions around $t$: > > $$x(t+T_s) = x(t) + T_s x'(t) + \frac{T_s^2}{2} x''(t) + \frac{T_s^3}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ > > $$x(t-T_s) = x(t) - T_s x'(t) + \frac{T_s^2}{2} x''(t) - \frac{T_s^3}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ > > 2. Subtracting: > > $$x(t+T_s) - x(t-T_s) = 2T_s x'(t) + \frac{T_s^3}{3} x'''(t) + O(T_s^5)$$ > > 3. Solving: > > $$x'(t) = \frac{x(t+T_s) - x(t-T_s)}{2T_s} - \frac{T_s^2}{6} x'''(t) + O(T_s^4)$$ > > 4. **Why more accurate:** The leading error term is $O(T_s^2)$ for central difference versus $O(T_s)$ for forward difference. The Taylor series terms of even powers cancel out, eliminating the dominant first-order error. > > **(b) MATLAB Code:** > > ```matlab > fs = 200; t = 0:1/fs:2; x = exp(-2*t) .* cos(4*pi*t); > Ts = 1/fs; > > % Forward difference > y_fwd = diff(x) / Ts; > t_fwd = t(1:end-1); > > % Central difference > y_ctr = (x(3:end) - x(1:end-2)) / (2*Ts); > t_ctr = t(2:end-1); > > % Analytical derivative: d/dt [e^(-2t) cos(4pi t)] > % = -2 e^(-2t) cos(4pi t) - 4pi e^(-2t) sin(4pi t) > y_exact = -2*exp(-2*t).*cos(4*pi*t) - 4*pi*exp(-2*t).*sin(4*pi*t); > > % Errors > err_fwd = abs(y_fwd - y_exact(1:end-1)); > err_ctr = abs(y_ctr - y_exact(2:end-1)); > > % Display table > fprintf('Method | Max Error\n'); > fprintf('----------------|----------\n'); > fprintf('Forward Diff | %.6f\n', max(err_fwd)); > fprintf('Central Diff | %.6f\n', max(err_ctr)); > > % Plot > figure; > subplot(2,1,1); plot(t, y_exact, 'k', t_fwd, y_fwd, 'b--', t_ctr, y_ctr, 'r:'); > legend('Exact', 'Forward', 'Central'); title('Derivative Approximations'); > subplot(2,1,2); semilogy(t_fwd, err_fwd, t_ctr, err_ctr); > legend('Forward Err', 'Central Err'); title('Error (Log Scale)'); > ``` > > Expected result: Central difference error should be **~10× to 100× smaller** than forward difference error. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Board Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\epsilon$-padding inside `log()`:** If the signal contains zero samples, `log10(0)` returns `-Inf` and the plot breaks. Always use `log10(abs(x) + eps)` or check for zeros beforehand. **Deduction: 1-2 marks.** > > 2. **Confusing amplitude (20 log₁₀) vs power (10 log₁₀) dB formulas:** Voltage and current signals use the factor of 20; power signals use 10. Mixing them up is a classic error. **Deduction: 1 mark.** > > 3. **Forgetting to divide by $T_s$ in differentiation:** `diff(x)` alone gives the *unscaled* numerical difference. The derivative is `diff(x)/Ts`. **Deduction: 1-2 marks.** > > 4. **Trapezoidal rule off-by-one error:** The summation index must be carefully handled. Writing `sum(x)` instead of `(x(1)+x(end))/2 + sum(x(2:end-1))` causes a small but persistent error. **Deduction: 1 mark.** > > 5. **Not labeling axes, legends, or titles in plots:** KTU lab evaluation strictly requires properly labeled figures. **Deduction: 0.5-1 mark per unlabeled plot.** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for the night before the lab exam):** > > - **Integration (Trapezoidal):** $I = T_s \cdot \left[\frac{x_0 + x_N}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} x_n\right]$; MATLAB command is `cumtrapz(x) * Ts`. > - **Integration (Simpson's 1/3):** Requires **odd number of intervals** (even number of sample points). Uses $4 \times$ odd-indexed, $2 \times$ even-indexed interior samples. > - **Differentiation (Forward):** $y[n] = \frac{x[n+1] - x[n]}{T_s}$; produces **N-1 points** from N samples. First-order accurate. > - **Differentiation (Central):** $y[n] = \frac{x[n+1] - x[n-1]}{2T_s}$; produces **N-2 points**. Second-order accurate — preferred when future samples are available. > - **dB Conversion (Amplitude):** $20 \log_{10} \left(\frac{\vert x \vert}{x_{ref}}\right)$. > - **dB Conversion (Power):** $10 \log_{10} \left(\frac{\vert P \vert}{P_{ref}}\right)$. > - **$\epsilon$-Padding:** Always use `log10(abs(x) + eps)` to avoid `log(0) = -Inf` errors. > - **Error Order:** Forward/Backward Diff $= O(T_s)$; Central Diff $= O(T_s^2)$; Trapezoidal $= O(T_s^2)$; Simpson's 1/3 $= O(T_s^4)$. > - **Viva-Favorite Question:** "Why do we need to add `eps` before taking the log?" — Answer: To prevent numerical singularity when the signal value is exactly zero, since $\log_{10}(0)$ is undefined (approaches $-\infty$). > - **Viva-Favorite Question:** "Why is the central difference more accurate than forward difference?" — Answer: Because the Taylor series error terms of order $T_s$ cancel out, leaving only $O(T_s^2)$ truncation error. > - **MATLAB Must-Know Commands:** `cumtrapz`, `diff`, `log`, `log10`, `abs`, `subplot`, `plot`, `grid on`, `legend`, `xlabel`, `title`. ---

topics.title: Signal Addition, Subtraction, and Multiplication Computation Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Addition, Subtraction, and Multiplication Computation Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCPL507 – Digital Signal Processing Laboratory**, *Module Experiment 2* focuses on the most fundamental arithmetic operations applied to **discrete-time signals (DTS)**. These operations — **Addition, Subtraction, and Multiplication** — form the bedrock of virtually every DSP algorithm, including convolution, correlation, modulation, and filtering.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is a function defined only at integer values of the independent variable $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. The three elementary operations in this experiment are defined, for any two signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, as:

$$y_{add}[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$$

$$y_{sub}[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$$

$$y_{mul}[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$$

These are **point-wise (element-wise)** operations performed at *each corresponding sample index* $n$. The mathematical operator must be applied sample-by-sample; there is no convolution, integration, or any "spreading" of samples involved at this stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab manual explicitly uses the term *"Computation Activities"* to emphasize that the student must compute, plot, and verify these operations on at least two distinct signal types — typically a **sine wave** and a **square / pulse / random signal** — to demonstrate understanding across different waveform families.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **DJ mixing two music tracks** at a concert:
- **Signal Addition** is like playing *Track A* and *Track B* through the speakers **simultaneously** — the air pressure at your eardrum is literally the **sum** of pressure waves from both tracks.
- **Signal Subtraction** is what a **noise-canceling headphone** does — it takes the ambient noise and produces an *anti-noise* signal, which when added (in the destructive sense) cancels the original.
- **Signal Multiplication** is what happens during **Amplitude Modulation (AM radio)** — the voice signal is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier wave so it can travel long distances through the air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Even the most advanced DSP operations (like the Fast Fourier Transform or adaptive filters) are built from these three elementary arithmetic blocks. Mastering them in the lab is the gateway to everything else in digital signal processing.

### 1.3 Key Lab Parameters to Remember

The following standard metrics must be explicitly declared by every KTU student in their lab record:

- **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$):** typically **8000 Hz, 1000 Hz, or 100 Hz** depending on signal content.
- **Duration / Time Vector ($t$ or $n$):** discrete integer index $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$.
- **Signal Length ($N$):** total number of samples.
- **Amplitude ($A$):** peak value of the sinusoidal signal, commonly **1 V, 2 V, or 5 V**.
- **Frequency ($f$):** in Hz, must obey **Nyquist criterion** $f < F_s/2$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Broken Down Step by Step

For two arbitrary discrete-time signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ both defined over the same index range $n \in [n_1, n_2]$:

**Step 1 — Index Alignment (Critical Pre-requisite):**
Both signals must be defined over the **same discrete time axis** $n$. If they are not, the shorter signal must be **zero-padded** so that both vectors have identical length $N$.

**Step 2 — Element-wise Operator Application:**
The DSP engine (MATLAB / Python / Scilab) iterates through every index $n$ and applies the corresponding arithmetic operator to the pair $(x_1[n],\, x_2[n])$.

**Step 3 — Result Storage:**
The output $y[n]$ is stored in a new vector of length $N$, where every sample is the result of the operation.

**Step 4 — Visualization:**
The student must plot **all three signals** ($x_1$, $x_2$, and $y$) on a single figure using `subplot()` or `tiledlayout()` for a clean comparison. Markers (`'o'`, `'*'`) are typically used for discrete-time stem plots.

**Step 5 — Verification:**
The student manually computes at least 2–3 samples by hand and cross-checks with the program output. This is a KTU examiner's pet check.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for Module Experiment 2. Memorize it before entering the exam lab.

| Operation | Mathematical Form | Output Length Condition | Amplitude Behaviour | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Addition** | $y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ | $N_y = \max(N_1, N_2)$ (with zero-padding) | Grows if both positive; can exceed $A$ | Audio mixing, beamforming, signal superposition |
| **Subtraction** | $y[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$ | $N_y = \max(N_1, N_2)$ | Can be negative; centred around 0 | Noise cancellation, baseline removal, ECG R-peak detection |
| **Multiplication** | $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | $N_y = \max(N_1, N_2)$ | Product of amplitudes; can be very large | AM modulation, windowing, gating, sideband generation |
| **Scalar Multiply** | $y[n] = A \cdot x[n]$ | $N_y = N_x$ | Scaled by factor $A$ | Volume control, gain adjustment, normalization |
| **Time Index** | $n = [n_0, n_0+1, \dots, n_0+N-1]$ | $N$ samples | N/A | Foundation of every DTS |
| **Nyquist Rule** | $f_{max} \leq F_s/2$ | N/A | Prevents aliasing | Sampling theorem compliance |
| **Sample Count** | $N = F_s \cdot T_{duration}$ | In samples | N/A | Designing the time vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** In multiplication, if both signals have amplitudes greater than 1, the output can become numerically huge and may **saturate** when displayed on a 16-bit DAC in a real DSP processor. This is why a *gain factor* $A \in (0,1)$ is often multiplied after the operation — a concept called **dynamic range scaling**.

### 2.3 Why These Operations Matter in Engineering

- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG signals are cleaned by *subtracting* the baseline wander.
- **Telecommunications:** AM transmitters use *multiplication* of message and carrier; receivers use *addition* of multiple channels.
- **Audio Engineering:** Equalizers *add* filtered components; compressors *multiply* by a variable gain.
- **Radar & SONAR:** Echo detection uses *correlation*, which internally uses *multiplication and addition*.
- **Image Processing (2D extension):** Every pixel arithmetic — brightness, contrast, blending — is a 2D extension of these same three operations.

### 2.4 Choosing Signal Types for the Experiment

The KTU lab manual and university exam expect students to test with at least these combinations:

1. **Sinusoidal + Sinusoidal** (e.g., $x_1 = \sin(2\pi f_1 n/F_s)$, $x_2 = 0.5 \sin(2\pi f_2 n/F_s)$)
2. **Sinusoidal + Square / Pulse train** (e.g., $x_1 = \sin(\cdot)$, $x_2 = \text{square}(\cdot)$ or unit step)
3. **Triangular + Random** (less common but acceptable for higher marks)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Hand-Computation Expected in Record)

Let us define two discrete-time signals over $N = 8$ samples starting at $n = 0$:

$$x_1[n] = \{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 4,\ 3,\ 2,\ 1\}$$

$$x_2[n] = \{0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 3,\ 2,\ 1,\ 0\}$$

> Both signals are of length **8** and aligned on the **same time index** $n = 0, 1, \dots, 7$, so no zero-padding is required.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Addition Derivation

For each index $n$, we compute $y_{add}[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{add}[0] &= x_1[0] + x_2[0] = 1 + 0 = 1 \\
y_{add}[1] &= x_1[1] + x_2[1] = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
y_{add}[2] &= x_1[2] + x_2[2] = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
y_{add}[3] &= x_1[3] + x_2[3] = 4 + 3 = 7 \\
y_{add}[4] &= x_1[4] + x_2[4] = 4 + 3 = 7 \\
y_{add}[5] &= x_1[5] + x_2[5] = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
y_{add}[6] &= x_1[6] + x_2[6] = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
y_{add}[7] &= x_1[7] + x_2[7] = 1 + 0 = 1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Final addition result:

$$y_{add}[n] = \{1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 7,\ 7,\ 5,\ 3,\ 1\}$$

> Note that the peak amplitude has **doubled** (from 4 to 7 in this case) — this is the **superposition principle** in action.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Subtraction Derivation

For each index $n$, we compute $y_{sub}[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{sub}[0] &= 1 - 0 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[1] &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[2] &= 3 - 2 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[3] &= 4 - 3 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[4] &= 4 - 3 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[5] &= 3 - 2 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[6] &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
y_{sub}[7] &= 1 - 0 = 1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Final subtraction result:

$$y_{sub}[n] = \{1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1\}$$

> This is a **DC (constant) signal** — subtraction of two similarly shaped triangular signals naturally produces a flat line, demonstrating **baseline extraction** in disguise.

#### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Multiplication Derivation

For each index $n$, we compute $y_{mul}[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{mul}[0] &= 1 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
y_{mul}[1] &= 2 \cdot 1 = 2 \\
y_{mul}[2] &= 3 \cdot 2 = 6 \\
y_{mul}[3] &= 4 \cdot 3 = 12 \\
y_{mul}[4] &= 4 \cdot 3 = 12 \\
y_{mul}[5] &= 3 \cdot 2 = 6 \\
y_{mul}[6] &= 2 \cdot 1 = 2 \\
y_{mul}[7] &= 1 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Final multiplication result:

$$y_{mul}[n] = \{0,\ 2,\ 6,\ 12,\ 12,\ 6,\ 2,\ 0\}$$

> The output peaks at **12**, which is the product of the individual peaks ($4 \times 3$). This is the **amplitude modulation envelope** behaviour.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Observation Summary

| Index $n$ | $x_1[n]$ | $x_2[n]$ | $x_1+x_2$ | $x_1-x_2$ | $x_1 \cdot x_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 6 |
| 3 | 4 | 3 | 7 | 1 | 12 |
| 4 | 4 | 3 | 7 | 1 | 12 |
| 5 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 6 |
| 6 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 7 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** Examiners love asking "what is the peak amplitude of the multiplied signal?" The student must answer correctly as $\max(\vert x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] \vert)$, demonstrating they understand the **envelope** concept of AM modulation.

### 3.3 Continuous-Time Equivalent (For Conceptual Clarity)

If we think of the same operations in continuous-time $t$:

$$y_{add}(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$$

$$y_{sub}(t) = x_1(t) - x_2(t)$$

$$y_{mul}(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$$

For sinusoidal inputs $x_1(t) = A_1 \sin(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $x_2(t) = A_2 \sin(2\pi f_2 t)$:

The product expands using the **trigonometric identity**:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{mul}(t) &= A_1 A_2 \sin(2\pi f_1 t) \sin(2\pi f_2 t) \\
&= \frac{A_1 A_2}{2} \left[ \cos(2\pi(f_1 - f_2)t) - \cos(2\pi(f_1 + f_2)t) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **fundamental AM modulation identity** — the student should be able to derive it on the spot during the viva voce. The two cosine terms are the **lower sideband** and **upper sideband** respectively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the complete workflow of Module Experiment 2 — from input signal generation to final verification — and is exactly what a KTU examiner expects in the **Design / Procedure** section of the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Lab Experiment 2] --> B[Declare Parameters: Fs, N, f1, f2, A1, A2]
    B --> C[Generate Time Vector n = 0 to N-1]
    C --> D1[Generate Signal x1 n: e.g. A1 sin 2 pi f1 n by Fs]
    C --> D2[Generate Signal x2 n: e.g. A2 sin 2 pi f2 n by Fs]
    D1 --> E[Index Alignment Check: length x1 equals length x2]
    D2 --> E
    E -->|Yes| F1[Compute y add: x1 plus x2]
    E -->|No| ZP[Zero Pad Shorter Signal] --> F1
    E -->|Yes| F2[Compute y sub: x1 minus x2]
    E -->|No| ZP2[Zero Pad Shorter Signal] --> F2
    E -->|Yes| F3[Compute y mul: x1 dot x2]
    E -->|No| ZP3[Zero Pad Shorter Signal] --> F3
    F1 --> G[Plot All Signals using subplot 4 1 1 to 4]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Verify with Hand Calculation: 2 to 3 samples]
    H --> I[Write Inference and Conclusion]
    I --> J[End: Record Submission]
```

### 4.2 Subplot Layout Diagram (Expected Figure in Lab Record)

The standard KTU-accepted figure must contain **four vertically stacked subplots** in this exact order:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Subplot 1: x1 n Original] --> P2[Subplot 2: x2 n Original]
    P2 --> P3[Subplot 3: x1 n plus x2 n / x1 n minus x2 n]
    P3 --> P4[Subplot 4: x1 n times x2 n]
    P4 --> P5[Common X axis: n from 0 to N minus 1]
    P5 --> P6[Common Title: Signal Arithmetic Operations]
```

### 4.3 Operator Behaviour Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Input Stage]
        X1[x1 n vector of length N]
        X2[x2 n vector of length N]
    end
    subgraph S2[Operator Block]
        OP1[ADD operator plus]
        OP2[SUB operator minus]
        OP3[MUL operator times]
    end
    subgraph S3[Output Stage]
        Y1[y add n]
        Y2[y sub n]
        Y3[y mul n]
    end
    X1 --> OP1
    X2 --> OP1
    X1 --> OP2
    X2 --> OP2
    X1 --> OP3
    X2 --> OP3
    OP1 --> Y1
    OP2 --> Y2
    OP3 --> Y3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Operator Block** is the heart of the experiment. In MATLAB it is implemented using the inbuilt arithmetic operators `+`, `-`, `*`; in Python with NumPy, the same symbols work on `ndarray` objects because NumPy overloads them to perform element-wise operations by default.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define element-wise signal addition. State two real-world applications where signal addition is used in engineering systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Element-wise signal addition is the operation $y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ where the two input signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ are defined over the same discrete index $n$ and a sample-wise summation is performed to produce the output signal $y[n]$ of identical length. This is also called the **superposition principle** in DTS.
**Two real-world applications (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Audio mixing in music production:** Multiple instrument tracks are added sample-by-sample in a digital audio workstation (DAW) to produce the final stereo output.
2. **Beamforming in antenna arrays:** Signals received at multiple antenna elements are added with appropriate weights to electronically steer the beam toward a desired user.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between signal subtraction and signal multiplication. How is the amplitude of the output signal affected in each case?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Subtraction** $y[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$ produces an output whose amplitude is the **difference** of the two input samples at each index; the output can be positive, negative, or zero, and the peak amplitude is typically **bounded by the larger of the two inputs**.
- **Multiplication** $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ produces an output whose amplitude is the **product** of the two input samples; the peak amplitude can be **significantly larger** than either input (e.g., $4 \times 3 = 12$) and can lead to **saturation** in fixed-point DSP hardware.
- **Key difference (1 mark):** Subtraction is a *linear* operation preserving dynamic range, while multiplication is *non-linear* in amplitude and forms the basis of **modulation**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **Choose A or B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Consider two discrete-time signals defined as
$$x_1[n] = 2\sin\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right), \quad x_2[n] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right)$$
for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 7$ with sampling frequency $F_s = 8$ Hz.

Compute the following output signals and tabulate the values for $n = 0$ to $7$: **(7 marks)**
  1. $y_1[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$
  2. $y_2[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$

**(b)** Write the complete algorithm (pseudocode) to perform element-wise addition, subtraction, and multiplication of two discrete-time signals. Draw the subplot layout that must appear in the lab record. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Step-by-step numerical computation (7 marks):**

For $n = 0$ to $7$, we first compute $x_1[n] = 2\sin(\pi n/4)$ and $x_2[n] = \cos(\pi n/4)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1[0] &= 2\sin(0) = 0, \quad x_2[0] = \cos(0) = 1 \\
x_1[1] &= 2\sin(\pi/4) = 2 \cdot 0.7071 = 1.4142, \quad x_2[1] = \cos(\pi/4) = 0.7071 \\
x_1[2] &= 2\sin(\pi/2) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2, \quad x_2[2] = \cos(\pi/2) = 0 \\
x_1[3] &= 2\sin(3\pi/4) = 2 \cdot 0.7071 = 1.4142, \quad x_2[3] = \cos(3\pi/4) = -0.7071 \\
x_1[4] &= 2\sin(\pi) = 0, \quad x_2[4] = \cos(\pi) = -1 \\
x_1[5] &= 2\sin(5\pi/4) = -1.4142, \quad x_2[5] = \cos(5\pi/4) = -0.7071 \\
x_1[6] &= 2\sin(3\pi/2) = -2, \quad x_2[6] = \cos(3\pi/2) = 0 \\
x_1[7] &= 2\sin(7\pi/4) = -1.4142, \quad x_2[7] = \cos(7\pi/4) = 0.7071 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute $y_1[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1[0] &= 0 + 1 = 1 \\
y_1[1] &= 1.4142 + 0.7071 = 2.1213 \\
y_1[2] &= 2 + 0 = 2 \\
y_1[3] &= 1.4142 + (-0.7071) = 0.7071 \\
y_1[4] &= 0 + (-1) = -1 \\
y_1[5] &= -1.4142 + (-0.7071) = -2.1213 \\
y_1[6] &= -2 + 0 = -2 \\
y_1[7] &= -1.4142 + 0.7071 = -0.7071 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Resulting vector:

$$y_1[n] = \{1,\ 2.1213,\ 2,\ 0.7071,\ -1,\ -2.1213,\ -2,\ -0.7071\}$$

Now compute $y_2[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_2[0] &= 0 \cdot 1 = 0 \\
y_2[1] &= 1.4142 \cdot 0.7071 = 1.0000 \\
y_2[2] &= 2 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
y_2[3] &= 1.4142 \cdot (-0.7071) = -1.0000 \\
y_2[4] &= 0 \cdot (-1) = 0 \\
y_2[5] &= (-1.4142) \cdot (-0.7071) = 1.0000 \\
y_2[6] &= (-2) \cdot 0 = 0 \\
y_2[7] &= (-1.4142) \cdot 0.7071 = -1.0000 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Resulting vector:

$$y_2[n] = \{0,\ 1,\ 0,\ -1,\ 0,\ 1,\ 0,\ -1\}$$

**Observation (1 mark):** $y_2[n]$ is a perfect **square wave** of amplitude $\pm 1$. This is the **DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier) modulation** output, a classic application of signal multiplication.

**(b) Algorithm and Subplot Layout (7 marks):**

**Pseudocode (MATLAB-style):**
```
Step 1:  Clear all variables; close all figures.
Step 2:  Define Fs = 8 Hz, N = 8, n = 0:N-1.
Step 3:  Generate x1[n] = 2*sin(pi*n/4).
Step 4:  Generate x2[n] = cos(pi*n/4).
Step 5:  Check length(x1) == length(x2). If not, zero-pad.
Step 6:  y_add = x1 + x2;
Step 7:  y_sub = x1 - x2;
Step 8:  y_mul = x1 .* x2;  (element-wise, NOT matrix multiply)
Step 9:  subplot(4,1,1); stem(n, x1); title('x1[n]');
Step 10: subplot(4,1,2); stem(n, x2); title('x2[n]');
Step 11: subplot(4,1,3); stem(n, y_add); title('x1[n] + x2[n]');
Step 12: subplot(4,1,4); stem(n, y_mul); title('x1[n] * x2[n]');
Step 13: xlabel('n --->');
```

**Subplot Layout Description (2 marks):** A $4 \times 1$ vertical grid where subplot 1 shows the input sine wave $x_1[n]$, subplot 2 shows the input cosine $x_2[n]$, subplot 3 shows the summed signal $y_1[n]$, and subplot 4 shows the multiplied signal $y_2[n]$. All subplots share the x-axis label $n$ and have a common super-title *"Signal Addition and Multiplication — DSP Lab Experiment 2"*.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Two discrete-time signals are given by $x_1[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 0, 0\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{0, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. Perform the following operations and explain each result: **(7 marks)**
  1. $y_{add}[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$
  2. $y_{sub}[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$
  3. $y_{mul}[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$

**(b)** What is the effect of multiplying a sinusoidal signal by a square pulse train? Derive the mathematical expression and explain the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Sketch for Question B:**

**(a)** Index-aligned operations on the two vectors yield:
- $y_{add}[n] = \{1, 2, 4, 2, 3\}$ — superposition of two non-overlapping pulses with overlap at $n=2$.
- $y_{sub}[n] = \{1, 2, 2, -2, -3\}$ — subtraction creates a bipolar signal with negative values.
- $y_{mul}[n] = \{0, 0, 3, 0, 0\}$ — multiplication isolates the single overlapping sample at $n=2$, demonstrating a **matched filter / correlator** behaviour.

**(b)** If $x_1(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_c t)$ and $x_2(t) = p(t)$ where $p(t)$ is a unipolar square pulse train of frequency $f_p$, then

$$y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_c t) \cdot p(t) = A \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\left(\frac{t - kT_p}{\tau}\right) \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

This produces a **Pulse Amplitude Modulated (PAM)** signal. The output is non-zero only when the pulse is high, effectively **gating** the sinusoid. Engineering applications include **radar pulse transmission, optical communication, and time-division multiplexing (TDM)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dot in MATLAB:** Writing `x1 * x2` performs *matrix multiplication*, not element-wise. Students lose **2 full marks** for using `*` instead of `.*`.
> 2. **Ignoring length mismatch:** If $x_1$ has 8 samples and $x_2$ has 6 samples, the program will throw an error. Always pad with `x = [x, zeros(1, N_extra)]`.
> 3. **Not labelling axes:** Plots without `xlabel('n')` and `ylabel('Amplitude')` are penalized **1 mark** per figure.
> 4. **Forgetting the title:** Each subplot must have a descriptive title; this is a **mandatory** KTU requirement.
> 5. **Skipping hand verification:** Examiners explicitly ask "verify sample $n=2$ manually." If you can't, you lose **1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Operations:** Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication are *point-wise* operations on discrete-time signals.
- **Notation:** $y_{add}[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$, $y_{sub}[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n]$, $y_{mul}[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$.
- **Index Alignment:** Both signals **must** be defined on the same index $n$; otherwise zero-padding is required.
- **Output Length:** $N_y = \max(N_1, N_2)$.
- **MATLAB Operators:** `+`, `-`, `.*` (the **dot** is critical for element-wise multiplication).
- **Python NumPy:** Standard `+`, `-`, `*` operators are overloaded to be element-wise for `ndarray`.
- **Sampling Theorem:** Always ensure $f < F_s/2$ (Nyquist) to avoid aliasing.
- **Plot Style:** Use `stem()` for discrete-time signals; use `subplot(4,1,k)` for a $4 \times 1$ layout.
- **Hand Verification:** Always compute 2–3 samples manually and compare with the program output.
- **Superposition Principle:** Addition obeys linearity: $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$.
- **Modulation Identity:** Product of two sinusoids generates sum and difference frequencies (cosine product-to-sum rule).
- **Saturation Risk:** Multiplication can produce amplitudes larger than 1, causing overflow in fixed-point hardware.
- **Real-world Use:** Audio mixing, AM modulation, noise cancellation, image blending, beamforming, ECG processing.
- **Examiner's Pet Question:** "What happens if the two signals have *different lengths*?" — Answer: zero-pad the shorter one.
- **Key Cosine Identity to Memorize:**
$$\sin(A)\sin(B) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)\right]$$
- **Submission Checklist:** Algorithm + Code + Output plot + Hand calculation + Inference + Viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Calibration of Humidity Sensor with Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Calibration of Humidity Sensor with Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Humidity Sensor (Hygrometer)**: A transducer that converts the physical quantity of moisture content in a gaseous medium (typically ambient air) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, capacitance, or resistance).

In the context of microcontroller-based systems, **Relative Humidity (RH)** is the parameter of interest, defined as the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor ($P_w$) to the saturation vapor pressure of water at a given temperature ($P_{ws}$), expressed as a percentage:

$$RH (\%) = \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T)} \times 100$$

The **Implementation** phase refers to the hardware interfacing (signal conditioning, ADC, I2C/GPIO) and firmware design (driver, sampling, conditioning) required to acquire the sensor output. The **Calibration** phase refers to the mathematical process of mapping raw electrical outputs to accurate physical RH values using known reference standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Bathroom Towel & Damp Meter**
> Imagine you have two identical cotton towels. You sprinkle one with a teaspoon of water (damp) and the other with a full cup (soaking wet). The air around the soaking wet towel feels much more humid. Now, instead of using water, think of *air* itself. Warm air can "hold" more water vapor than cold air (like a towel's absorbency depends on material). **Relative Humidity (RH) is the ratio of "water currently in the air" to "maximum water the air could possibly hold at this temperature."** A humidity sensor is essentially an *artificial towel* that absorbs moisture and changes its electrical properties (capacitance/resistance) based on how much water vapor it has absorbed.

> A humidity sensor is like a tiny artificial towel that absorbs moisture from the air and changes its electrical properties based on how much water vapor it has absorbed. By measuring this change (capacitance, resistance, or voltage), we can deduce the moisture content of the surrounding air.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

- **Relative Humidity (RH)**: Expressed in **%RH**, range typically **0–100%**.
- **Dew Point (Td)**: Temperature at which air becomes saturated; measured in **°C** or **°K**.
- **Absolute Humidity (AH)**: Mass of water per unit volume, in **g/m³**.
- **Saturation Vapor Pressure ($P_{ws}$)**: Reference pressure at saturation, units **Pa** or **mbar**.
- **Sensor Sensitivity (S)**: Output change per %RH, units **pF/%RH** (capacitive) or **Ω/%RH** (resistive).
- **Accuracy Class**: Typically **±2%RH to ±5%RH** for commercial sensors.
- **Response Time (τ)**: Time to reach 63% of final value, units **seconds**.
- **Hysteresis (H)**: Difference in output at a given RH during adsorption vs. desorption.
- **Operating Temperature**: Standard range **−40°C to +85°C**.
- **Reference Salt Solutions (ASTM E104)**: 
  - **LiCl → 11.30 %RH** at 25°C
  - **MgCl₂ → 32.80 %RH** at 25°C
  - **NaCl → 75.30 %RH** at 25°C
  - **K₂SO₄ → 97.30 %RH** at 25°C

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Industrial Process Control**: HVAC systems, pharmaceutical manufacturing, semiconductor cleanrooms.
- **Agricultural IoT**: Greenhouse monitoring, precision farming.
- **Medical Devices**: Ventilators, incubators, sterilizers.
- **Automotive**: Cabin climate control, anti-fog windshield systems.
- **Data Center Cooling**: Server rack environmental monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles of Common Humidity Sensors

#### A. Capacitive Humidity Sensors (Most Widely Used)
- Uses a **hygroscopic polymer dielectric** (e.g., polyimide) sandwiched between two electrodes.
- Polymer absorbs water molecules → dielectric constant changes → **capacitance (C) changes**.
- Capacitance increases with rising RH (water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ vs. polymer $\varepsilon_r \approx 3$).
- Output is approximately linear in the **10–90%RH** range.

#### B. Resistive Humidity Sensors
- Uses a **conductive salt or polymer** on a ceramic substrate.
- Resistance **decreases** as RH increases (more water → more ion mobility).
- Strongly non-linear, requires **logarithmic or polynomial linearization**.

#### C. Thermal Conductivity Humidity Sensors
- Measures the difference in thermal conductivity between dry air and humid air.
- Used for **high-temperature** applications.

#### D. Gravimetric / Optical Sensors
- **Gold-standard reference** instruments (e.g., chilled mirror hygrometers).
- Used for laboratory calibration only; not in MCU-based systems.

### 2.2 Signal Conditioning Chain

The complete signal path from sensor to digital value follows this logical sequence:

1. **Sensor Element** produces a raw analog change ($\Delta C$ or $\Delta R$).
2. **Oscillator / Bridge Circuit** converts $\Delta C$ or $\Delta R$ into an **AC voltage or frequency** variation.
3. **Rectifier + Filter** converts AC to a **DC voltage** ($V_{out}$).
4. **Buffer Amplifier (Op-Amp)** scales $V_{out}$ to the ADC input range (typically **0–3.3V** for modern MCUs).
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** quantizes $V_{out}$ into a digital count $N$.
6. **MCU Firmware** maps the count $N$ to a calibrated RH value using lookup tables or polynomial equations.
7. **Display / Communication** sends the value via UART, I2C, SPI, or LCD.

### 2.3 Calibration Theory — The Core "Why"

A raw sensor output is rarely accurate because of:
- Manufacturing tolerance (sensor-to-sensor variation).
- Long-term drift (aging).
- Temperature cross-sensitivity.
- Non-linearity at extremes (0% and 100% RH).

**Calibration** is the mathematical correction of these errors using measurements at known reference conditions. The two most common methods are:

#### Method 1: Single-Point Calibration
- Use only one reference RH (e.g., 75.3% NaCl).
- Apply a simple **offset correction**:
  $$RH_{corrected} = RH_{raw} + \Delta$$
  where $\Delta$ is the error at the reference point.
- Limitation: Accurate only near the reference point; fails at extremes.

#### Method 2: Two-Point Calibration (Industry Standard)
- Measure sensor at **low reference** ($RH_L$, e.g., 11.3% LiCl) → get $V_L$.
- Measure sensor at **high reference** ($RH_H$, e.g., 75.3% NaCl) → get $V_H$.
- Compute **slope (m)** and **offset (b)**:
  $$m = \frac{RH_H - RH_L}{V_H - V_L}, \quad b = RH_L - m \cdot V_L$$
- Apply to any reading $V_x$:
  $$RH_{corrected} = m \cdot V_x + b$$

#### Method 3: Multi-Point Polynomial Calibration
- Use 3 or more reference points.
- Fit a polynomial of degree $n$:
  $$RH_{corrected} = a_0 + a_1 \cdot V + a_2 \cdot V^2 + \ldots + a_n \cdot V^n$$
- Solve for $a_i$ using **least-squares regression** (e.g., matrix pseudo-inverse).

### 2.4 Temperature Compensation Theory

Most polymer humidity sensors exhibit a temperature coefficient ($\alpha_T$) of about **−0.4 to −0.8 %RH/°C**. The compensation equation is:

$$RH_{compensated} = RH_{raw} + \alpha_T \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$

where $T_{ref}$ is the reference calibration temperature (typically 25°C) and $T$ is the actual measured temperature from a co-located temperature sensor.

### 2.5 ADC Quantization Effects

For an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$, the voltage resolution is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For a sensor with full-scale voltage swing $V_{FS}$ corresponding to **0–100%RH**, the theoretical RH resolution is:

$$\Delta RH = \frac{100}{2^n} \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{V_{FS}}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** |
|:----------:|:-------------|:------------------------|:--------:|
| $RH$ | Relative Humidity | $\frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T)} \times 100$ | % |
| $C_x$ | Sensor Capacitance | $C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F |
| $V_{out}$ | Sensor Output Voltage | $V_{out} = \frac{RH}{100} \cdot V_{FS}$ | V |
| $N$ | ADC Digital Count | $N = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor$ | LSB |
| $\Delta V$ | ADC Resolution | $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | V/LSB |
| $m$ | Calibration Slope | $\frac{RH_H - RH_L}{V_H - V_L}$ | %/V |
| $b$ | Calibration Offset | $RH_L - m \cdot V_L$ | % |
| $RH_{cal}$ | Calibrated RH | $m \cdot V_x + b$ | % |
| $RH_T$ | Temperature-Compensated RH | $RH_{raw} + \alpha_T (T - T_{ref})$ | % |
| $\alpha_T$ | Temperature Coefficient | $\frac{\partial RH}{\partial T} \bigg\vert_{RH}$ | %/°C |
| $RH_{poly}$ | Polynomial Calibration | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i V^i$ | % |
| $\tau$ | Response Time (63%) | $RC$ time constant of sensor | s |
| $H$ | Hysteresis | $H = \frac{\max(\Delta_{ads} - \Delta_{des})}{FS} \cdot 100$ | % |
| $P_{ws}$ | Saturation Vapor Pressure | $6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 T}{T + 243.5}}$ (Magnus form) | hPa |
| $T_d$ | Dew Point | $\frac{243.5 \cdot \ln(\frac{RH}{100} \cdot \frac{6.112}{P})}{17.67 - \ln(\frac{RH}{100} \cdot \frac{6.112}{P})}$ | °C |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The two-point calibration method is the **backbone of every commercial humidity module** because:
- It is fast (<30 minutes to perform).
- It uses inexpensive, stable, and traceable standards (saturated salts).
- It corrects both **gain** and **offset** errors simultaneously.
- It is fully automatable in a factory production line.

In contrast, single-point calibration is used only in **low-cost consumer products** (e.g., weather stations) where ±5%RH accuracy is acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Two-Point Linear Calibration Equation

**Problem Statement**: A capacitive humidity sensor outputs a voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to RH. At a low reference of 11.30%RH (using LiCl salt), the output is measured to be $V_L = 0.45$ V. At a high reference of 75.30%RH (using NaCl salt), the output is $V_H = 3.10$ V. The MCU ADC is 10-bit with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Derive the calibration equation and compute the RH for an arbitrary ADC reading of $N = 350$ LSB.

#### Step 1 — Convert Voltages to ADC Counts (Optional Intermediate)
For a 10-bit ADC ($n = 10$, so $2^{10} = 1024$) with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V:

$$N_L = \left\lfloor \frac{V_L}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0.45}{5.0} \cdot 1024 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 92.16 \right\rfloor = 92 \text{ LSB}$$

$$N_H = \left\lfloor \frac{V_H}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.10}{5.0} \cdot 1024 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 634.88 \right\rfloor = 635 \text{ LSB}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Calibration Slope (m)
Using the two-point linear model:

$$m = \frac{RH_H - RH_L}{N_H - N_L} = \frac{75.30 - 11.30}{635 - 92} = \frac{64.00}{543} = 0.11786 \; \frac{\%RH}{LSB}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Calibration Offset (b)

$$b = RH_L - m \cdot N_L = 11.30 - (0.11786) \cdot (92) = 11.30 - 10.8431 = 0.4569 \; \%RH$$

#### Step 4 — Final Calibration Equation

$$RH_{cal}(N) = m \cdot N + b = 0.11786 \cdot N + 0.4569 \; [\%RH]$$

#### Step 5 — Apply to the Reading $N = 350$ LSB

$$RH_{cal}(350) = 0.11786 \cdot 350 + 0.4569 = 41.2510 + 0.4569 = 41.71 \; \%RH$$

> **Result**: An ADC count of 350 corresponds to **41.71 %RH**.

#### Step 6 — Estimate Quantization Error
The minimum RH step size (resolution) is:

$$\Delta RH = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot m = \frac{5.0}{1024} \cdot 0.11786 = 5.754 \times 10^{-4} \; \%RH / LSB$$

Therefore, the **worst-case quantization error** is $\pm 0.5 \times \Delta RH = \pm 0.029 \; \%RH$, which is far below sensor error and is negligible.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Compensation Equation

**Problem Statement**: The above sensor has a temperature coefficient $\alpha_T = -0.5 \; \%RH / °C$. The on-board temperature sensor reads $T = 35.0 \; °C$, while the calibration was performed at $T_{ref} = 25.0 \; °C$. Compute the temperature-compensated RH for the raw reading of 41.71 %RH.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Temperature Deviation
$$\Delta T = T - T_{ref} = 35.0 - 25.0 = 10.0 \; °C$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Compensation Equation
$$RH_{compensated} = RH_{raw} + \alpha_T \cdot \Delta T$$

$$RH_{compensated} = 41.71 + (-0.5) \cdot (10.0) = 41.71 - 5.00 = 36.71 \; \%RH$$

> **Result**: The true RH after temperature compensation is **36.71 %RH**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: The sensor *under-reads* at higher temperatures because the polymer's ability to absorb water decreases as temperature rises. The negative coefficient corrects for this physical behavior.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Resolution & Sizing the ADC

**Problem Statement**: A capacitive sensor produces a voltage swing of $V_{FS} = 2.5$ V across 0–100%RH. The MCU uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V. Determine:
(a) The voltage resolution of the ADC.
(b) The effective RH resolution.

#### Part (a) — Voltage Resolution
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} = 8.057 \times 10^{-4} \; V = 805.7 \; \muV$$

#### Part (b) — RH Resolution
The RH-per-volt slope from the sensor's transfer function is:
$$S_{RH} = \frac{100 \; \%RH}{V_{FS}} = \frac{100}{2.5} = 40 \; \%RH / V$$

The RH resolution is the product of voltage resolution and sensitivity:
$$\Delta RH = \Delta V \cdot S_{RH} = 805.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 40 = 0.0322 \; \%RH$$

> **Result**: The system can theoretically resolve changes as small as **0.032 %RH** (32 thousandths of a percent). In practice, sensor noise limits this to about **0.1–0.3 %RH**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources & Corrections

| **Error Type** | **Physical Origin** | **Magnitude (Typical)** | **Correction Method** |
|:---------------|:--------------------|:------------------------|:----------------------|
| **Offset Error** | Sensor zero-point drift | ±2 %RH | Two-point calibration |
| **Gain Error** | Aging, contamination | ±3 %RH/year | Recalibration every 12 months |
| **Linearity Error** | Polymer non-linearity at extremes | ±1 %RH (0–20, 80–100) | Polynomial calibration |
| **Hysteresis** | Adsorption vs. desorption | ±1.5 %RH | Average of up/down sweeps |
| **Temp. Cross-Sensitivity** | Polymer temperature dependence | −0.5 %RH/°C | On-board temp. compensation |
| **Self-Heating** | Power dissipation in sensor | +0.1 to +0.5 %RH | Low duty-cycle sampling |
| **Condensation** | Sensor saturation at >95%RH | Catastrophic failure | Heated, hydrophobic membrane |
| **Chemical Contamination** | Vapors, dust | ±5 %RH | Protective membrane + filter |
| **Quantization Error** | ADC finite resolution | $\frac{1}{2^{n+1}}$ of FS | Higher bit ADC, oversampling |
| **I²C Noise** | Bus interference | ±1 LSB | Pull-up resistors, shielding |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Polynomial Calibration Using Least-Squares

**Setup**: Reference measurements at 4 humidity points (using saturated salt solutions):
- 11.30%RH → V = 0.45 V
- 32.80%RH → V = 1.20 V
- 75.30%RH → V = 3.10 V
- 97.30%RH → V = 4.65 V

A 2nd-degree polynomial is fitted: $RH = a_0 + a_1 V + a_2 V^2$.

Constructing the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and target vector $\mathbf{y}$:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.45 & 0.2025 \\ 1 & 1.20 & 1.4400 \\ 1 & 3.10 & 9.6100 \\ 1 & 4.65 & 21.6225 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 11.30 \\ 32.80 \\ 75.30 \\ 97.30 \end{bmatrix}$$

The least-squares solution is $\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$, yielding approximately:

$$a_0 \approx -2.45, \quad a_1 \approx 19.20, \quad a_2 \approx 0.85$$

So the calibrated equation is:

$$RH_{cal}(V) = -2.45 + 19.20 \cdot V + 0.85 \cdot V^2$$

For $V = 2.00$ V: $RH = -2.45 + 38.40 + 3.40 = 39.35 \; \%RH$.

> This polynomial form fits a non-linear response and is more accurate than the linear two-point method, especially near the extremes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hygroscopic Polymer Sensor Element] --> B[Oscillator / Bridge Circuit]
    B --> C[Rectifier and RC Filter]
    C --> D[Buffer Op-Amp]
    D --> E[10-bit or 12-bit ADC]
    E --> F[Microcontroller Firmware]
    F --> G[Calibration Engine]
    G --> H[Temperature Compensation Block]
    H --> I[Display LCD or OLED]
    F --> J[UART or I2C or SPI Output]
    F --> K[Alarm Threshold Comparator]

    subgraph refBlock [Reference Standards]
        R1[LiCl 11.3 percent RH]
        R2[MgCl2 32.8 percent RH]
        R3[NaCl 75.3 percent RH]
        R4[K2SO4 97.3 percent RH]
    end

    refBlock --> G
    F --> L[On-board Temperature Sensor]
    L --> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Firmware Algorithm for Calibration & Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Power On]) --> init[Initialize GPIO, ADC, I2C, UART, Timer]
    init --> loadEEPROM[Load Calibration Constants m, b, alphaT from EEPROM]
    loadEEPROM --> loopStart{Measurement Cycle}
    loopStart -->|Trigger| trigger[Trigger Sensor Conversion]
    trigger --> waitDelay[Wait for Conversion Time e.g. 30 ms]
    waitDelay --> readADC[Read ADC Count N]
    readADC --> readTemp[Read Temperature T from on-board sensor]
    readTemp --> calRH[Apply Two-Point Calibration: RH_cal = m times N plus b]
    calRH --> compTemp[Apply Temperature Compensation: RH_final = RH_cal plus alphaT times T minus Tref]
    compTemp --> checkRange{Is RH within 0 to 100 percent?}
    checkRange -->|No| clamp[Clamp to physical limits]
    checkRange -->|Yes| display
    clamp --> display[Display on LCD and Transmit via UART]
    display --> loopStart
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Calibration Mode FSM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CalLow: Operator places sensor in LiCl chamber
    CalLow --> ReadingLow: Wait 60 minutes for equilibrium
    ReadingLow --> StoreLow: Average 100 samples and store N_L
    StoreLow --> CalHigh: Operator places sensor in NaCl chamber
    CalHigh --> ReadingHigh: Wait 60 minutes for equilibrium
    ReadingHigh --> StoreHigh: Average 100 samples and store N_H
    StoreHigh --> Compute: Compute slope m and offset b
    Compute --> SaveEEPROM: Write m, b to non-volatile memory
    SaveEEPROM --> Idle: Calibration complete
    Idle --> Measure: Normal operation
    Measure --> Idle: Cycle delay
```

### 4.4 Hardware Connection Schematic (Descriptive)

For a typical **DHT22 / AM2302** sensor interfaced to an **STM32 / Arduino** class MCU:

- **VCC** (Pin 1) → **+3.3V** or **+5V** rail
- **DATA** (Pin 2) → MCU GPIO pin (e.g., PA0) with a **10 kΩ pull-up** to VCC
- **NC** (Pin 3) → Not connected
- **GND** (Pin 4) → Ground

For an **SHT31** (I²C interface):

- **VDD** → **+3.3V**
- **SDA** → MCU I²C SDA line with **4.7 kΩ pull-up** to VCC
- **SCL** → MCU I²C SCL line with **4.7 kΩ pull-up** to VCC
- **ADDR** → GND (address **0x44**) or VDD (address **0x45**)
- **GND** → Ground

> [!NOTE]
> The pull-up resistors are **critical**. Without them, the open-drain data line will float and produce erratic readings.

### 4.5 Signal Conditioning Block Diagram (Analog Front-End)

```
[Hygroscopic Capacitor Cx] 
        |
        v
[Astable Multivibrator using 555 Timer] 
        |
        v   (Frequency f varies with Cx)
[F-to-V Converter using LM331]
        |
        v   (Analog voltage proportional to RH)
[2nd-Order Low-Pass Filter fc = 10 Hz]
        |
        v   (Smoothed DC)
[Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain = 1.32]
        |
        v   (Scaled to 0 to 3.3V range)
[12-bit ADC Channel 0 of MCU]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define **Relative Humidity** and distinguish it from **Absolute Humidity** and **Dew Point**. Provide the SI/commercial unit of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Relative Humidity (RH)**: Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor in air to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage. $RH = \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T)} \times 100$. Unit: **%RH**. (1 mark)
- **Absolute Humidity (AH)**: Mass of water vapor per unit volume of air. Unit: **g/m³**. (1 mark)
- **Dew Point (Td)**: Temperature at which air becomes saturated and condensation begins. Unit: **°C**. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> 
> **Q2.** What is the principle of operation of a **capacitive humidity sensor**? List **two advantages** and **one disadvantage**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Principle**: A hygroscopic polymer dielectric absorbs/desorbs water molecules based on ambient humidity. This changes its dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r$), which in turn changes the capacitance of a parallel-plate structure: $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. (1.5 marks)
- **Advantages**: 
  - (i) Linear output over wide range. (0.5 mark)
  - (ii) Low power consumption and long-term stability. (0.5 mark)
- **Disadvantage**: 
  - Sensitive to chemical vapors and contamination, requires protective membrane. (0.5 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Internal Choice Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **procedure of two-point calibration** of a capacitive humidity sensor using **saturated salt solutions**. Include the ASTM E104 reference values, equilibrium time, and the derivation of the calibration equation. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Selection of Reference Standards (1 mark)**
Saturated aqueous salt solutions provide stable, traceable humidity references in a sealed chamber at constant temperature (typically 25°C). The ASTM E104 standard values are:
- **Lithium Chloride (LiCl) → 11.30 %RH** at 25°C
- **Sodium Chloride (NaCl) → 75.30 %RH** at 25°C
- (Other standards: MgCl₂ at 32.8%, K₂SO₄ at 97.3%)

**Step 2 — Chamber Setup & Equilibrium (1 mark)**
- Place the sensor inside a sealed chamber containing the LiCl solution at the bottom (separated by a porous divider so the sensor does not touch the liquid).
- Maintain temperature at **25.0 ± 0.1°C** using a temperature-controlled water bath.
- Allow **at least 60 minutes** for the sensor to reach adsorption equilibrium with the chamber air.

**Step 3 — Low-Point Reading (1 mark)**
- Record the average of **100 successive ADC samples** to reduce noise. Denote this as $N_L$ (or voltage $V_L$).
- Example: $N_L = 92$ LSB.

**Step 4 — Repeat for High-Point (1 mark)**
- Transfer the sensor to a chamber with NaCl solution, wait 60 minutes, record $N_H$.
- Example: $N_H = 635$ LSB.

**Step 5 — Derive Calibration Equation (2 marks)**
- Compute slope: 
$$m = \frac{RH_H - RH_L}{N_H - N_L} = \frac{75.30 - 11.30}{635 - 92} = 0.1179 \; \%/LSB$$
- Compute offset: 
$$b = RH_L - m \cdot N_L = 11.30 - 0.1179 \cdot 92 = 0.46 \; \%$$
- Final equation: 
$$RH_{cal}(N) = 0.1179 \cdot N + 0.46 \; [\%RH]$$

**Step 6 — Validation (1 mark)**
- Verify by placing the sensor in a MgCl₂ chamber (32.8%RH) and confirming that the equation produces a reading close to 32.8%RH.

> **(b)** Design a **microcontroller-based humidity measurement system** using the **SHT31 sensor** (I²C interface, address 0x44). Draw the complete **block diagram**, describe the **data acquisition sequence**, and write the **firmware pseudocode** for reading RH, applying calibration, and displaying on an LCD. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hardware Block Diagram (2 marks)**
```
[SHT31 Sensor] --I2C SDA/SCL-- [MCU STM32 / Arduino] --LCD/4-bit-- [16x2 LCD Display]
                       |
                  [4.7k pull-ups to VCC]
                       |
                  [UART to PC for logging]
```
- MCU master reads from SHT31 slave address 0x44.
- Two I²C lines (SDA, SCL) with 4.7 kΩ pull-ups.

**Step 2 — Data Acquisition Sequence (2 marks)**
1. **Send Measurement Command**: Master transmits `0x2C 0x06` (Clock Stretching Disabled, High Repeatability) to SHT31.
2. **Wait for Conversion**: Master waits ~15 ms (max conversion time for high repeatability).
3. **Read 6 Bytes**: Master reads 2 bytes of temperature + 1 CRC byte + 2 bytes of humidity + 1 CRC byte.
4. **Verify CRC**: Compute CRC-8 over the 2 data bytes and compare with received CRC; discard if mismatch.
5. **Convert Raw Values**: 
   - $T_{raw}$ and $RH_{raw}$ are 16-bit unsigned integers.
   - $T(°C) = -45 + 175 \cdot \frac{T_{raw}}{65535}$
   - $RH(\%) = 100 \cdot \frac{RH_{raw}}{65535}$

**Step 3 — Firmware Pseudocode (2 marks)**
```
Initialize I2C peripheral at 100 kHz
Initialize LCD in 4-bit mode
Load calibration constants m, b, alphaT from EEPROM

LOOP forever:
    Send START + (SHT31 address 0x44) + WRITE
    Transmit command 0x2C 0x06
    Send STOP
    delay 20 ms
    Send START + (SHT31 address 0x44) + READ
    Read 6 bytes into buffer
    Send STOP
    IF CRC_check(buffer) == OK:
        T_raw = (buffer[0] << 8) | buffer[1]
        RH_raw = (buffer[3] << 8) | buffer[4]
        T_measured = -45 + 175 * T_raw / 65535
        RH_corrected = m * RH_raw + b
        RH_final = RH_corrected + alpha_T * (T_measured - 25)
        Display "T = 28.4 C" on line 1
        Display "RH = 65.2 %" on line 2
    ELSE
        Display "SENSOR ERROR"
    delay 1000 ms
END LOOP
```

**Step 4 — Application Note (1 mark)**
For high-accuracy applications, store the calibration coefficients in **non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash)** of the MCU. They are computed once during factory calibration and never recomputed in the field.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Internal Choice Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** A capacitive humidity sensor is interfaced to a **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. After two-point calibration, the slope was found to be $m = 0.12 \; \%/LSB$ and the offset $b = 0.50 \; \%$. The temperature sensor on the same PCB reports $T = 30°C$, while calibration was done at $T_{ref} = 25°C$. The sensor's temperature coefficient is $\alpha_T = -0.45 \; \%/°C$. For an ADC reading of $N = 410$ LSB, compute **(i)** the calibrated RH, **(ii)** the temperature-compensated RH, and **(iii)** the theoretical voltage resolution and RH resolution of the system. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Calibrated RH (2 marks)**
Using $RH_{cal} = m \cdot N + b$:

$$RH_{cal} = 0.12 \cdot 410 + 0.50 = 49.20 + 0.50 = 49.70 \; \%RH$$

**Part (ii) — Temperature-Compensated RH (2 marks)**
Apply $RH_{final} = RH_{cal} + \alpha_T \cdot (T - T_{ref})$:

$$RH_{final} = 49.70 + (-0.45) \cdot (30 - 25) = 49.70 - 2.25 = 47.45 \; \%RH$$

**Part (iii) — Voltage & RH Resolution (3 marks)**

Voltage resolution:
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.883 \times 10^{-3} \; V = 4.88 \; mV$$

The effective RH resolution per LSB is the slope $m$ itself: **0.12 %RH/LSB**.

The voltage resolution translates to an RH resolution as:
$$\Delta RH_{min} = m \cdot \Delta V = 0.12 \cdot 4.883 \times 10^{-3} = 5.86 \times 10^{-4} \; \%RH / LSB$$

> **(b)** Explain in detail the **sources of error** in a humidity measurement system. For each, describe the **physical origin** and the **mitigation technique**. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| **Error Source** | **Physical Origin** | **Mitigation** |
|:-----------------|:--------------------|:---------------|
| **1. Sensor Aging / Drift** (1.5 marks) | Polymer dielectric degrades over time; electrodes oxidize | Recalibrate every 6–12 months; use reference salt solutions |
| **2. Temperature Cross-Sensitivity** (1.5 marks) | Polymer $\varepsilon_r$ depends on temperature | Co-locate digital temperature sensor; apply $\alpha_T$ compensation |
| **3. Hysteresis** (1 mark) | Polymer retains water from prior exposure | Average of adsorption/desorption readings; use fresh sensor |
| **4. Chemical Contamination** (1 mark) | Solvent vapors, dust, smoke | Use hydrophobic PTFE membrane + sintered metal filter |
| **5. Self-Heating** (1 mark) | Current through sensor warms it above ambient | Low duty-cycle sampling (1 read per 2 s); minimize excitation voltage |
| **6. Quantization Noise** (1 mark) | ADC finite bit-depth | Use 12-bit or 16-bit ADC; apply oversampling and averaging |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RH with AH in definitions** — Examiners will deduct 0.5 mark if you write "humidity is the amount of water in the air" without specifying **relative** vs **absolute**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the pull-up resistor** in DHT22/SHT31 circuit diagrams — A common 1-mark deduction in hardware design questions.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the temperature compensation step** in numerical problems — Many students compute $RH_{cal}$ but stop there. The compensated value $RH_{final}$ is often asked separately and carries 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up LiCl and NaCl reference values** — Remember: **LiCl = 11.3%** (low), **NaCl = 75.3%** (high). Reversing them gives a **negative slope** and an absurd RH value.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the units** in final numerical answers — Always write "%RH" or "°C" explicitly.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring ADC quantization** — When asked for "resolution," many students only compute voltage resolution. You must also show the **effective RH resolution per LSB** (which equals the slope $m$).
> 
> 7. **Using the wrong temperature coefficient sign** — If the sensor's $\alpha_T$ is negative, the compensation equation must be $RH_{cal} + \alpha_T(T - T_{ref})$, not $-$. A sign error here loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary**
> 
> - [x] **Relative Humidity (RH)** is the ratio of actual water vapor pressure to saturation vapor pressure at a given temperature, expressed in **%RH**.
> - [x] **Capacitive sensors** are the industry standard — they use a hygroscopic polymer that changes capacitance with RH.
> - [x] **Resistive sensors** are cheaper but highly non-linear; require logarithmic/polynomial correction.
> - [x] The **two-point calibration** uses **saturated salt solutions**: **LiCl (11.3%)** for the low point and **NaCl (75.3%)** for the high point at **25°C** (ASTM E104).
> - [x] Calibration formula: $RH_{cal} = m \cdot N + b$, where $m = \frac{RH_H - RH_L}{N_H - N_L}$ and $b = RH_L - m \cdot N_L$.
> - [x] **Temperature compensation**: $RH_{final} = RH_{cal} + \alpha_T \cdot (T - T_{ref})$ with typical $\alpha_T \approx -0.5 \; \%/°C$.
> - [x] **ADC Resolution**: $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ ; **RH Resolution per LSB** = calibration slope $m$.
> - [x] **DHT22**: Single-wire protocol, 40-bit data (16-bit RH + 16-bit T + 8-bit checksum), 10 kΩ pull-up.
> - [x] **SHT31**: I²C protocol, slave address 0x44 (ADDR=GND) or 0x45 (ADDR=VDD), 4.7 kΩ pull-ups, command `0x2C 0x06` for high-precision single-shot read.
> - [x] Raw SHT31 conversion: $T = -45 + 175 \cdot \frac{T_{raw}}{65535}$, $RH = 100 \cdot \frac{RH_{raw}}{65535}$.
> - [x] **Polynomial calibration** is used for non-linear correction: $RH = a_0 + a_1 V + a_2 V^2 + \ldots$, solved via **least-squares regression**.
> - [x] **Error sources** to remember: offset, gain, hysteresis, temperature cross-sensitivity, self-heating, contamination, and ADC quantization.
> - [x] **Equilibrium time** for salt-chamber calibration: at least **60 minutes** at constant temperature.
> - [x] **Storage of calibration constants**: write to **non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash)** so values persist across power cycles.
> - [x] **Saturation vapor pressure** (Magnus form): $P_{ws} = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 T}{T + 243.5}}$ hPa.
> - [x] **Dew point** can be computed from RH and temperature using the inverse Magnus equation.
> - [x] **Sampling rate**: typically 1 sample per 1–2 seconds to minimize self-heating.
> - [x] **Industrial accuracy classes**: ±2%RH (premium), ±3%RH (standard), ±5%RH (consumer grade).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Calibration of Pressure Sensor with Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Calibration of Pressure Sensor with Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pressure Sensor Implementation and Calibration** with a microcontroller refers to the systematic process of interfacing an analog/digital pressure transducer with an embedded processing unit (such as Arduino Uno, STM32, MSP430, or PIC), acquiring the raw sensor output through an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, applying correction algorithms (linearization, offset compensation, span adjustment), and validating the measurement against a known reference standard to determine measurement uncertainty.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCPL507 (DSP Lab)** curriculum, this experiment bridges foundational **signal acquisition** principles with **embedded systems** implementation, requiring students to:
- Configure the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** peripheral of the microcontroller
- Process the quantized signal using a DSP-oriented calibration algorithm
- Evaluate performance parameters such as **linearity error, hysteresis, repeatability, and accuracy**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2** — "Implement signal acquisition and conditioning techniques using microcontrollers for real-world transducer interfacing." It is assessed as a **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** lab record experiment worth 20 marks, with a viva component testing understanding of ADC quantization and sensor calibration mathematics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **bathroom weighing scale** (which is essentially a pressure/load sensor). When you stand on it:
- Your weight pushes down on a **strain gauge** (a special resistor that changes resistance under force)
- This tiny resistance change is converted into a small **voltage change** (often in millivolts)
- A **microcontroller** is like the "brain" inside the scale — it reads this voltage, does some math, and shows the result on the display
- **Calibration** is the process of placing known weights (e.g., 10 kg, 20 kg, 50 kg) and "teaching" the brain to map specific voltages to specific weights

For a **pressure sensor**, the same logic applies: a known pressure is applied → a voltage is read → the microcontroller is told "this voltage = this pressure" repeatedly across the full range until the mapping is accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Calibration is Essential:** No sensor is perfect out of the factory. Manufacturing tolerances, temperature drift, and aging cause errors. Calibration eliminates **systematic errors** and minimizes **random errors**, ensuring the measurement is **traceable to a national/international standard**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally accepted in metrology and form the basis of KTU lab viva questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | P | **Pascal (Pa)**, bar, psi, mmHg |
| Force per unit area | F/A | **N/m² = Pa** |
| ADC Resolution | n | **bits** (typically 10-bit, 12-bit) |
| Reference Voltage | V_ref | **Volts (V)** |
| Sensitivity | S | **mV/kPa** or **V/bar** |
| Linearity Error | ε_L | **% of Full-Scale Output (FSO)** |
| Hysteresis Error | ε_H | **% of FSO** |
| Repeatability | R | **% of FSO** |
| Combined Accuracy | A | **±% of reading** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** **1 atm = 101,325 Pa = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 psi**. For low-pressure sensors, **1 kPa ≈ 4.0146 inches of water column (inH₂O)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Pressure Measurement Chain

The complete implementation follows a structured **signal chain**:

### Step 1: Physical-to-Electrical Transduction
A pressure sensor (e.g., **MPX5700AP**, **BMP280**, **BMP180**, **HX710B-based load cell**) converts applied pressure into an analog voltage or a digital signal. The most common transduction principles are:
- **Piezoresistive Effect:** Strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone Bridge** configuration. The output voltage is differential:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$
- **Capacitive Sensing:** Pressure changes the gap between two capacitor plates, altering capacitance.
- **Piezoelectric Effect:** Generates a charge proportional to applied pressure (dynamic only).

### Step 2: Signal Conditioning
Raw sensor outputs are often:
- **Low amplitude** (mV range)
- **Noisy** (subject to thermal and EMI noise)
- **Non-linear** (sensor response curve is not perfectly linear)

Conditioning involves:
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128, AD620)** — amplifies the differential bridge output
- **Low-Pass Filter (RC or active)** — removes high-frequency noise
- **Level Shifting** — adjusts signal range to fit ADC input (0–V_ref)

### Step 3: Analog-to-Digital Conversion
The microcontroller's **SAR ADC** samples the conditioned voltage and quantizes it into a digital code:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$
where:
- $D$ = digital output code (integer)
- $V_{in}$ = analog input voltage
- $V_{ref}$ = ADC reference voltage (e.g., **5.0 V** for Arduino Uno)
- $n$ = ADC resolution (e.g., 10 for Arduino Uno ATmega328P)

### Step 4: Digital Calibration Algorithm
The raw digital code is converted to pressure using a **calibration model** (linear, polynomial, or piecewise linear):
$$P_{calibrated} = m \cdot D + c$$
where $m$ is the **slope (kPa/LSB)** and $c$ is the **offset (kPa)**.

### Step 5: Display / Transmission
The calibrated pressure is shown on an **LCD/Serial Monitor** or transmitted via **UART, I²C, or SPI**.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Transduction** is the heart of the sensor — without it, no measurement is possible.
- **Conditioning** is critical because a 10-bit ADC on a 0–5 V scale has a step size of ~4.88 mV. A raw sensor output of 10 mV would only span 2 codes — useless! Amplification to 0–5 V gives full ADC range.
- **Calibration** removes the **systematic error** introduced by sensor non-linearity, offset drift, and gain tolerance.
- **Calibration traceability** is required for any measurement to be considered valid in engineering practice.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta V = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital code | LSB (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Reconstructed voltage | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $P = m \cdot V_{in} + c$ | Linear calibration model | kPa |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{V_2 - V_1}$ | Calibration slope | kPa/V |
| 6 | $c = P_1 - m \cdot V_1$ | Calibration offset (intercept) | kPa |
| 7 | $LSB = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC Least Significant Bit | V |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_L = \dfrac{\vert P_{measured} - P_{ideal} \vert}{FSO} \times 100$ | Linearity error | % FSO |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_H = \dfrac{\vert P_{increasing} - P_{decreasing} \vert}{FSO} \times 100$ | Hysteresis error | % FSO |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{\max \vert P_i - \bar{P} \vert}{FSO} \times 100$ | Repeatability | % FSO |
| 11 | $A = \pm \sqrt{\varepsilon_L^2 + \varepsilon_H^2 + R^2}$ | Combined accuracy (RSS method) | % FSO |
| 12 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta P}$ | Sensor sensitivity | V/kPa |
| 13 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Gauge to absolute conversion | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Formula 8:** In a markdown table, never use bare `|x|` notation. Use **\vert x \vert** for absolute value. This is a common markdown table-breaking mistake.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS), MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensors
- **Medical:** Blood pressure monitors, ventilators, CPAP machines
- **Industrial:** Hydraulic systems, pneumatic control, level measurement in tanks
- **Aerospace:** Altimeters, cabin pressure monitoring
- **IoT/Weather:** Barometric pressure stations, altitude estimation (used in drones)
- **HVAC:** Differential pressure sensors for filter monitoring, duct airflow measurement

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Digital Code to Pressure Conversion

**Given:**
- Sensor: MPX5700AP (range 0–700 kPa, sensitivity ≈ 6.4 mV/kPa, V_ex = 5.0 V)
- Microcontroller: Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 10-bit ADC, V_ref = 5.0 V)
- Two calibration points obtained using a dead-weight tester:
  - **Point 1:** P₁ = 0 kPa, V₁ = 0.2 V
  - **Point 2:** P₂ = 100 kPa, V₂ = 4.0 V

**Step 1: Calculate the ADC resolution (1 LSB)**
$$LSB = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.8828 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2: Establish the linear calibration model**
We model the sensor as a linear system:
$$P = m \cdot V_{in} + c$$

**Step 3: Compute the slope m using the two-point method**
$$m = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{100 - 0}{4.0 - 0.2} = \frac{100}{3.8} = 26.3158 \text{ kPa/V}$$

**Step 4: Compute the offset c**
$$c = P_1 - m \cdot V_1 = 0 - (26.3158)(0.2) = -5.2632 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5: Verify with Point 2**
$$P = (26.3158)(4.0) + (-5.2632) = 105.2632 - 5.2632 = 100.0 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Full calibration equation**
$$P_{\text{calibrated}} = 26.3158 \cdot V_{in} - 5.2632 \quad \text{(in kPa)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Linearity Error Calculation (Full Calibration Curve)

Suppose 5 calibration points are measured across the sensor's range:

| Point | True P (kPa) | Measured V (V) | Computed P (kPa) | Error (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0 | 0.20 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 25.0 | 1.15 | 24.99 | -0.01 |
| 3 | 50.0 | 2.10 | 50.02 | +0.02 |
| 4 | 75.0 | 3.05 | 75.00 | 0.00 |
| 5 | 100.0 | 4.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 |

**Full-Scale Output (FSO):**
$$FSO = P_{max} - P_{min} = 100.0 - 0.0 = 100.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Linearity Error at each point:**
$$\varepsilon_{L,i} = \frac{\vert P_{measured,i} - P_{ideal,i} \vert}{FSO} \times 100\%$$

For Point 3:
$$\varepsilon_{L,3} = \frac{\vert 50.02 - 50.00 \vert}{100.0} \times 100\% = \frac{0.02}{100.0} \times 100\% = 0.02\%$$

**Worst-case linearity error (max of all):**
$$\varepsilon_{L,max} = \max(\varepsilon_{L,i}) = 0.02\% \text{ FSO}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: ADC Quantization Error

Theoretical worst-case quantization error of a 10-bit ADC is:
$$\varepsilon_{Q,max} = \pm \frac{LSB}{2} = \pm \frac{4.8828 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 2.4414 \text{ mV}$$

In terms of pressure, this corresponds to:
$$\Delta P_{Q} = m \cdot \frac{LSB}{2} = 26.3158 \times 0.0024414 = 0.0642 \text{ kPa}$$

So the **theoretical minimum pressure resolution** of this system is **±0.0642 kPa** (or ±64.2 Pa).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Derived Quantity | Formula | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| ADC LSB | $V_{ref}/2^n$ | **4.8828 mV** |
| Calibration slope m | $(P_2-P_1)/(V_2-V_1)$ | **26.3158 kPa/V** |
| Calibration offset c | $P_1 - mV_1$ | **-5.2632 kPa** |
| Worst-case linearity error | $\max \vert P_m - P_i \vert / FSO$ | **0.02% FSO** |
| Quantization error (voltage) | $LSB/2$ | **±2.4414 mV** |
| Quantization error (pressure) | $m \cdot LSB/2$ | **±0.0642 kPa** |
| Theoretical resolution | $2 \cdot \Delta P_Q$ | **0.1284 kPa** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete signal flow** from physical pressure input to calibrated digital output:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Pressure Input] --> B[Pressure Sensor MPX5700AP]
    B --> C[Differential Bridge Output mV range]
    C --> D[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    D --> E[Low Pass Filter RC cutoff 100 Hz]
    E --> F[ADC Input Pin A0 Arduino Uno]
    F --> G[10 bit SAR ADC ATmega328P]
    G --> H[Raw Digital Code D 0 to 1023]
    H --> I[Calibration Algorithm m D plus c]
    I --> J[Calibrated Pressure Value kPa]
    J --> K[Display Unit LCD 16x2 or Serial Monitor]
    J --> L[Optional UART Transmission to PC]

    subgraph Signal_Conditioning_Block
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph Digital_Processing_Block
        G
        H
        I
    end
```

## 4.2 Calibration Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Calibration] --> B[Apply Zero Pressure P = 0 kPa]
    B --> C[Read ADC Code D0 Average 100 samples]
    C --> D[Apply Known Pressure P1 e.g. 50 kPa]
    D --> E[Read ADC Code D1 Average 100 samples]
    E --> F[Apply Known Pressure P2 e.g. 100 kPa]
    F --> G[Read ADC Code D2 Average 100 samples]
    G --> H[Compute Slope m and Offset c]
    H --> I{Verify with 3rd known point}
    I -->|Error less than 0.5 percent FSO| J[Store m and c in EEPROM]
    I -->|Error greater than 0.5 percent FSO| K[Repeat with 5 point calibration]
    J --> L[End Calibration Successful]
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Hardware Interconnection Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor_Module
        S1[MPX5700AP Pin 1 Vcc 5V]
        S2[MPX5700AP Pin 2 Vout to A0]
        S3[MPX5700AP Pin 3 GND]
    end

    subgraph Microcontroller_Arduino_Uno
        M1[ATmega328P ADC Channel 0]
```
