# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set A]**
Explain the role of an optical encoder in an RPM measurement system. How is the encoder resolution related to the accuracy of RPM computation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An optical encoder consists of a slotted disc, an LED light source, and a photodiode detector. As the motor shaft rotates, the slots alternately block and unblock the light, producing a train of electrical pulses whose frequency is proportional to the rotational speed. The **encoder resolution**, measured in **Pulses Per Revolution (PPR)**, directly determines the smallest angular increment that can be detected: a 500 PPR encoder gives 0.72° resolution, while a 2000 PPR quadrature-decoded encoder gives 0.18° resolution. Higher PPR yields a more accurate RPM estimate (smaller quantization error) and smoother control, but increases the maximum count rate, requiring a higher timer input frequency.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
Define the term "anti-windup" in a digital PI controller. Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Anti-windup** is a protection mechanism that prevents the integrator term of a PI controller from accumulating indefinitely when the control output saturates (i.e., when the computed duty cycle exceeds 100% or falls below 0%). Without anti-windup, the integral sum grows during saturation, causing **large overshoot and slow recovery** once the setpoint becomes reachable. It is implemented by **clamping the integrator sum** within the physical actuator limits, by **back-calculation** (feeding back the difference between saturated and unsaturated output), or by **conditional integration** (freezing the integrator when saturated).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the discrete-time difference equation of a PI controller from the continuous-time law $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau$ using the **backward rectangular rule** for integration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC motor is interfaced to a Tiva C microcontroller. An optical encoder with 500 PPR in ×4 quadrature mode (effective 2000 counts/rev) is read over a gate time of 100 ms. The counter reads 800 counts. Calculate the measured RPM, and design the PWM compare register value to drive the motor at 45% duty cycle using a Timer0A with $f_{clk} = 80$ MHz and $PWM_{LOAD} = 79999$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the continuous-time PI law and identify the integral term that needs discretization.
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Discretize the integral using the **backward rectangle approximation**:
$$\int_0^{k T_s} e(\tau) d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Write $u[k]$ and $u[k-1]$ explicitly.
$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$
$$u[k-1] = K_p e[k-1] + K_i T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} e[n]$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Subtract to eliminate the summation.
$$u[k] - u[k-1] = K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final recursive form:
$$\boxed{u[k] = u[k-1] + (K_p + K_i T_s) e[k] - K_p e[k-1]}$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute measured RPM.
$$PPR_{eff} = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}, \quad T_g = 0.1 \text{ s}, \quad C = 800$$
$$N_{meas} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2000 \times 0.1} = \frac{48000}{200} = 240 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Verify encoder max frequency.
$$f_{enc} = \frac{800}{0.1} = 8000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz} \quad \text{(well within MCU capability)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** PWM period computation.
$$T_{PWM} = \frac{PWM_{LOAD} + 1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{80000}{80 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$
$$f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ kHz} \quad \text{✓}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare register value for 45% duty.
$$CMPA = 0.45 \times 80000 = 36000$$
$$\boxed{N_{meas} = 240 \text{ RPM}, \quad CMPA = 36000 \text{ for 45\% duty}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete closed-loop RPM control system using a microcontroller. Identify all the sub-systems and the signal flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motor has a measured step-response rise time of 100 ms. Determine an appropriate sampling period $T_s$ for the digital controller, and justify using the Nyquist criterion. The PI gains are $K_p = 0.8$ and $K_i = 4.5$. With $T_s$ you have chosen, compute the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the recursive difference equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the goal — maintain motor RPM at a reference value despite disturbances.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Draw and label the **five-block closed-loop architecture**:
1. **Reference input** (setpoint RPM $N_{ref}$)
2. **Error detector** (summation node: $e = N_{ref} - N_{meas}$)
3. **Digital PI Controller** (in software)
4. **Actuator** (PWM generator + H-bridge driver)
5. **Plant + Sensor** (DC motor + optical encoder providing $N_{meas}$ feedback)

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Describe the signal flow: the encoder generates pulses proportional to speed; the MCU counts them over a gate window $T_g$, computes RPM, compares with $N_{ref}$, executes the PI difference equation, clamps the result, and updates the PWM compare register.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Mention the **anti-aliasing filter** for any analog feedback (current/voltage), the **saturation block** (clamp 0–100% duty), and the **anti-windup logic** to protect the integrator.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute bandwidth.
$$f_{bw} \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r} = \frac{0.35}{0.1} = 3.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply 20× safety factor to Nyquist.
$$f_s = 20 \times 3.5 = 70 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_s \approx 14.3 \text{ ms}$$
Choose $T_s = 10$ ms ($f_s = 100$ Hz) for cleaner code timing.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $\alpha$.
$$\alpha = K_p + K_i T_s = 0.8 + 4.5 \times 0.01 = 0.8 + 0.045 = 0.845$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute $\beta$.
$$\beta = -K_p = -0.8$$

**Final answer (boxed):**
$$\boxed{T_s = 10 \text{ ms}, \quad \alpha = 0.845, \quad \beta = -0.8}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this experiment:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in RPM formula:** Writing $T_g = 100$ instead of $0.1$ in the denominator yields 1000× error. (−3 marks)
> 2. **Confusing PPR vs. CPR:** A 500 PPR encoder is **2000 counts/rev** in ×4 quadrature mode. Forgetting the ×4 factor gives 4× wrong RPM. (−2 marks)
> 3. **No anti-windup clause:** If the code does not clamp $u[k]$ to [0, 100], the PWM register overflows, and the motor either freezes or oscillates wildly. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Wrong timer prescaler:** Computing $T_{PWM}$ without accounting for the prescaler value mis-sets the PWM frequency by 2–256×. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Using forward-rectangle integration in PI:** This can destabilize the loop for large $K_i T_s$. Examiners specifically look for **backward rectangle** or **trapezoidal** rule. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Not justifying sampling rate:** Just writing $T_s = 10$ ms without invoking Nyquist loses the 1-mark justification point. (−1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **RPM formula:** $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot C}{PPR \cdot T_g}$, with $T_g$ **in seconds**.
- ✅ **Encoder resolution:** ×4 quadrature decoding quadruples the effective CPR.
- ✅ **PI difference equation (velocity form):** $u[k] = u[k-1] + (K_p + K_i T_s)e[k] - K_p e[k-1]$.
- ✅ **PI coefficient shorthand:** $\alpha = K_p + K_i T_s$, $\beta = -K_p$.
- ✅ **Backward rectangle** is the safest discretization; forward rectangle can destabilize.
- ✅ **Sampling period rule:** $T_s \leq \dfrac{\tau_m}{10}$ or equivalently $f_s \geq 10 f_{bw}$.
- ✅ **PWM compare register:** $CMPA = D \times (PWM_{LOAD} + 1)$.
- ✅ **Anti-windup is mandatory** — clamp $u[k]$ to [0, 100%].
- ✅ **PI controller gives $e_{ss} = 0$ for step inputs** (Type-1 system).
- ✅ **Five sub-systems:** Sensor → Counter → PI Controller → PWM Driver → Plant.
- ✅ **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{bw}$ (minimum); use 10–20× for safety.
- ✅ **Z-transform of integrator (backward rule):** $\dfrac{T_s z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** CNC spindle, hard-disk drive, drone ESC, EV traction, CPU fan.
- ✅ **KTU-preferred kit:** Tiva C TM4C123GH6PM or TMS320F28379D + 500 PPR DC geared motor + L298N.
- ✅ **Ziegler–Nichols tuning:** Set $K_i = 0$, increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation at $K_u$, period $T_u$, then $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $K_i = 0.54 K_u / T_u$.
- ✅ **Always mention** anti-aliasing RC filter for any ADC feedback.
- ✅ **Lab mantra:** *Sample fast enough (Nyquist), saturate the output, anti-windup, and tune $K_p$ first, then $K_i$.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Validation of Illumination Controller with Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Validation of Illumination Controller with Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Illumination Controller (KTU Definition):** An *embedded closed-loop feedback system* that continuously senses ambient light intensity using a photosensitive transducer, processes the analog signal through an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and dynamically adjusts the luminous output of a light source (LED/Lamp) via **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** such that the effective illumination at a target surface remains constant, regardless of variations in natural daylight.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme DSP/Microcontroller Laboratory framework, the **Illumination Controller Experiment** is treated as a canonical *transducer → signal conditioning → ADC → decision algorithm → actuator* pipeline, implemented on an **8-bit AVR (ATmega328P / Arduino Uno)**, **8051**, or **PIC** family microcontroller.

### 1.2 System Boundary Definition

| Boundary Element | Function | Typical Component |
|------------------|----------|-------------------|
| Input Transducer | Converts lux → electrical signal | **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** / Photodiode |
| Signal Conditioning | Resistor divider / Amplifier | Resistor network, Op-Amp (LM358) |
| Processing Unit | Decision algorithm | **ATmega328P** Microcontroller |
| Output Actuator | Converts PWM → light | LED / MOSFET-driven Lamp |
| Human Interface | Status display | 16x2 LCD / Serial Monitor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests students on the *mapping* between **ADC count (0–1023)** and **PWM duty cycle (0–255)** for ATmega328P. This mapping is a high-yield area in board examinations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are reading a book near a window on a partly cloudy day. As clouds pass, the natural sunlight dims, but you don't consciously think about it — your brain simply tells your eyes to *dilate the pupils* to let in more light. Your eyes are the **sensor**, the brain is the **microcontroller**, and the dilated pupil is the **actuator** adjusting how much external light reaches your retina.

An **Illumination Controller** performs exactly this role for an artificial light source:

- The **LDR** is the "pupil" — it senses ambient light.
- The **Microcontroller** is the "brain" — it computes how much artificial light is needed.
- The **PWM-driven LED** is the "compensating response" — it brightens when ambient light dims and dims when ambient light brightens.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:** This principle is the foundation of *smart street lighting* (IS 1944 standards), *automatic headlights* in automobiles, *greenhouse horticulture lighting*, and *museum display case illumination*, all of which demand energy-efficient, sensor-driven lighting.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference Illuminance:** $E_{ref} = 500 \text{ lux}$ (Office / Reading environment, per IS 3646)
- **LDR Spectral Peak Sensitivity:** $\lambda_{peak} \approx 540 \text{ nm}$ (Human eye matched, photopic vision)
- **ATmega328P ADC Reference Voltage:** $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ (default AVcc)
- **ATmega328P ADC Resolution:** $n = 10 \text{ bits}$ → $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels
- **PWM Frequency (Arduino Default, Pin 9):** $f_{PWM} \approx 490 \text{ Hz}$ (Fast PWM mode)
- **PWM Counter Resolution:** $8 \text{ bits}$ → 256 discrete duty steps (0 to 255)
- **LDR Dark Resistance:** $R_{dark} \approx 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ (at 0 lux)
- **LDR Light Resistance:** $R_{light} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (at 1000 lux)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Five Logical Stages

The illumination controller operates in a **closed-loop, repetitive cycle** (a real-time system), typically executing at a sampling rate of $f_s \approx 10 \text{ Hz}$ (i.e., one control action every 100 ms).

### Stage 1 — Photonic Sensing (LDR Physics)

- A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, also called a *photoresistor*, is a passive semiconductor component whose **electrical resistance decreases as incident light intensity increases**.
- The underlying physical mechanism is the **photoconductive effect**: incoming photons with energy $E_{photon} = h\nu$ excite valence-band electrons into the conduction band of a *cadmium sulfide (CdS)* or *cadmium selenide (CdSe)* polycrystalline thin film, increasing the free carrier concentration $n$, and therefore the conductivity $\sigma = q \cdot n \cdot \mu$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Why is the LDR response non-linear?"* — Because the recombination rate of photo-generated carriers is itself dependent on the carrier density, producing a **logarithmic / power-law** $R$ vs $L$ relationship.

### Stage 2 — Voltage Divider Signal Conditioning

- The LDR cannot directly interface with a digital ADC. It must first be converted into a **voltage level** using a simple voltage divider:

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}(L)}
$$

- **Design Rule:** Place $R_{fixed}$ such that the divider output $V_{out}$ spans the full **0 V to 5 V** range as illumination varies from 0 lux to 1000 lux. Typical choice: $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (a midpoint compromise).

### Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)

- The ATmega328P contains a **successive approximation register (SAR) ADC** with 10-bit resolution.
- The digital output (ADC count) is computed as:

$$
D_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor
$$

- Since $D_{ADC} \in [0, 1023]$ and $V_{in} \in [0, V_{ref}]$, the **LSB voltage** (quantization step) is:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
$$

### Stage 4 — Decision Algorithm (Control Law)

- The microcontroller executes a simple linear mapping: low ADC count (dark) → high PWM duty cycle (bright LED); high ADC count (bright ambient) → low PWM duty cycle (dim LED).

$$
\text{PWM}_{value} = 255 - D_{ADC} \cdot \left( \frac{255}{1023} \right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real-world systems use a **PID controller** for smoother response, but the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates only a *proportional (P-only)* control law for this experiment — sufficient for slow-varying illumination disturbances.

### Stage 5 — PWM Actuation (LED Brightness Control)

- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** rapidly switches the LED between ON and OFF states at a fixed frequency $f_{PWM}$. The *average* voltage across the LED — and therefore its perceived brightness — is proportional to the duty cycle:

$$
D_{\%} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%
$$

- The **average LED voltage** is:

$$
V_{LED,avg} = D_{\%} \cdot V_{CC}
$$

- Because $f_{PWM} \gg$ human flicker fusion threshold ($\approx 100 \text{ Hz}$), the eye perceives a **steady, dimmable** light source.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the right-most column — these are the *only* formulas tested for this experiment in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | LDR Voltage Divider | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$ | $V_{out}$ in **V**, $R$ in **$\Omega$** | $0 \le V_{out} \le V_{CC}$ |
| 2 | ADC Digital Output | $D_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor$ | $D_{ADC}$ dimensionless, $V$ in **V** | $0 \le D_{ADC} \le 1023$ |
| 3 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | $V_{LSB}$ in **V** | For $n=10$, $V_{LSB} \approx 4.88$ mV |
| 4 | PWM Duty Cycle (%) | $D_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | $T$ in **seconds** | $0\% \le D_{\%} \le 100\%$ |
| 5 | PWM 8-bit Register Value | $D_{reg} = \left\lfloor D_{\%} \cdot 2.55 \right\rfloor$ | $D_{reg}$ integer | $0 \le D_{reg} \le 255$ |
| 6 | Linear Inverse Mapping (Control Law) | $D_{reg} = 255 - \left\lfloor D_{ADC} \cdot \dfrac{255}{1023} \right\rfloor$ | Dimensionless | $D_{reg} = 255$ when $D_{ADC}=0$ |
| 7 | Average LED Voltage | $V_{LED,avg} = D_{\%} \cdot V_{CC}$ | $V$ in **V** | $0 \le V_{LED,avg} \le V_{CC}$ |
| 8 | LDR Resistance Model (Empirical) | $R_{LDR}(L) = R_{dark} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{ref}}{L} \right)^{\gamma}$ | $L$ in **lux**, $\gamma \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ | $\gamma$ depends on CdS material |
| 9 | Sampling Period (Application Layer) | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $T_s$ in **s**, $f_s$ in **Hz** | Typical $f_s = 10$ Hz |
| 10 | Power Dissipation in LED | $P_{LED} = V_{LED,avg} \cdot I_{LED}$ | $P$ in **W**, $V$ in **V**, $I$ in **A** | Use $R_{series}$ to limit $I_{LED} \le 20$ mA |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Street Lighting (India — Smart Cities Mission):** LDR + RTC-based dusk-to-dawn control saves ~40% energy.
- **Automotive Auto-Headlamps:** ECE R48 regulation mandates automatic switching based on ambient light.
- **Greenhouse Cultivation:** Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density (PPFD) controllers maintain $400 \text{ μmol/m}^2/\text{s}$ for plant growth.
- **Museum Conservation:** UV-filtered LED arrays with lux-lock prevent artwork degradation.
- **Horticulture / Vertical Farming:** Closed-loop illumination control optimizes crop yield per kWh.
- **Solar-Powered Lanterns (KTU Mini-Project domain):** Energy harvesting + LDR dimming extends battery life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Note:** Modern luxury vehicles use *multi-zone* illumination control with *matrix LED* arrays — each LED segment is PWM-controlled independently, all driven by the same fundamental transducer-ADC-PWM pipeline you are implementing today.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: ADC Count to Illumination Mapping (Inverse Transfer Function)

**Problem Context:** Given the LDR voltage divider output $V_{out}$ is fed to the ATmega328P ADC, derive an explicit expression for the illumination level $L$ in lux as a function of the digital ADC count $D_{ADC}$ read by the microcontroller.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical LDR resistance model**

The light-dependent resistance of an LDR is empirically modeled as a power-law function of illumination:

$$
R_{LDR}(L) = R_{dark} \cdot \left( \frac{L_{ref}}{L} \right)^{\gamma}
$$

where $R_{dark}$ is the resistance at zero illumination, $L_{ref}$ is a reference illumination level (typically 1 lux), and $\gamma$ is the material exponent (typically $0.7 \le \gamma \le 0.9$ for CdS).

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider equation**

The voltage at the ADC input pin is:

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}(L)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the LDR model into the divider equation**

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{dark} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{ref}}{L} \right)^{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the illumination L**

Rearranging to isolate $R_{LDR}(L)$:

$$
R_{LDR}(L) = R_{fixed} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{out}} - 1 \right)
$$

Equating to the empirical model:

$$
R_{dark} \cdot \left( \frac{L_{ref}}{L} \right)^{\gamma} = R_{fixed} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{out}} - 1 \right)
$$

Isolating $L$:

$$
\left( \frac{L_{ref}}{L} \right)^{\gamma} = \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{dark}} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{out}} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
L = L_{ref} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_{dark}}{R_{fixed}} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{out}}{V_{CC} - V_{out}} \right) \right]^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert analog voltage to ADC count**

The ATmega328P ADC produces:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{ref}
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression for L in terms of D_ADC**

$$
L = L_{ref} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_{dark}}{R_{fixed}} \cdot \left( \frac{D_{ADC} \cdot V_{ref}}{1023 \cdot V_{CC} - D_{ADC} \cdot V_{ref}} \right) \right]^{1/\gamma}
$$

For the common simplification $V_{ref} = V_{CC} = 5.0 \text{ V}$:

$$
L = L_{ref} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_{dark}}{R_{fixed}} \cdot \left( \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023 - D_{ADC}} \right) \right]^{1/\gamma}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** KTU examiners love asking *"Given ADC count = X, what is the approximate illumination?"* — and this formula is the only correct path to the answer.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PWM Duty Cycle to LED Luminous Flux (Forward Transfer Function)

**Problem Context:** Derive the relationship between the 8-bit PWM register value (0–255) and the luminous flux $\Phi$ (in lumens) emitted by the LED.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Relate PWM register value to duty cycle fraction**

The 8-bit PWM register accepts values from 0 to 255:

$$
D_{fraction} = \frac{D_{reg}}{255}, \quad \text{where } 0 \le D_{reg} \le 255
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average voltage across the LED**

Because the LED is switched between 0 V and $V_{CC}$ at high frequency, its time-averaged voltage is:

$$
V_{LED,avg} = D_{fraction} \cdot V_{CC} = \frac{D_{reg}}{255} \cdot V_{CC}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average current through the LED (with series resistor $R_S$)**

The LED current is governed by Ohm's law applied to the series resistor:

$$
I_{LED,avg} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{LED,drop} - V_{LED,avg}}{R_S}
$$

where $V_{LED,drop} \approx 2.0 \text{ V}$ for a typical red LED.

**Step 4 — Relate LED current to luminous flux using the luminous efficacy $\eta_{lum}$**

The luminous flux emitted by an LED is given by:

$$
\Phi = \eta_{lum} \cdot P_{electrical} = \eta_{lum} \cdot V_{LED,avg} \cdot I_{LED,avg}
$$

where $\eta_{lum}$ is the **luminous efficacy** in **lumens per watt** (lm/W).

**Step 5 — Substitute the PWM variable**

$$
\Phi = \eta_{lum} \cdot \left( \frac{D_{reg}}{255} \cdot V_{CC} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC} - V_{LED,drop} - \dfrac{D_{reg}}{255} \cdot V_{CC}}{R_S} \right)
$$

For the special case where $V_{LED,drop}$ is small compared to $V_{CC}$:

$$
\Phi \approx \eta_{lum} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}^2}{255 \cdot R_S} \cdot D_{reg} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{D_{reg}}{255} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** This reveals that luminous flux is **NOT linearly** proportional to $D_{reg}$. The $(1 - D_{reg}/255)$ factor creates a parabolic response. The flux is maximized at $D_{reg} = 127$ (50% duty cycle). For $D_{reg} > 127$, the LED current *decreases* because the average LED forward voltage itself increases — the LED spends more time in its non-linear knee region.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Sample KTU-Style Numerical)

**Given:**
- $V_{CC} = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $n = 10$ bits
- $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{dark} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $L_{ref} = 1$ lux, $\gamma = 0.8$
- ADC reading from LDR divider: $D_{ADC} = 300$

**Find:** (a) $V_{out}$ at ADC pin, (b) $R_{LDR}$ value, (c) Approximate illumination $L$ in lux, (d) Required 8-bit PWM register value to *fully compensate* (i.e., produce maximum brightness).

### Solution

**Part (a) — Compute $V_{out}$:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{300}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \approx 1.466 \text{ V}
$$

**Part (b) — Compute $R_{LDR}$:**

$$
R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{out}} - 1 \right) = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot \left( \frac{5.0}{1.466} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
R_{LDR} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot (3.4107 - 1) = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 2.4107 \approx 24.107 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Part (c) — Compute illumination L:**

$$
L = L_{ref} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_{dark}}{R_{fixed}} \cdot \left( \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023 - D_{ADC}} \right) \right]^{1/\gamma}
$$

$$
L = 1 \cdot \left[ \frac{1 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^3} \cdot \left( \frac{300}{723} \right) \right]^{1/0.8}
$$

$$
L = \left[ 100 \cdot 0.4149 \right]^{1.25} = (41.49)^{1.25}
$$

$$
L \approx 41.49^{1.25} \approx 96.85 \text{ lux}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An ADC count of 300 corresponds to a relatively dim room (~97 lux) — typical of a corridor or stairwell. Full compensation would require the LED at near-maximum brightness.

**Part (d) — Required PWM register value for maximum compensation (full LED ON):**

Since lower ADC counts indicate darker environments, the controller ramps up the LED. For complete compensation at $D_{ADC} = 300$:

$$
D_{reg} = 255 - \left\lfloor 300 \cdot \frac{255}{1023} \right\rfloor = 255 - \left\lfloor 74.78 \right\rfloor = 255 - 74 = 181
$$

So the LED operates at approximately $181/255 \approx 70.98\%$ duty cycle — visually perceived as "fairly bright" but not blinding.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Table:** This matrix identifies *how* the system output changes when each parameter is perturbed. Examiners may ask students to comment on sensitivity.

| Parameter Variation | Effect on $V_{out}$ | Effect on $D_{ADC}$ | Effect on LED Brightness |
|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Ambient light ↑ (L increases) | $R_{LDR}$ ↓, so $V_{out}$ ↓ | $D_{ADC}$ ↓ | $D_{reg}$ ↑ (LED brightens to compensate) |
| Ambient light ↓ (L decreases) | $R_{LDR}$ ↑, so $V_{out}$ ↑ | $D_{ADC}$ ↑ | $D_{reg}$ ↓ (LED dims) |
| $R_{fixed}$ ↑ (larger fixed resistor) | $V_{out}$ becomes more sensitive to $R_{LDR}$ changes | $D_{ADC}$ changes amplified | Greater contrast in LED brightness |
| $V_{ref}$ ↑ (higher reference) | $V_{LSB}$ ↑, coarser quantization | Smaller count range for same input | Smoother LED response (less visible steps) |
| $V_{ref}$ ↓ (lower reference) | $V_{LSB}$ ↓, finer quantization | Larger count range for same input | Stepper LED response (more visible flicker) |
| $f_{PWM}$ ↑ (higher PWM frequency) | No effect on $V_{out}$ | No effect on $D_{ADC}$ | Flicker-free even at low duty cycles |

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control System Block Diagram (Signal Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference 500 lux] -->|+| Sum1[Summing Junction]
    F[Feedback: LDR measured] -->|- | Sum1
    Sum1 -->|Error e| C[Controller P-only Kp]
    C -->|PWM command| P[Plant LED + LDR sensor]
    P -->|Illuminated Surface| M[Measurement Output]
    M --> F
    style R fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** This is the *classical control theory* view of the illumination controller. The summing junction computes the *error* between the *desired* lux (reference) and the *measured* lux (LDR feedback). The proportional controller (K_p) produces a PWM command that drives the LED — which in turn affects the measured illumination, closing the loop. For the KTU 2024 experiment, $K_p$ is implicitly absorbed into the linear mapping $D_{reg} = 255 - D_{ADC} \cdot 255/1023$.

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Reading:** A 25% duty cycle means the LED is ON for only ~510 μs out of every 2.04 ms period. The eye integrates this into a "dim" perception. A 75% duty cycle means the LED is ON for ~1.53 ms — perceived as "bright." This is the *temporal averaging* principle behind PWM dimming.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define an *illumination controller* and list the four essential hardware blocks required to implement it on a microcontroller platform.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** An illumination controller is an embedded closed-loop system that senses ambient light intensity and automatically adjusts the brightness of an artificial light source to maintain a constant illumination level at a target surface.

- **Hardware Blocks (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **Light Sensor** — LDR or photodiode (input transducer)
  2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — voltage divider network
  3. **Microcontroller Unit** — ATmega328P with built-in ADC
  4. **Actuator** — LED driven via MOSFET using PWM

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain why **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is preferred over a simple variable resistor (rheostat) for LED brightness control in microcontroller-based systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Power Efficiency Argument (1 Mark):** A rheostat dissipates excess power as heat ($P = I^2R$), wasting energy. A PWM-driven MOSFET switch dissipates almost zero power when fully ON or fully OFF — efficiency approaches ~95% versus ~50% for rheostatic control.

- **Digital Control Compatibility (1 Mark):** PWM is generated directly by the microcontroller's timer hardware, requiring no DAC or analog peripheral. The 8-bit duty register maps directly to a 0–255 brightness scale.

- **Thermal Stability (1 Mark):** Rheostats heat up and drift, while MOSFET switches remain thermally stable, ensuring consistent brightness over time.
