## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **confuse ADC resolution with PWM resolution** (10-bit vs 8-bit) — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if incorrect.
> - Many students **forget the voltage divider derivation** and write the LDR output as a direct voltage — losing 3 marks.
> - Avoid writing generic "we use Arduino" answers — KTU requires **register-level understanding** (e.g., `ADMUX`, `ADCSRA`, `OCR1A`).
> - The mapping formula $D_{reg} = 255 - D_{ADC} \cdot 255/1023$ is the *core deliverable* — examiners will check it appears with correct sign.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module Experiment 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a microcontroller-based illumination controller. Identify each block and state the function of the same. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the 10-bit ADC count obtained from the LDR voltage divider and the corresponding 8-bit PWM register value required to drive the LED in a *proportional inverse* control scheme. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

The illumination controller is implemented using the following block-level architecture:

| Block # | Block Name | Component | Function |
|---------|------------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Light Sensor | LDR (GL5528) | Converts incident light intensity (lux) into a resistance variation |
| 2 | Voltage Divider | LDR + 10 kΩ fixed resistor | Converts resistance variation into an analog voltage $V_{out} \in [0, 5]$ V |
| 3 | ADC | ATmega328P Channel ADC0 (Pin A0) | Converts analog $V_{out}$ into 10-bit digital $D_{ADC} \in [0, 1023]$ |
| 4 | Microcontroller | ATmega328P @ 16 MHz | Executes control algorithm and generates PWM |
| 5 | PWM Generator | Timer1 Fast PWM, Pin D9 | Produces variable duty-cycle square wave at 490 Hz |
| 6 | LED Driver | IRFZ44N N-MOSFET | Switches LED current according to PWM signal |
| 7 | Light Source | 1 W White LED | Provides compensating artificial illumination |
| 8 | Feedback Path | Optical coupling (ambient + LED) | Returns to LDR — closes the control loop |

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1).**

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark for sensor identification, 1 mark for divider, 1 mark for ADC, 1 mark for MCU, 1 mark for PWM, 1 mark for MOSFET driver, 1 mark for closed-loop identification.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Express $V_{out}$ at the ADC pin in terms of $D_{ADC}$:**

The ATmega328P ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and $n = 10$ bits produces:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{D_{ADC}}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the inverse relationship:** A dark environment produces a *low* $D_{ADC}$ (since the LDR is dark, the divider outputs a low voltage), but a *high* $D_{reg}$ is required (to make the LED bright). Therefore, the control law must be **inversely proportional**:

$$
D_{reg} = 255 - f(D_{ADC})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply linear scaling:** The 10-bit ADC count must be linearly mapped to an 8-bit PWM range:

$$
D_{reg} = 255 - \left( D_{ADC} \cdot \frac{255}{1023} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply floor function to ensure integer output:**

$$
D_{reg} = 255 - \left\lfloor D_{ADC} \cdot \frac{255}{1023} \right\rfloor, \quad 0 \le D_{reg} \le 255
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary verification:**
- If $D_{ADC} = 0$ (pitch dark) → $D_{reg} = 255$ (LED at full brightness) ✓
- If $D_{ADC} = 1023$ (max bright) → $D_{reg} = 0$ (LED off) ✓

**Step 6 — Final boxed answer:**

$$
\boxed{D_{reg} = 255 - \left\lfloor \frac{255 \cdot D_{ADC}}{1023} \right\rfloor}
$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark for ADC equation, 1 mark for identifying inverse relationship, 2 marks for linear mapping derivation, 1 mark for floor function, 1 mark for boundary verification, 1 mark for final boxed result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module Experiment 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A laboratory illumination controller uses an LDR with $R_{dark} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ and $R_{light} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ at 1000 lux, connected in a voltage divider with $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $V_{CC} = 5$ V. The divider output is fed to a 10-bit ADC. Calculate: **(i)** the ADC count when the ambient illumination is 100 lux, **(ii)** the corresponding 8-bit PWM register value to fully compensate. Assume the LDR follows the power-law model with $\gamma = 0.8$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the complete circuit schematic of the illumination controller (label all component values) and explain the role of the N-channel MOSFET in the LED driver stage. Why is a current-limiting resistor essential? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{LDR}$ at 100 lux using the power-law model.**

We use the empirical model with $R_{dark} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ at $L_{ref} = 1$ lux and the constraint that $R_{LDR} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ at $L = 1000$ lux.

From $R_{LDR}(L) = R_{dark} \cdot (L_{ref}/L)^{\gamma}$:

$$
R_{LDR}(1000) = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot (1/1000)^{0.8} = 10^6 \cdot 10^{-2.4} = 10^6 \cdot 3.98 \times 10^{-3} = 3.98 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

But the problem states $R_{light} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ at 1000 lux. We must reconcile by adjusting $\gamma$ — or we take the problem at face value and use linear interpolation. The KTU-expected approach:

$$
R_{LDR}(100) = R_{dark} \cdot \left(\frac{R_{light}}{R_{dark}}\right)^{(1 - L/L_{max})}
$$

where $L_{max} = 1000$ lux:

$$
R_{LDR}(100) = 10^6 \cdot \left(\frac{2 \times 10^3}{10^6}\right)^{(1 - 100/1000)} = 10^6 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})^{0.9}
$$

$$
R_{LDR}(100) = 10^6 \cdot (0.002)^{0.9} \approx 10^6 \cdot 3.866 \times 10^{-3} \approx 3.87 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the divider output voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega + 3.87 \text{ k}\Omega}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{10}{13.87} \approx 3.605 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 10-bit ADC count:**

$$
D_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.605}{5.0} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 737.6 \right\rfloor = 737
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 8-bit PWM register value for full compensation:**

$$
D_{reg} = 255 - \left\lfloor 737 \cdot \frac{255}{1023} \right\rfloor = 255 - \left\lfloor 183.7 \right\rfloor = 255 - 183 = 72
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $D_{ADC} \approx 737$
- (ii) $D_{reg} = 72$ (corresponds to ~28.2% LED brightness — partial compensation since ambient is reasonably bright)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

**Circuit Schematic (Refer to Section 4.2).** The key elements are:

- LDR connected between $V_{CC}$ and the ADC pin (Pin A0)
- $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ connected between ADC pin and GND
- LED + series resistor $R_S = 47 \text{ \Omega}$ connected between $V_{CC}$ and the MOSFET drain
- MOSFET source connected to GND
- MOSFET gate driven by PWM pin (D9) through a 100 Ω gate resistor

**Role of the N-channel MOSFET (2 marks):**
The IRFZ44N acts as a **low-side electronic switch**. When the PWM signal is HIGH, the gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ exceeds the threshold (typically $V_{GS,th} \approx 4$ V), turning the MOSFET ON and allowing current to flow through the LED. When PWM is LOW, the MOSFET turns OFF, blocking current. The high switching speed (nanoseconds) and very low $R_{DS,on}$ (typically 17.5 mΩ) make it ideal for high-frequency PWM dimming without significant power dissipation.

**Why a current-limiting resistor is essential (2 marks):**
An LED is a **non-linear current-driven device**. Without $R_S$, the LED's I-V curve would allow the current to rise to destructive levels once the forward voltage is exceeded. The resistor $R_S$ limits the peak current to a safe value:

$$
I_{LED,max} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{LED,drop}}{R_S} = \frac{5.0 - 3.2}{47} \approx 38.3 \text{ mA}
$$

This keeps the current below the LED's maximum rating (typically 50–100 mA) and prevents thermal runaway. Additionally, $R_S$ provides a *soft start* during PWM transitions, reducing inrush current spikes.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 2 marks for circuit, 2 marks for MOSFET explanation, 2 marks for current-limiting resistor, 1 mark for numerical example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Board Exam Revision — Memorize This Block**

### Core Concept Traps

- ☐ **ADC is 10-bit, PWM is 8-bit** — this asymmetry is intentional and central to the experiment.
- ☐ The mapping formula is **inverse**: $D_{reg} = 255 - (\text{something proportional to } D_{ADC})$. The minus sign is NOT optional.
- ☐ The voltage divider equation: $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{fixed} / (R_{fixed} + R_{LDR})$. Memorize this.
- ☐ $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / 2^n$. For 5 V and 10-bit ADC, $V_{LSB} \approx 4.88$ mV.

### Hardware Pin Map (Arduino Uno / ATmega328P)

- ☐ **A0** → LDR voltage divider output (ADC channel 0)
- ☐ **D9** → PWM output to MOSFET gate (Timer1 OC1A)
- ☐ **5V** → $V_{CC}$ for sensor and LED anode side
- ☐ **GND** → Common ground for all modules

### Critical Constants (Memorize)

- ☐ $R_{dark} \approx 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, $R_{light} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- ☐ $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ (AVcc default)
- ☐ $f_{PWM} = 490 \text{ Hz}$ (Arduino default, Pin 9)
- ☐ Human flicker fusion threshold: $\approx 100$ Hz
- ☐ Standard office lux: $500$ lux (IS 3646)

### Algorithm Steps (Firmware Loop)

- ☐ Step 1: `analogRead(A0)` → 10-bit $D_{ADC}$
- ☐ Step 2: Compute $D_{reg} = 255 - D_{ADC} \cdot 255/1023$
- ☐ Step 3: `analogWrite(9, D_{reg})` → 8-bit PWM
- ☐ Step 4: `delay(100)` — 100 ms sampling period

### Common Board Pitfalls (Avoid These)

- ☐ ❌ Writing `$D_{reg} = D_{ADC} \cdot 255/1023$` (forgot the **inversion**) → -2 marks
- ☐ ❌ Confusing 1024 vs 1023 in the ADC equation → -1 mark
- ☐ ❌ Omitting the floor function `⌊ ⌋` for integer conversion → -1 mark
- ☐ ❌ Using 16-bit PWM register values (0–65535) instead of 8-bit (0–255) → -3 marks
- ☐ ❌ Drawing a rheostat instead of a MOSFET driver for the LED → -2 marks
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting the current-limiting resistor in the circuit → -2 marks
- ☐ ❌ Not identifying the **closed-loop** nature of the system → -1 mark

### One-Line Definition (For 1-Mark Sub-Questions)

- ☐ *"An illumination controller is a closed-loop embedded system that uses an LDR sensor and PWM-driven LED to maintain constant ambient light, implemented via the formula $D_{reg} = 255 - \lfloor 255 \cdot D_{ADC} / 1023 \rfloor$."*

### Real-World Application Recall (For Higher-Mark Questions)

- ☐ Smart street lighting, automotive auto-headlamps, greenhouse horticulture, museum artifact preservation, solar lantern brightness control, mobile phone auto-brightness.

### Final Master Formula (Universal)

$$
\boxed{D_{reg}(D_{ADC}) = 255 - \left\lfloor \frac{255 \cdot D_{ADC}}{1023} \right\rfloor, \quad D_{ADC} \in [0, 1023]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Closing Advice:** When the KTU examiner asks *"Implement the illumination controller,"* they are really asking for **four deliverables**: (1) the circuit diagram, (2) the mapping formula, (3) the algorithm flowchart, and (4) a verification table showing $D_{ADC}$ vs $D_{reg}$ for at least 5 sample points. Master these four, and full marks are virtually guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Validation of Water Level Controller with Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Validation of Water Level Controller with Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Water Level Controller (WLC)** is a real-time, closed-loop **embedded signal processing and control system** that uses a **microcontroller-based acquisition and decision unit** to monitor the level of water in a tank (or reservoir) through analogue or digital sensors, perform **signal conditioning** (amplification, filtering, thresholding), and actuate a pump or solenoid valve to maintain the level within prescribed upper and lower bounds.

In the context of **DSP Lab (PCCPL507)**, the experiment is framed as a **Discrete-Time Control Application** of the Digital Signal Processor (DSP) or general-purpose microcontroller, where the water level acts as the **continuous-time physical signal** $h(t)$ that is sampled at discrete intervals $t = nT_s$, quantized, and processed using a deterministic control law:

$$h[n] = h(nT_s), \quad T_s = \text{Sampling Period}$$

The output of the controller, $u[n]$, drives a final control element (relay/MOSFET/thyristor) to energize or de-energize the pump motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment demonstrates three foundational DSP concepts — **signal acquisition (sampling/quantization), signal conditioning (filtering/hysteresis), and real-time decision making (threshold-based control)** — implemented on a programmable digital platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bathroom shower with an instant-typed water heater**. The heater doesn't blast full power the moment you turn the tap. Instead, it:

1. **Feels** the water temperature (sense)
2. **Compares** it to a comfortable setpoint (process)
3. **Switches ON/OFF** the heating element when the temperature drifts outside a small comfort band (actuate)

A water level controller is the *vertical* (level) cousin of that *horizontal* (temperature) feedback system. The microcontroller is the "thermostat brain" of the tank. The only difference is that the physical variable is **height of water (cm/m)** rather than **temperature (°C)**, and the controller's job is to switch a **pump** ON when the water falls too low and OFF when it rises too high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> - The sensor is the *eyes* of the system.
> - The microcontroller is the *brain* applying rules.
> - The relay + pump is the *muscle* doing the work.
> - The hysteresis band is the *patience* of the controller — it prevents frantic switching (chattering) when the level hovers near a single threshold.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Remarks |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|---------|
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | **100 ms – 500 ms** | Dictated by tank dynamics |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | **2 Hz – 10 Hz** | $f_s = 1/T_s$ |
| Hysteresis band | $\Delta h$ | **5 % – 10 %** of full-scale | Prevents relay chattering |
| ADC resolution | $n$ | **10-bit / 12-bit** | Common in MSP430, STM32, Arduino |
| Quantization step | $q$ | $\text{FSR}/2^n$ | Smallest detectable level change |
| Full-scale range | $\text{FSR}$ | **0 – 5 V or 0 – 3.3 V** | Depends on sensor interface |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Viva:** A water level controller is essentially an **A/D Converter → Algorithm → D/A (or GPIO) Converter** pipeline. This is the simplest possible real-world DSP application and is frequently asked in viva voce to test whether the student truly understands the link between continuous physical signals and discrete digital processing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

The complete operation of a microcontroller-based Water Level Controller is divided into **six sequential blocks**. Each block has a clear DSP/embedded meaning:

1. **Physical Signal Source** — The water in the tank is the continuous-time system. Its level $h(t)$ is a **band-limited** (low-frequency, $f_m \ll 1\,\text{Hz}$) signal.
2. **Transducer (Sensor Stage)** — Converts $h(t)$ into a measurable electrical quantity (resistance, voltage, frequency, or digital pulse).
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification (op-amp), RC low-pass filtering to suppress **50 Hz mains hum** and sensor noise, and level shifting to fit ADC input range.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Samples and quantizes the conditioned signal. This is the **first DSP operation**.
5. **Decision Algorithm (Software)** — Compares $h[n]$ with thresholds, applies **hysteresis** and **de-bouncing** to produce a clean binary control output $u[n] \in \{0, 1\}$.
6. **Actuation Stage** — A relay, opto-isolated driver, or solid-state switch (MOSFET/SSR/TRIAC) energizes the single-phase pump motor.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why sample slowly?** Water level changes very slowly (minutes timescale), so $f_s$ can be as low as **1–10 Hz**, well below the Nyquist limit. This leaves CPU headroom for other tasks.
- **Why add hysteresis?** Without it, a wave on the water surface or a noisy ADC reading would cause the relay to flip **hundreds of times per minute** — destroying the relay contacts and the pump motor.
- **Why use a low-pass filter?** Sensor cables behave like antennas picking up electromagnetic interference (EMI) from the 230 V AC mains. A first-order RC filter with $f_c \approx 1\,\text{Hz}$ is more than sufficient.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU exam question on this topic is solvable with these equations.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | How often we read the ADC |
| 2 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Avoid aliasing of level fluctuations |
| 3 | ADC quantization step | $q = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1}$ | LSB of the A/D converter |
| 4 | Level resolution | $\Delta h = \dfrac{h_{\max}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable water-level change (cm) |
| 5 | Sensor transfer function (conductive probe) | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{water}}}{R_{\text{ref}} + R_{\text{water}}}$ | Voltage divider with water as resistor |
| 6 | Sensor transfer function (ultrasonic HC-SR04) | $h = \dfrac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$ | Time-of-flight principle ($v_{\text{sound}} \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$) |
| 7 | Hysteresis ON threshold | $h_{\text{ON}} = h_{\text{low}} + \Delta h / 2$ | Pump switches ON below this level |
| 8 | Hysteresis OFF threshold | $h_{\text{OFF}} = h_{\text{high}} - \Delta h / 2$ | Pump switches OFF above this level |
| 9 | RC low-pass filter cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Anti-aliasing and noise suppression |
| 10 | Relay coil current | $I_{\text{coil}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cc}}}{R_{\text{coil}}}$ | Must be within GPIO sink current limit |
| 11 | De-bounce time | $t_d = N \cdot T_s$ | Confirm level reading $N$ consecutive times |
| 12 | Control loop latency | $t_{\text{loop}} = t_{\text{ADC}} + t_{\text{filter}} + t_{\text{decision}} + t_{\text{actuate}}$ | Real-time deadline that must be met |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The same architecture (sense → condition → process → actuate) is used in **industrial boiler control, irrigation automation, chemical reactor level management, pharmaceutical batch processing, and flood-warning systems**. Mastering this experiment gives the student a reusable template for any real-time DSP-driven control project.

## 2.3 Practical Engineering Constraints

When implementing this system, the following physical and electrical constraints **must** be respected (these are common viva questions):

- **Galvanic isolation** between the 230 V AC pump line and the 5 V DC microcontroller via an **opto-coupler (e.g., MOC3021 + BT136 TRIAC)** or a relay with proper flyback diode (1N4007).
- **Surge protection** with an MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor) across the relay contacts.
- **Debouncing** both the **mechanical relay** (in software) and the **mechanical float switch** (in hardware via RC + Schmitt trigger).
- **Watchdog timer** must be enabled to recover the controller from software lockup due to EMI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Step of a 10-bit ADC

The microcontroller (e.g., Arduino Uno's ATmega328P) has a **10-bit successive approximation ADC** with a default reference voltage of $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of discrete output codes**

For an $n$-bit ADC, the total number of quantization levels is:

$$L = 2^n$$

For $n = 10$:

$$L = 2^{10} = 1024$$

**Step 2 — Compute the voltage per Least Significant Bit (LSB)**

The quantization step (often denoted $q$ or $\Delta V$) is the voltage difference between two adjacent codes. Since the ADC spans $0\,\text{V}$ to $V_{\text{ref}}$ across $2^n - 1$ intervals (the "minus one" accounts for the zero code):

$$q = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1}$$

Substituting $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\,\text{V}$ and $n = 10$:

$$q = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.888 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \approx 4.89\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Convert quantization step to physical water-level resolution**

If the sensor's full-scale range corresponds to a tank height of $h_{\max} = 100\,\text{cm}$ mapped to $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\,\text{V}$, the smallest detectable level change is:

$$\Delta h = \frac{h_{\max}}{2^n} = \frac{100\,\text{cm}}{1024} \approx 0.0977\,\text{cm} \approx 1\,\text{mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering interpretation:** With a 10-bit ADC, the system can detect a water-level change of roughly **1 mm** — far finer than necessary for domestic applications, confirming that 10-bit resolution is more than adequate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Choice of Sampling Period (Nyquist Applied to Tank Dynamics)

The water level in a domestic overhead tank typically fills in 5–10 minutes and empties in 1–2 hours. The dominant signal frequency is therefore:

$$f_m = \frac{1}{T_{\text{fill}}} \approx \frac{1}{300\,\text{s}} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Hz}$$

The Nyquist sampling theorem requires:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 3.3 \times 10^{-3} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Hz}$$

This is an absurdly slow requirement. In practice, we sample much faster (typically 1–10 Hz) for two reasons:

1. To give the user **responsive feedback** (LED/LCD update within 100 ms).
2. To **average out** random sensor noise — a moving average of $N = 10$ samples reduces variance by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.

Thus the **practical** choice is:

$$f_s = 10\,\text{Hz} \implies T_s = 100\,\text{ms}$$

This is **1500× faster** than the Nyquist minimum, leaving enormous CPU margin.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hysteresis Bandwidth for Anti-Chatter

Without hysteresis, the controller flips state at a single threshold $h_{\text{th}}$. A noisy reading fluctuating by $\pm \delta h$ around $h_{\text{th}}$ causes multiple transitions:

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & h[n] \leq h_{\text{th}} \\ 0, & h[n] > h_{\text{th}} \end{cases}$$

**With hysteresis**, two thresholds are defined:

$$h_{\text{low}} = h_{\min} \quad (\text{pump turns ON when level falls below this})$$

$$h_{\text{high}} = h_{\max} \quad (\text{pump turns OFF when level rises above this})$$

The hysteresis band is:

$$\Delta h = h_{\text{high}} - h_{\text{low}}$$

**For a 100 cm tank, a sensible choice is:**

$$h_{\text{low}} = 20\,\text{cm} \quad (\text{20% of full})$$

$$h_{\text{high}} = 90\,\text{cm} \quad (\text{90% of full})$$

$$\Delta h = 90 - 20 = 70\,\text{cm}$$

**Worked numeric example — Convert to ADC counts:**

Assume the sensor's analog voltage is linear: $V(h) = 0.05 \cdot h$ (so $5\,\text{V}$ at $h = 100\,\text{cm}$).

Voltage at lower threshold:

$$V_{\text{low}} = 0.05 \times 20 = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

ADC count for $1.0\,\text{V}$:

$$N_{\text{low}} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{low}}}{q} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.0}{0.00489} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 204.5 \right\rfloor = 204$$

Voltage at upper threshold:

$$V_{\text{high}} = 0.05 \times 90 = 4.5\,\text{V}$$

ADC count for $4.5\,\text{V}$:

$$N_{\text{high}} = \left\lfloor \frac{4.5}{0.00489} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 920.25 \right\rfloor = 920$$

The firmware then becomes:

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1 \; (\text{ON}), & \text{if } (u[n-1] = 0) \text{ AND } (h[n] < h_{\text{low}}) \\ 0 \; (\text{OFF}), & \text{if } (u[n-1] = 1) \text{ AND } (h[n] > h_{\text{high}}) \\ u[n-1], & \text{otherwise (hold previous state)} \end{cases}$$

This is a classic **two-state machine with memory** (a Schmitt trigger in software).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter Design

To suppress 50 Hz mains hum induced in the sensor cable, we need a low-pass filter with cutoff well below 50 Hz. Choosing:

$$f_c = 1.59\,\text{Hz} \quad (\text{so that } 50\,\text{Hz} \text{ is attenuated by } \approx 30\,\text{dB})$$

Choose $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$ (a non-polarized film capacitor for sensor signal path).

Solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1.59 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{9.99 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 100\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Verification of attenuation at 50 Hz:**

Magnitude of first-order LPF transfer function:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

For $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $f_c = 1.59\,\text{Hz}$:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (50/1.59)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (31.45)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{990.3}} \approx 0.0318$$

In decibels:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.0318) \approx -29.96\,\text{dB} \approx -30\,\text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The 50 Hz interference is attenuated by a factor of **~31** (i.e., the noise voltage is reduced to about 3 % of its original value). This is more than adequate for a robust WLC.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sensing Methods

| Method | Output Type | Range | Accuracy | Cost | DSP Complexity | KTU Exam Favourite? |
|--------|------------|-------|----------|------|----------------|---------------------|
| Float switch (mechanical) | Digital (ON/OFF) | Discrete levels | ±1 switch position | **Low** | Trivial (GPIO) | ✓✓✓ |
| Conductive probes (resistive) | Analog (voltage) | Continuous | ±1 cm | **Low** | ADC + thresholding | ✓✓ |
| Capacitive sensor | Analog (capacitance → voltage) | Continuous | ±0.5 cm | Medium | ADC + calibration | ✓ |
| Ultrasonic HC-SR04 | Digital (pulse width) | 2 cm – 400 cm | ±3 mm | **Low** | Timer capture + math | ✓✓✓ |
| Hydrostatic pressure transducer | Analog (4–20 mA / 0.5–4.5 V) | Continuous | ±0.1 % FS | High | ADC + linearization | ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "Why not use just a float switch?" — Because a float switch is a **point sensor** (detects only one level), while a **continuous sensor + ADC** gives the controller a **proportional view** of the entire tank, enabling smarter algorithms (PID, predictive emptying, leak detection).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

This diagram shows the **complete signal flow** from the physical water tank to the final pump motor, with the microcontroller's three internal subsystems (ADC, CPU, GPIO) explicitly shown.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankA[Water Tank - Physical Reservoir] --> sensA[Level Sensor - Transducer]
    sensA --> condA[Signal Conditioning - Amplifier and RC Low-Pass Filter]
    condA --> adcA[ADC Subsystem - 10 bit Successive Approximation]
    adcA --> cpuA[CPU Core - Hysteresis Control Algorithm]
    cpuA --> gpioA[GPIO Output - Galvanically Isolated Driver]
    gpioA --> relayA[Relay or Solid State Switch]
    relayA --> pumpA[AC Pump Motor 230V Single Phase]
    cpuA --> lcdA[LCD 16x2 Display - Real Time Level in cm and percent]
    cpuA --> ledA[Status LEDs - Green ON Red OFF Yellow Alarm]
    cpuA --> wdtA[Watchdog Timer - System Health Monitor]
    wdtA -.resets.-> cpuA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace any signal from the tank to the pump. Every block transforms the signal — physical (height) → electrical (voltage) → digital (counts) → logical (decision) → electrical (relay coil) → physical (motor rotation). This is a textbook **transducer → processor → actuator** chain.

## 4.2 Control Algorithm Flowchart (Finite State Machine)

This diagram represents the **software control loop** that runs on the microcontroller every $T_s = 100\,\text{ms}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([System Power On]) --> initA[Initialize GPIO, ADC, LCD, Watchdog]
    initA --> readA[Read Sensor Voltage via ADC Channel]
    readA --> filtA[Apply Digital Moving Average Filter N equals 8]
    filtA --> calcA[Convert ADC Count to Water Level in cm]
    calcA --> decA{Is Current Pump State ON or OFF}
    decA -->|OFF path| chk1A{Is Level less than Lower Threshold hlow}
    chk1A -->|YES| act1A[Activate Relay - Pump ON]
    chk1A -->|NO| hold1A[Hold Pump OFF - Return to Loop]
    act1A --> updA[Update LCD and Status LED]
    hold1A --> updA
    decA -->|ON path| chk2A{Is Level greater than Upper Threshold hhigh}
    chk2A -->|YES| act2A[Deactivate Relay - Pump OFF]
    chk2A -->|NO| hold2A[Hold Pump ON - Return to Loop]
    act2A --> updA
    hold2A --> updA
    updA --> wdtA2[Kick Watchdog Timer]
    wdtA2 --> delayA[Delay Ts equals 100 ms]
    delayA --> readA
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis State Diagram (Schmitt Trigger Equivalent)

This diagram isolates the **decision logic** — the heart of the anti-chatter mechanism.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateOff
    stateOff --> stateOn : level falls below hlow AND debounce confirmed
    stateOn --> stateOff : level rises above hhigh AND debounce confirmed
    stateOff : Pump OFF
    stateOff : Relay De-energized
    stateOff : Green LED lit
    stateOn : Pump ON
    stateOn : Relay Energized
    stateOn : Red LED lit
    note right of stateOff : Debounce counter resets if level returns above hlow
    note right of stateOn : Debounce counter resets if level returns below hhigh
```

## 4.4 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Block-Level Pin Map)

This diagram illustrates the **electrical connections** between the microcontroller, the sensor, the relay driver, and the indicators.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph uC[Microcontroller Unit - MSP430G2553 or Arduino Uno]
        pinVcc[Pin VCC - 5V or 3.3V]:::power
        pinGnd[Pin GND - 0V Reference]:::power
        pinA0[Pin A0 - ADC Input Channel 0]:::analog
        pinD2[Pin D2 - Digital GPIO Output]:::digital
        pinD3[Pin D3 - Digital GPIO Output]:::digital
        pinD4[Pin D4 - Digital GPIO Output]:::digital
        pinD5[Pin D5 - Digital GPIO Output]:::digital
    end
    pinVcc --- sensB[Sensor VCC]
    pinGnd --- sensC[Sensor GND]
    pinA0 --- sensD[Sensor Analog Output]
    pinA0 --- filtB[RC LPF - 100k and 1uF]
    filtB --- gnd1[GND]
    pinD2 --- ledB[Green LED - Pump OFF Indicator - 330 ohm]
    ledB --- gnd2[GND]
    pinD3 --- ledC[Red LED - Pump ON Indicator - 330 ohm]
    ledC --- gnd3[GND]
    pinD4 --- trB[Transistor BC547 Base - 10k Resistor]
    trB --- relayB[Relay Coil 5V - Flyback Diode 1N4007]
    relayB --- vcc2[VCC 5V]
    pinD5 --- lcdB[LCD 16x2 - RS Enable D4 D5 D6 D7]
    pinVcc --- vcc2
    classDef power fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef analog fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Record Submission:** In your lab record, always draw the schematic with **explicit pin numbers**, **component values**, and a **clear legend** separating the low-voltage DC side (microcontroller) from the high-voltage AC side (pump). The instructor awards marks for **neatness, correct component symbols, and proper isolation barrier indication**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the term "hysteresis band" in the context of a microcontroller-based water level controller. Why is it essential, and how is its width chosen?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hysteresis band** $\Delta h$ is the difference between the **upper threshold** $h_{\text{high}}$ at which the pump switches OFF and the **lower threshold** $h_{\text{low}}$ at which the pump switches ON:

$$\Delta h = h_{\text{high}} - h_{\text{low}}$$

It is **essential** because, without it, a noisy sensor reading fluctuating across a single threshold would cause the relay to **chatter** (switch ON/OFF rapidly), destroying the relay contacts, stressing the pump motor windings, and creating EMI. The hysteresis band creates a **dead zone** where the controller holds its previous state, providing noise immunity and a comfortable operational buffer.

The width is typically chosen as **5 % to 10 % of the full-scale tank height** — large enough to exceed the peak-to-peak noise amplitude, yet small enough to maintain useful operational range.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**List any three water level sensing techniques and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Technique | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Float switch (magnetic/mechanical)** | Simple, low cost, digital output | Only point sensing; mechanical wear |
| 2 | **Conductive probe (resistive)** | Continuous reading, very low cost | Subject to corrosion and electrolytic degradation |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | Non-contact, suitable for corrosive/foamy liquids | Affected by temperature (speed of sound) and beam angle |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always show units** in every numerical calculation. A correct number without a unit is treated as incomplete.
> 2. **Never confuse** the relay *coil current* with the *contact current*. The coil is on the low-voltage DC side; the contacts switch the high-voltage AC load.
> 3. **Do not** omit the **optical isolation** argument in the interfacing question. Examiners specifically look for "galvanic isolation between AC mains and DC logic" — a missing one-line explanation can cost 2 marks.
> 4. **Always justify** the choice of sampling frequency with reference to the Nyquist theorem applied to the physical signal bandwidth. A bare number "10 Hz" with no justification is treated as a guess.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

#### `(a)` Design the complete hardware interfacing circuit for a microcontroller-based water level controller using a 10-bit ADC and an ultrasonic sensor. Explain the role of each component and justify the choice of isolation technique between the microcontroller and the 230 V AC pump. **(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Component-by-component justification:**

1. **Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04):** Provides non-contact, continuous level measurement via time-of-flight. Trigger and Echo pins are interfaced to two GPIO pins of the microcontroller. The Echo pulse width is measured using a hardware timer (e.g., Timer1 in capture mode on MSP430, or `pulseIn()` on Arduino).

2. **Level Shifter / Voltage Divider (if Echo is 5 V and MCU is 3.3 V):** A resistive divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) drops the 5 V Echo signal to 3.3 V to protect the MCU's input pin.

3. **First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter ($R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$):** Placed on the analog signal line to suppress 50 Hz EMI. Cutoff $f_c \approx 1.59\,\text{Hz}$.

4. **Opto-Coupler (MOC3021 + BT136 TRIAC, or a 5 V reed relay with flyback diode 1N4007):** Provides **galvanic isolation** between the 5 V DC logic side and the 230 V AC load side. This is essential because any fault (e.g., relay contact welding) on the AC side could otherwise feed lethal mains voltage into the microcontroller, damaging it and endangering the user.

5. **Snubber Circuit (0.1 μF + 100 Ω across TRIAC):** Protects the TRIAC from $dv/dt$ triggering due to inductive loads.

6. **MOV (275 V Metal Oxide Varistor) across AC line:** Clamps transient overvoltages (e.g., from lightning or inductive kickback) to a safe level.

7. **Indicator LEDs (Green, Red, Yellow) with 330 Ω current-limiting resistors:** Visual status of pump state and fault.

8. **16x2 LCD in 4-bit mode:** Displays current level in cm, percentage full, and pump state.

**Isolation justification (2 marks):** Opto-isolation is chosen over a direct relay drive because it provides **complete electrical separation** — there is **no conductive path** between the 230 V AC mains and the 5 V DC logic. The control signal is transmitted only as light (photons), preventing ground loops, surge propagation, and electric shock hazards.

---

#### `(b)` A water tank of height 1.5 m is monitored using a microcontroller with a 10-bit ADC and a 5.0 V reference. The sensor voltage varies linearly from 0 V (empty) to 5.0 V (full). Design a hysteresis control algorithm that switches the pump ON when the level falls below 20 % and switches it OFF when the level rises above 80 %. Calculate the ADC count for each threshold and the hysteresis band in cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Tank height: $H = 1.5\,\text{m} = 150\,\text{cm}$
- ADC bits: $n = 10 \implies L = 1024$ levels
- Reference: $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\,\text{V}$
- Sensor: $V(h) = \left(\dfrac{5.0}{150}\right) h = 0.0333 \cdot h\,\text{V/cm}$

**Step 1 — Compute the quantization step**

$$q = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 4.888 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 4.888\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute lower threshold**

$$h_{\text{low}} = 0.20 \times 150 = 30\,\text{cm}$$

$$V_{\text{low}} = 0.0333 \times 30 = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

$$N_{\text{low}} = \left\lfloor \frac{1.0}{0.004888} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 204.6 \right\rfloor = 204$$

**Step 3 — Compute upper threshold**

$$h_{\text{high}} = 0.80 \times 150 = 120\,\text{cm}$$

$$V_{\text{high}} = 0.0333 \times 120 = 4.0\,\text{V}$$

$$N_{\text{high}} = \left\lfloor \frac{4.0}{0.004888} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 818.4 \right\rfloor = 818$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis band**

$$\Delta h = h_{\text{high}} - h_{\text{low}} = 120 - 30 = 90\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 5 — Firmware algorithm (pseudocode)**

```
initialize: pumpState = OFF
loop forever every Ts = 100 ms:
    raw = read_ADC(channel 0)
    filtered = moving_average(raw, N = 8)
    h_cm = filtered * (150.0 / 1023.0)
    
    if (pumpState == OFF) and (h_cm < 30.0):
        debounce_counter++
        if (debounce_counter >= 5):
            pumpState = ON
            set_gpio(PUMP_RELAY_PIN, HIGH)
    elif (pumpState == ON) and (h_cm > 120.0):
        debounce_counter++
        if (debounce_counter >= 5):
            pumpState = OFF
            set_gpio(PUMP_RELAY_PIN, LOW)
    else:
        debounce_counter = 0
    
    lcd_display(h_cm, pumpState)
    kick_watchdog()
```

**Answer summary table (for record):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $h_{\text{low}}$ | 30 cm |
| $h_{\text{high}}$ | 120 cm |
| $\Delta h$ | 90 cm |
| $N_{\text{low}}$ | 204 |
| $N_{\text{high}}$ | 818 |
| Quantization step | 4.888 mV |
| Level resolution | $\approx$ 0.146 cm |

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative)

#### `(a)` With the aid of a block diagram, describe the complete signal processing chain in a microcontroller-based water level controller. Identify the DSP operations performed at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete signal processing chain consists of the following six stages. At each stage, a specific DSP or embedded operation is performed:

1. **Continuous Physical Signal** — Water level $h(t)$ is a **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude** (analog) signal with very low bandwidth ($f_m < 0.01\,\text{Hz}$). The physical process is the tank's inflow and outflow dynamics.

2. **Transduction** — A sensor (ultrasonic, conductive, or capacitive) converts $h(t)$ into an electrical signal $v(t)$. This is the **first signal transformation** — from physical to electrical domain.

3. **Signal Conditioning** — An op-amp (e.g., LM358) amplifies the weak sensor signal, and an RC low-pass filter (with $f_c \approx 1.6\,\text{Hz}$) suppresses 50 Hz mains hum. This is **analog filtering** — a continuous-time DSP operation.

4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — The conditioned signal $v(t)$ is **sampled** at $t = nT_s$ and **quantized** to a finite number of levels, producing the discrete-time, discrete-amplitude sequence $h[n] = h(nT_s)$. This is the **sampling and quantization** step — the defining operation of any DSP system.

5. **Digital Processing** — The microcontroller (CPU) executes the **control algorithm** (threshold comparison, hysteresis, debouncing, moving-average filtering). The moving average is a simple **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter**:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[n-k]$$

6. **Digital-to-Physical Actuation** — The processed output $u[n] \in \{0, 1\}$ drives a GPIO pin, which energizes a relay/TRIAC and switches the AC pump motor. This is the **inverse** of transduction — from electrical back to physical.

**Block diagram (mermaid):** Refer to Section 4.1 for the full graphical block diagram.

**Key DSP insight:** This experiment is a complete end-to-end DSP pipeline, demonstrating that **DSP is not limited to audio/image processing** — it is the foundational paradigm of *any* digital measurement and control system.

---

#### `(b)` An RC low-pass filter is to be designed to suppress 50 Hz mains interference in a water level sensor signal. Choose a cutoff frequency of 1.6 Hz and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the required resistance, and verify the attenuation at 50 Hz in decibels. **(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — RC low-pass filter transfer function**

The magnitude of a first-order RC low-pass filter is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for R given $f_c = 1.6\,\text{Hz}$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \implies R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1.6 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.005 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 99.5 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 100\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Attenuation at 50 Hz**

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (50/1.6)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (31.25)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 976.56}}$$

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{977.56}} = \frac{1}{31.265} \approx 0.03198$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels**

$$A_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(0.03198) = 20 \times (-1.495) \approx -29.9\,\text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** A 50 Hz noise of 1 V amplitude at the sensor input is reduced to approximately **32 mV** at the ADC input — well below the 10-bit quantization step of 4.89 mV by a factor of 6.5, ensuring the interference does not produce false level transitions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam**

- [x] A **water level controller** is a real-time closed-loop system that samples a physical signal, processes it digitally, and actuates a pump.
- [x] The **sampling period** $T_s$ for a slow process (water level) can be 100–500 ms; the Nyquist criterion is trivially satisfied.
- [x] **ADC quantization step** $q = V_{\text{ref}} / (2^n - 1)$. For 10-bit and 5 V, $q \approx 4.89\,\text{mV}$.
- [x] **Level resolution** $\Delta h = h_{\max} / 2^n$. A 10-bit ADC over a 100 cm tank gives $\approx 1\,\text{mm}$.
- [x] **Hysteresis** prevents relay chattering by creating two thresholds: $h_{\text{low}}$ (pump ON) and $h_{\text{high}}$ (pump OFF).
- [x] **Hysteresis bandwidth** is typically **5–10 %** of full-scale.
- [x] **First-order RC low-pass filter**: $R \approx 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$ gives $f_c \approx 1.6\,\text{Hz}$ and $\approx -30\,\text{dB}$ attenuation at 50 Hz.
- [x] **Galvanic isolation** (opto-coupler or relay) is mandatory between 5 V DC logic and 230 V AC pump.
- [x] **Flyback diode (1N4007)** across the relay coil protects the GPIO pin from inductive kickback.
- [x] **MOV / snubber** across AC contacts protects the TRIAC/relay from voltage transients.
- [x] **Watchdog timer** must be enabled to recover from software lockup due to EMI.
- [x] **Moving average FIR filter**: $y[n] = (1/N) \sum h[n-k]$ — reduces noise variance by a factor of $N$.
- [x] **Ultrasonic time-of-flight**: $h = (v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}) / 2$ with $v_{\text{sound}} \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$.
- [x] **Debouncing** in software requires $N$ consecutive confirming samples before changing the pump state.
- [x] The system is essentially a **transducer → ADC → CPU → DAC/GPIO → actuator** pipeline — a complete DSP chain.
- [x] The same architecture generalises to **temperature control, pressure control, flow control, and motor speed control**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Validation of Temperature Controller with Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL507 - DSP LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Validation of Temperature Controller with Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Temperature Controller with Microcontrollers** is an embedded closed-loop feedback control system in which a microcontroller unit (MCU) continuously acquires an analog/digital temperature signal from a sensor, processes it against a user-defined setpoint using a deterministic control law (ON–OFF, Proportional, Proportional–Integral, or Proportional–Integral–Derivative), and actuates an external thermal load (heater/cooler/fan) through a driver interface. The validation phase verifies the static and dynamic performance of the controller using metrics such as **steady-state error**, **rise time**, **settling time**, **overshoot**, and **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment maps to **CO2 – Design and implement real-time control algorithms on microcontroller/DSP platforms** and **CO4 – Validate system response through experimental measurements and analytical comparison**. It integrates the *Acquire → Process → Actuate → Verify* paradigm of embedded systems engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **domestic geyser with a thermostat dial**:
- The **dial** is your *setpoint* (desired temperature).
- The **thermometer strip** on the tank is your *sensor* (measures actual temperature).
- The **bimetallic strip inside** is the *controller* — when actual < setpoint, it switches the heating coil ON; when actual ≥ setpoint, it switches it OFF.
- The **heating coil** is the *actuator*.
- The **water in the tank** is the *plant* (the system being controlled).

A microcontroller-based temperature controller does **exactly the same thing** — but with mathematical precision, programmability, hysteresis control, PID logic, and a digital display instead of a mechanical bimetallic strip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental triad of any control system is **SENSOR → CONTROLLER → ACTUATOR**. In this experiment, the microcontroller is the *brain* (controller), the LM35/DS18B20 is the *eyes* (sensor), and the relay/MOSFET driving a heater is the *muscle* (actuator).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (used in Validation)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Setpoint | $T_{sp}$ | °C | Desired temperature |
| Process Variable | $T_{pv}$ | °C | Measured temperature |
| Steady-State Error | $e_{ss}$ | °C | $\vert T_{sp} - T_{pv}(\infty) \vert$ |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | s | Time to go from 10% → 90% of setpoint |
| Settling Time | $t_s$ | s | Time to enter and stay within ±2% band |
| Overshoot | $M_p$ | % | Maximum peak beyond setpoint |
| Control Output | $u(t)$ | % PWM / V | Signal sent to actuator |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the System

The complete experiment is built on five **functional blocks**, each with a defined role:

1. **Temperature Sensing Block** — Converts physical heat into a measurable electrical quantity.
   - *LM35*: Linear analog output, **10 mV/°C**, range **0 °C to +150 °C**.
   - *DS18B20*: 1-Wire digital, **±0.5 °C accuracy**, range **−55 °C to +125 °C**.
   - *Thermistor (NTC)*: Resistance inversely proportional to temperature (Steinhart–Hart equation).

2. **Signal Conditioning Block** — Amplifies / level-shifts the sensor output to the MCU's ADC range (typically **0 V – 5 V** or **0 V – 3.3 V**).

3. **Microcontroller (MCU) Block** — Performs:
   - **ADC conversion** (10-bit, 12-bit depending on MCU).
   - **Digital filtering** (Moving Average / Exponential Smoothing).
   - **Control law computation** (ON–OFF or PID).
   - **PWM generation** for actuator.
   - **Display driving** (LCD 16×2 / 7-segment).

4. **Actuator Driver Block** — Isolates the low-power MCU from the high-power load using:
   - *Electromechanical Relay* (slow, isolated, ~10 ms latency).
   - *MOSFET / SSR (Solid State Relay)* (fast, PWM-compatible).
   - *Optocoupler (MOC3021, PC817)* for galvanic isolation.

5. **Plant (Thermal Load)** — The actual physical system being temperature-regulated (water bath, incubator, soldering iron tip, etc.).

## 2.2 The ON–OFF Control Law (Bang-Bang)

The simplest digital control algorithm. The control output is binary:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{min} & \text{if } e(t) \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

where the **error signal** is:
$$e(t) = T_{sp} - T_{pv}(t)$$

To prevent rapid chattering around the setpoint, a **hysteresis band** $H$ is introduced:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } T_{pv}(t) < (T_{sp} - H/2) \\ U_{min} & \text{if } T_{pv}(t) > (T_{sp} + H/2) \\ u(t-1) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hysteresis?** Without it, the relay chatters at very high frequency near the setpoint, causing electrical noise, contact wear, and reduced actuator life.

## 2.3 The PID Control Law (Continuous-Time Form)

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

| Term | Symbol | Effect on Response | Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proportional | $K_p$ | Reduces rise time | Increases overshoot |
| Integral | $K_i$ | Eliminates steady-state error | Can cause oscillation |
| Derivative | $K_d$ | Dampens oscillation | Sensitive to noise |

## 2.4 Discrete-Time PID (MCU-Implementable Form)

Since the MCU samples at a fixed interval $T_s$, the integral is replaced by a **summation (Euler backward)** and the derivative by a **first-difference**:

$$u[k] = K_p\, e[k] + K_i\, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

A **velocity-form (incremental) PID** is preferred in MCU implementation to avoid integral windup:

$$\Delta u[k] = K_p\big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i\, T_s\, e[k] + K_d \frac{e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]}{T_s}$$

$$u[k] = u[k-1] + \Delta u[k]$$

## 2.5 ADC Conversion Formula (LM35 + 10-bit ADC @ 5 V)

$$T_{pv} = \frac{ADC_{value} \times V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} \times \frac{1}{S}$$

For LM35 with $S = 0.01$ V/°C, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 10$:

$$T_{pv} = \frac{ADC_{value} \times 5.0}{1023. \times 0.01} \quad [^{\circ}C]$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = T_{sp} - T_{pv}(t)$ | Error computation | °C |
| 2 | $u(t) \in \{0, U_{max}\}$ | ON–OFF control | V / PWM% |
| 3 | $T_{pv} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023 \times S}$ | LM35 conversion | °C |
| 4 | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e + K_d \frac{\Delta e}{T_s}$ | Digital PID | % PWM |
| 5 | $\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$ | Velocity PID | – |
| 6 | $e_{ss} = \vert T_{sp} - T_{pv}(\infty) \vert$ | Steady-state error | °C |
| 7 | $M_p = \frac{T_{peak} - T_{sp}}{T_{sp}} \times 100$ | Overshoot | % |
| 8 | $IAE = \sum_{k=0}^{N} \vert e[k] \vert T_s$ | Performance index | °C·s |
| 9 | $\tau = -RC \ln\left(1 - \frac{T_{sp} - T_0}{T_{max} - T_0}\right)$ | First-order thermal plant | s |
| 10 | $H = 0.02 \times T_{sp}$ | Hysteresis band heuristic | °C |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Industrial Ovens & Furnaces** (heat treatment of metals).
> - **PCR Thermal Cyclers** (DNA amplification in medical diagnostics).
> - **3D Printer Hot Ends** (extruder temperature stability ±1 °C).
> - **Inkubators for Neonatal Care** (closed-loop infant warmers).
> - **Petrochemical Reactors** (exothermic reaction temperature control).
> - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** of EVs (cell temperature 25–45 °C).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting LM35 Analog Voltage to °C

**Given:**
- LM35 output: $V_{LM35} = 10$ mV/°C $= 0.01$ V/°C
- MCU ADC: 10-bit, $V_{ref} = 5$ V

**Step 1 — ADC Resolution:**
$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1024 - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 0.004888 \;\text{V/bit}$$

This means **each LSB of the ADC represents 4.888 mV**.

**Step 2 — Voltage corresponding to ADC reading $D$:**
$$V_{in} = D \times 0.004888 \;\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert voltage to temperature using LM35 scale factor $S = 0.01$ V/°C:**
$$T_{pv} = \frac{V_{in}}{S} = \frac{D \times 0.004888}{0.01}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$T_{pv} = D \times 0.4888 \;\;^{\circ}C$$

This is the **canonical KTU lab equation** — every batch student must memorize it.

**Step 5 — Sample numerical verification:**
If ADC reads $D = 204$, then $T_{pv} = 204 \times 0.4888 = 99.7$ °C ≈ **100 °C** (boiling water verification ✓).

## 3.2 Derivation: Hysteresis Band Design for ON–OFF Control

**Problem:** Setpoint $T_{sp} = 60$ °C, plant thermal time constant $\tau = 120$ s, relay switching delay $t_d = 0.5$ s, heater power causes a temperature ramp of $0.5$ °C/s.

**Step 1 — Maximum temperature swing without hysteresis:**
If switching delay is $0.5$ s and ramp rate is $0.5$ °C/s:
$$\Delta T_{max} = \text{ramp} \times t_d = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \;^{\circ}C$$

**Step 2 — Hysteresis chosen as 4× the maximum swing (safety factor):**
$$H = 4 \times \Delta T_{max} = 4 \times 0.25 = 1.0 \;^{\circ}C$$

**Step 3 — Upper and lower switching thresholds:**
$$T_{upper} = T_{sp} + H/2 = 60 + 0.5 = 60.5 \;^{\circ}C$$
$$T_{lower} = T_{sp} - H/2 = 60 - 0.5 = 59.5 \;^{\circ}C$$

**Step 4 — Heater behaviour:**
- Heater ON when $T_{pv} < 59.5$ °C
- Heater OFF when $T_{pv} > 60.5$ °C
- Hold previous state when $59.5 \leq T_{pv} \leq 60.5$ °C

## 3.3 Derivation: Ziegler–Nichols Tuning for PID

**Step 1 — Set $K_i = 0$, $K_d = 0$. Use pure P-control.**

**Step 2 — Increase $K_p$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations**. Record:**
- Critical gain $K_u$ (gain at which oscillation begins).
- Oscillation period $T_u$.

**Step 3 — Apply Ziegler–Nichols first method (for PID):**

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $0.50 K_u$ | – | – |
| PI | $0.45 K_u$ | $0.80 T_u$ | – |
| **PID** | $\mathbf{0.60 K_u}$ | $\mathbf{0.50 T_u}$ | $\mathbf{0.125 T_u}$ |

**Step 4 — Convert to digital gains:**
$$K_p^{dig} = 0.60 K_u$$
$$K_i^{dig} = \frac{K_p^{dig} \times T_s}{0.50 T_u}$$
$$K_d^{dig} = \frac{K_p^{dig} \times 0.125 T_u}{T_s}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**
Suppose $K_u = 8.0$, $T_u = 20$ s, $T_s = 0.1$ s.
$$K_p^{dig} = 0.60 \times 8.0 = 4.8$$
$$K_i^{dig} = \frac{4.8 \times 0.1}{10} = 0.048$$
$$K_d^{dig} = \frac{4.8 \times 2.5}{0.1} = 120$$

## 3.4 Derivation: First-Order Thermal Plant Model

A heater–sensor system behaves as a **first-order lag** plus **time delay**:

$$G(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where:
- $K$ = steady-state gain (°C/%PWM)
- $\tau$ = thermal time constant (s)
- $L$ = dead time (s)

**Identification by Step Response:**
1. Apply 50% PWM step input.
2. Record temperature $T(t)$.
3. Measure final value $T_\infty$.
4. Compute gain: $K = T_\infty / 50$.
5. Find time to reach 63.2% of $T_\infty$ → that is $\tau + L$.
6. Find time to reach 28% of $T_\infty$ → that is $L$ alone.
7. $\tau = t_{63\%} - t_{28\%}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Laws

| Feature | ON–OFF | P | PI | PID |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error | Exists | $e_{ss} = T_{sp}/(1+K_pK)$ | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 |
| Rise time | Medium | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| Overshoot | High | High | Medium | Low |
| Settling time | Slow | Medium | Medium | Fast |
| Computational load | Trivial | Low | Low | Medium |
| Suitability for MCU | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input Setpoint via Keypad / Potentiometer] --> B[Microcontroller MCU ATmega328 / 8051 / Tiva]
    C[Temperature Sensor LM35 / DS18B20] --> D[Signal Conditioning Amplifier / Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Channel 10-bit or 12-bit]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Control Algorithm ON-OFF or PID]
    F --> G[PWM Output or GPIO]
    G --> H[Driver Stage MOSFET / Relay / SSR]
    H --> I[Actuator Heater Coil / Fan / Peltier]
    I --> J[Thermal Plant Water Bath / Oven]
    J --> C
    B --> K[LCD 16x2 Display Setpoint and PV]
    B --> L[Serial UART Logging to PC]
```

## 4.2 Control Loop Dataflow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Read ADC value from LM35] --> S2[Step 2 Convert ADC to temperature degC]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute error e = Setpoint - PV]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4 Control Mode}
    S4 --> M1[ON-OFF with Hysteresis] --> S5a
    S4 --> M2[PID Computation] --> S5b
    S5a[Step 5a Set PWM 0 percent or 100 percent] --> S6[Step 6 Update LCD]
    S5b[Step 5b Clamp u between 0 and 100] --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Wait T_s milliseconds]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Log to Serial / EEPROM]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Pin Mapping)

| MCU Pin | Connected To | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PC0 (A0) | LM35 Vout | ADC Input |
| PB1 (D9) | MOSFET Gate (via 100 Ω) | PWM Output (Timer1) |
| PD2 | Relay IN1 (via transistor) | Heater ON/OFF |
| PD3 | Buzzer | Over-temperature alarm |
| PC4, PC5 (SDA, SCL) | LCD 16×2 (I²C) | Display |
| PD0 (RX) | USB-UART | PC logging |
| PD7 | User Button | Increment setpoint |
| PB0 | User Button | Decrement setpoint |
| AREF | 100 nF to GND + 5 V | ADC reference bypass |

## 4.4 State Machine for ON–OFF Controller (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> HEATING: PV less than T_lower
    HEATING --> STABILIZING: PV reaches T_lower
    STABILIZING --> COOLING: PV exceeds T_upper
    COOLING --> STABILIZING: PV drops to T_upper
    STABILIZING --> ALARM: PV greater than T_max_safety
    COOLING --> ALARM: PV greater than T_max_safety
    ALARM --> IDLE: User RESET
    HEATING --> ALARM: Sensor fault open circuit
    COOLING --> ALARM: Sensor fault short circuit
```

## 4.5 Sample Pseudo-Code Skeleton (Algorithm Flow)

```
INITIALIZE:
    LCD_Init()
    ADC_Init(channel=0, prescaler=128)
    PWM_Init(timer1, freq=1kHz, resolution=8bit)
    Setpoint = 50;    // default
    H = 1.0;          // hysteresis
    Kp=4.8; Ki=0.048; Kd=120; Ts=0.1s
    e_prev=0; e_prev2=0; u=0;

LOOP_FOREVER:
    raw = ADC_Read(0)              // 0..1023
    T_pv = raw * 0.4888            // LM35 to degC
    e = Setpoint - T_pv

    // incremental PID
    du = Kp*(e - e_prev)
       + Ki*Ts*e
       + Kd/Ts*(e - 2*e_prev + e_prev2)
    u = constrain(u + du, 0, 100)

    PWM_Write(channel, u)          // duty in %
    LCD_Print("SP:", Setpoint, "PV:", T_pv)
    Serial_Print(T_pv, u)

    e_prev2 = e_prev
    e_prev  = e
    delay(Ts*1000)                 // 100 ms
END_LOOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a *descriptive algorithmic skeleton* (not a programming snippet) to demonstrate the control logic. Actual lab code in C/Embedded C follows this exact structure.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three temperature sensors that can be interfaced with a microcontroller. State the output type of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **LM35** — Analog linear voltage output (**10 mV/°C**).
2. **DS18B20** — Digital 1-Wire protocol, 12-bit resolution, **±0.5 °C** accuracy.
3. **Thermistor (NTC 10 kΩ)** — Resistance varying with temperature, requires a **voltage divider** to convert to voltage.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for sensor + correct output type)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is hysteresis in an ON–OFF temperature controller? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
Hysteresis is a **dead-band** (typically ±0.5 °C to ±2 °C) deliberately introduced around the setpoint within which the controller holds its previous output state unchanged. It is necessary to **prevent relay chattering** (rapid ON–OFF cycling) near the setpoint, which would otherwise cause excessive contact wear, electrical noise, and reduced actuator life. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a microcontroller-based temperature controller using an LM35 sensor. Identify each block and state its function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an ON–OFF controller algorithm with hysteresis for a water bath to be maintained at **70 °C**. The sensor LM35 is interfaced to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. Choose a suitable hysteresis band and write the decision logic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Block Diagram — must be reproduced in exam:**

```
[Setpoint Input] → [MCU + Control Algo] → [PWM/Digital Output]
                          ↑                          ↓
                    [ADC ← LM35]              [Driver → Heater]
                                                   ↓
                                            [Water Bath Plant]
```

**Block Functions (write 4–5 lines each):**

1. **Setpoint Input:** Provided via keypad, potentiometer (ADC), or PC UART. Stored in a global variable `T_sp`.
2. **Temperature Sensor (LM35):** Produces **10 mV/°C** analog output proportional to bath temperature.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Anti-aliasing RC low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 10 Hz) to suppress mains noise (50 Hz).
4. **ADC (10-bit):** Quantizes analog voltage into 0–1023 digital levels.
5. **Microcontroller:** Runs the control algorithm at a sampling period $T_s = 100$ ms.
6. **Driver Stage:** MOSFET (IRF540N) driven by MCU PWM, providing variable power to heater.
7. **Plant (Water Bath):** The first-order thermal system with parameters $K$, $\tau$, $L$.

**Conversion Equation:**
$$T_{pv}\,[^{\circ}C] = \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1023 \times 0.01} = ADC \times 0.4888$$

*(Full 7 marks if all 7 blocks labelled + equation derived.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Setpoint:** $T_{sp} = 70$ °C

**Step 2 — Hysteresis design:**
A 1 °C band is chosen for a 70 °C water bath (≈ 1.4% of setpoint, standard heuristic):
$$H = 1.0 \;^{\circ}C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{upper} = 70.5 \;^{\circ}C,\;\; T_{lower} = 69.5 \;^{\circ}C$$

**Step 3 — Decision Logic (write as pseudo-code or flow):**
```
T_pv = ADC_Read(0) * 0.4888    // read and convert
IF T_pv < 69.5 THEN
    Heater = 100% PWM          // FULL ON
ELSE IF T_pv > 70.5 THEN
    Heater = 0% PWM            // FULL OFF
ELSE
    Heater = Heater            // hold previous state
END IF
```

**Step 4 — Verification calculation at 70 °C:**
$$ADC = \frac{70}{0.4888} = 143.2 \approx 143$$
Expected ADC reading at setpoint = **143**.

**Step 5 — Safety extension:**
If $T_{pv} > 85$ °C, trigger alarm and cut-off (over-temperature protection).

*(7 marks: 1 hysteresis choice + 2 logic + 1 ADC verification + 1 safety = full marks.)*

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the digital implementation equation of a **PID controller** suitable for execution in a microcontroller. Explain how integral windup is prevented. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a thermal plant with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{0.8 \, e^{-2s}}{15s + 1}$ (°C / %PWM), determine the PID parameters using the **Ziegler–Nichols step-response method** (use the table-based approximation for first-order plus dead-time plants). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Continuous PID:**
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Discretize at sampling period $T_s$** using Euler backward rule:
- Integral → summation: $\int_0^{kT_s} e d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{j=0}^k e[j]$
- Derivative → backward difference: $\dfrac{de}{dt} \approx \dfrac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$

**Step 3 — Position-form digital PID:**
$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^k e[j] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Incremental (velocity) form** to enable bumpless transfer and avoid integral windup:

$$\Delta u[k] = K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2])$$

$$u[k] = u[k-1] + \Delta u[k]$$

**Step 5 — Anti-Windup Strategies:**
- **Clamping:** $u[k] = \min(\max(u[k], 0), 100)$.
- **Conditional Integration:** Stop integrating when $u$ is saturated.
- **Back-Calculation:** When saturated, reduce the integrator by a leakage factor $K_t$.

*(7 marks: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 ≈ distributed across derivation + windup.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Plant parameters from $G(s)$:**
- $K = 0.8$ °C / %PWM
- $L = 2$ s (dead time)
- $\tau = 15$ s (time constant)

**Step 2 — Ziegler–Nichols first method (Cohen–Coon / step-response tuning table):**

For a first-order plus dead-time plant, the ZN-PID formula is:
$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \tau}{K L}, \quad T_i = 2 L, \quad T_d = 0.5 L$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation:**
$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \times 15}{0.8 \times 2} = \frac{18}{1.6} = 11.25$$

$$T_i = 2 \times 2 = 4 \;\text{s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{11.25}{4} = 2.8125 \;\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \times 2 = 1 \;\text{s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_d = K_p \times T_d = 11.25 \times 1 = 11.25 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Choose $T_s$ using thumb rule** $T_s = 0.1 \tau = 1.5$ s ≈ **0.1 s** (faster is better for digital implementation; pick $T_s = 0.1$ s).

**Step 5 — Digital gains for MCU:**
$$K_p^{dig} = 11.25$$
$$K_i^{dig} = K_p \times T_s / T_i = 11.25 \times 0.1 / 4 = 0.2813$$
$$K_d^{dig} = K_p \times T_d / T_s = 11.25 \times 1 / 0.1 = 112.5$$

**Step 6 — Final velocity PID difference equation:**
$$\Delta u[k] = 11.25(e[k] - e[k-1]) + 0.0281\, e[k] + 1125(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2])$$

*(7 marks: 1 plant ID + 2 formula + 2 numerical + 1 conversion + 1 final equation.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ADC value to °C** before applying the control law. The error $e$ must be in °C, not in raw ADC counts. *(−2 marks typical)*
> 2. **Confusing LM35 (10 mV/°C) with LM34 (10 mV/°F)**. Always verify the sensor part number on the lab kit.
> 3. **No hysteresis in ON-OFF** — full-marks deduction is rare but the question *specifically* testing hysteresis will deduct 1 mark if not designed.
> 4. **Sampling period not stated** in the PID design — examiners check for it. Always mention $T_s$ explicitly.
> 5. **Missing anti-windup clause** in PID derivation. Marks are reserved for *anti-windup* in the 14-mark questions.
> 6. **No units in final answer** — even if numerically correct, answers without °C, s, %, etc., lose 0.5–1 mark cumulatively.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Canonical LM35 Equation:** $T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{ADC \times 5.0}{1023 \times 0.01} = ADC \times 0.4888$
- **LM35 Scale Factor:** **10 mV/°C**, range **0–150 °C**.
- **DS18B20** uses 1-Wire protocol; no ADC needed; range **−55 to +125 °C**; accuracy **±0.5 °C**.
- **ON–OFF Controller** is the simplest; **always** use hysteresis to prevent chattering.
- **PID Gains:** $K_p$ → speed, $K_i$ → accuracy (kills $e_{ss}$), $K_d$ → damping.
- **Velocity-form PID** is preferred for MCU implementation (bumpless transfer + anti-windup).
- **Sampling period** $T_s$ should be ≤ $\tau/10$ for the discrete PID to behave like the continuous one.
- **Ziegler–Nichols (first method) for FOPDT plant:** $K_p = 1.2\tau/(KL)$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$.
- **Validation metrics** to record in the lab report: $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$, $e_{ss}$, IAE.
- **Isolation** of MCU and power side via **optocoupler** is mandatory for safety and noise immunity.
- **Safety cut-off** (software + hardware) must always be implemented — examiners love this point.
- **Display** typically LCD 16×2 (I²C variant uses only 2 MCU pins).
- **PWM frequency** for thermal control: choose **low frequency** (~1 kHz or less) so that heating element thermal mass integrates the pulses.
- **Lab Record Must Include:** Circuit diagram, control algorithm pseudo-code, step-response plot, tuned PID values, performance table, conclusions on stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Viva Voce Quick-Fire Questions (practice these):**
> 1. *Why is derivative term sensitive to noise?* → Because $\Delta e/\Delta t$ amplifies high-frequency components. Solved by **derivative filter** $K_d\, s / (1 + \tau_f s)$.
> 2. *What is integral windup?* → Integrator accumulates large error during actuator saturation; solved by clamping or back-calculation.
> 3. *Can we use a triac instead of a MOSFET for AC heaters?* → Yes, via **phase-angle control** or **zero-crossing detection**, but requires zero-cross detector circuit.
> 4. *Why filter the LM35 output?* → Mains 50 Hz pickup and ADC switching noise.
> 5. *Difference between software and hardware PID?* → Software PID is flexible (gain scheduling, auto-tuning), hardware is faster but inflexible.
